name: MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY

topics.title: Introduction to Engineering Drawing: Principles of Drawing, Free Hand Sketching, Manual Drawing, and CAD Drawing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Engineering Drawing: Principles of Drawing, Free Hand Sketching, Manual Drawing, and CAD Drawing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Engineering Drawing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Drawing (ED)** is the *universal graphical language of engineers* used to communicate the geometry, dimensions, tolerances, materials, and manufacturing details of a physical object on a 2D plane (paper or digital screen), in accordance with standardized conventions prescribed by **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards — SP 46:1988)** and **ISO (International Organization for Standardization — ISO 128 & ISO 5455)**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the laboratory course **PCRUL307 — Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling Lab** is built on the premise that an engineer must be able to *read, interpret, and produce* engineering drawings fluently — both by hand (manual) and using **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** tools.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Language" Metaphor

Think of engineering drawing as the **"language of industry"**:
- **Grammar** = Drawing Standards (BIS/ISO rules, line types, line weights)
- **Alphabet** = Lines, symbols, hatching patterns, dimensioning notations
- **Sentences** = Orthographic and isometric views
- **Paragraphs** = Complete assembly drawings with part lists
- **Spoken vs Written** = Free-hand sketching (spoken/spontaneous) vs Manual/CAD drawing (written/formal)

Just as two engineers from different countries (e.g., Kerala and Germany) can understand the same engineering drawing because they both follow ISO standards, engineering drawing acts as a *lingua franca* for global manufacturing.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Engineering Drawing in KTU Lab

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Principles of Drawing:** The theoretical foundation — projections, views, scales, sections, dimensioning.
> **Pillar 2 — Free-Hand Sketching:** Quick, un-instrumented visual ideation using pencil on paper.
> **Pillar 3 — Manual Drawing:** Precision instrument-based drawing on a drafting board.
> **Pillar 4 — CAD Drawing:** Digital drawing/modeling using software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360, Creo).

### 1.4 The Golden Metric Every Student Must Know

The **standard drawing sheet sizes** as per **ISO 5457 / BIS 10714** are based on the **A-series**, where the aspect ratio is fixed at:

$$\text{Aspect Ratio} = 1 : \sqrt{2} \approx 1 : 1.414$$

The **A0 sheet** has an area of **1 m²**, and successive sizes are obtained by halving the previous one along the longer side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIS/ISO Standard Sheet Sizes:** A0 = 1189 × 841 mm, A1 = 841 × 594 mm, A2 = 594 × 420 mm, A3 = 420 × 297 mm, A4 = 297 × 210 mm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Engineering Drawing — Structured Logic

The principles form the **rule-book** for producing unambiguous technical drawings. They can be organized into seven foundational blocks:

**A. Projection Theory**
- **Orthographic Projection:** Object projected onto planes using parallel projectors perpendicular to the plane.
  - **First-Angle Projection** (used in India, Europe, ISO default) — object placed *between* observer and projection plane.
  - **Third-Angle Projection** (used in USA, Canada) — projection plane placed *between* observer and object.
- **Isometric Projection:** A type of axonometric projection where the three axes are at **120°** to each other and foreshortening is applied to give a true 3D feel.
- **Perspective Projection:** Used mainly in architectural drawing; projectors converge at a vanishing point.

**B. Views Arrangement (First-Angle, KTU Default)**
- **Front View (FV)** is central.
- **Top View (TV)** is placed *below* the FV.
- **Left Side View (LSV)** is placed on the *right* of the FV.
- *Mnemonic: "In First-Angle, Top goes Below, Left goes Right."*

**C. Line Conventions (BIS SP 46:1988)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Line Types Every KTU Student Must Memorize:**
> 1. **Continuous Thick Line** — visible edges, outlines (line weight ≈ 0.7 mm).
> 2. **Continuous Thin Line** — dimension lines, extension lines, hatching, leader lines (≈ 0.35 mm).
> 3. **Dashed Thin Line** — hidden edges.
> 4. **Chain Thin Line (with thick ends)** — cutting planes, section views.
> 5. **Chain Thin Line (with thin ends)** — centerlines, axes of symmetry.
> 6. **Phantom Thin Line** — alternative positions, repeated detail.

**D. Dimensioning Rules (BIS 11669 / ISO 129)**
- Dimensions must be placed *outside* the view wherever possible.
- Units in **mm** (no need to write "mm").
- Angular dimensions in **degrees**.
- Arrows or oblique strokes may be used as terminators.

**E. Scales (BIS 10718 / ISO 5455)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scale** is the ratio of the *drawn size* to the *actual size* of the object.
> $$\text{Scale Factor (RF)} = \frac{\text{Drawn Length}}{\text{Actual Length}}$$
> Where **RF** stands for **Representative Fraction**.

**F. Sectioning and Hatching**
- Hatching is done with **continuous thin lines at 45°**, spaced **1 to 3 mm apart**.
- Different materials have different hatching symbols (e.g., cast iron has crossed lines; wood has wavy lines).

**G. Limits, Fits, and Tolerances**
- Fundamental deviation and tolerance grade determine the limits of size for shafts and holes — foundational for interchangeable manufacturing.

---

### 2.2 Free-Hand Sketching — Operating Principles

- Sketching is done **without instruments** — only a **soft pencil (HB or 2B)** and paper.
- The goal is **proportion, not precision**.
- Proportions are estimated using **"box" or "envelope" method** — first draw a bounding box of correct aspect ratio, then sketch the object inside.
- Lines should be drawn in **continuous sweeping motions** of the wrist and forearm, not short hesitant strokes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Free-hand sketching is a thinking tool, not a communication tool.** It is used in design ideation, concept generation, and field notes — not in final manufacturing documentation.

---

### 2.3 Manual Drawing — Operating Principles

- Performed on a **drafting board** with **T-square, set-squares (45° and 30°-60°), compass, dividers, protractor, French curves, and a scale**.
- Pencil grades: **2H / H for construction lines**, **HB for object lines**, **2B for lettering**.
- Drawing sheets are pinned or taped to the board; borders and title blocks are drawn first.

---

### 2.4 CAD Drawing — Operating Principles

- Performed using software like **AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo Parametric, Siemens NX, or Fusion 360**.
- The workflow is: **2D Sketch → 3D Model → Drawing View Generation → Dimensioning → Plotting/Exporting to PDF/DWG**.
- CAD drawings are stored in **vector format** (mathematical coordinates, not pixels), ensuring infinite zoom without loss of quality.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Exam (Memorize all)**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Representative Fraction (RF) | $\text{RF} = \dfrac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Enlargement Scale RF | $\text{RF} = \dfrac{\text{Number of times enlarged}}{1}$ | e.g., 2:1 means drawn 2× the actual |
| 3 | Reduction Scale RF | $\text{RF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Number of times reduced}}$ | e.g., 1:5 means drawn 1/5 of actual |
| 4 | Actual Length from Drawing | $L_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{\text{RF}}$ | mm |
| 5 | Drawn Length from Actual | $L_{\text{drawn}} = L_{\text{actual}} \times \text{RF}$ | mm |
| 6 | Standard Scale Ratios (BIS) | 1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:5000 | Recommended scale series |
| 7 | Enlargement Scale Ratios (BIS) | 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 50:1, 100:1 | For small components |
| 8 | Sheet Area Ratio (A-series) | $A_{n-1} = 2 \times A_n$ | Each halving doubles count |
| 9 | Long side of sheet | $L_n = L_0 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^n$ | n = 0 for A0, n = 1 for A1, etc. |
| 10 | Short side of sheet | $S_n = L_0 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n+1}$ | Same series as above |
| 11 | Line Weight Ratio (Thick:Thin) | $2 : 1$ | e.g., 0.7 mm : 0.35 mm |
| 12 | Hatching Line Spacing | $1 \text{ to } 3 \text{ mm}$ apart, at 45° | Uniform across one part |
| 13 | Lettering Height (BIS) | $\geq 2.5 \text{ mm}$ for dimensions | Capital letters preferred |
| 14 | Isometric Axis Angle | 30°, 30°, 90° between axes | Foreshortening factor = 0.816 |
| 15 | Isometric Length Correction | $L_{\text{iso}} = L_{\text{actual}} \times 0.816$ | Because cos(35.264°) ≈ 0.816 |

> **Note on line weight ratio** — for B-series or heavy technical drawings, ratios of **4:1** may also be used.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Every car part is designed in CAD and documented via orthographic + sectional views.
- **Aerospace:** Assembly drawings with GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing) ensure jet engine parts fit to micron-level precision.
- **Civil Engineering:** Building plans, structural drawings, and reinforcement details.
- **Manufacturing:** Without proper drawings, CNC machines, 3D printers, and assembly lines cannot function.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — A-Series Sheet Geometry from First Principles

**Given:** A0 sheet area = **1 m²** and aspect ratio = $1 : \sqrt{2}$.

Let the short side = $a$ and the long side = $b$.
Then $b = a\sqrt{2}$ and $a \times b = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$.

**Step 1:** Substitute the second relation into the area equation.

$$a \times a\sqrt{2} = 1{,}000{,}000$$

$$a^2 \sqrt{2} = 1{,}000{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $a$ (short side of A0).

$$a^2 = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.41421356}$$

$$a^2 \approx 707{,}106.78 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$a \approx \sqrt{707{,}106.78} \approx 840.90 \text{ mm} \approx 841 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $b$ (long side of A0).

$$b = a\sqrt{2} = 841 \times 1.41421356 \approx 1189 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, **A0 = 1189 mm × 841 mm** (matches BIS/ISO standard). Each subsequent size halves the long side.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Finding Actual Length from a Scaled Drawing

**Problem:** A machine part is drawn at a scale of **1:5**, and a feature on the drawing measures **37 mm**. Find the actual size.

**Step 1:** Recall the RF formula.

$$\text{RF} = \frac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{L_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{1}{5}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for actual length.

$$L_{\text{actual}} = \frac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{\text{RF}} = \frac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{1/5}$$

$$L_{\text{actual}} = L_{\text{drawn}} \times 5$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical value.

$$L_{\text{actual}} = 37 \times 5 = 185 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** The actual feature size is **185 mm**. The drawing is a **reduction scale** because RF < 1.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drawing Length from Enlargement Scale

**Problem:** A small gear pin of actual diameter **8 mm** must be drawn on a sheet at a **10:1 enlargement**. Find the drawn diameter.

**Step 1:** For enlargement, RF = 10:1 = 10.

**Step 2:** Apply the drawn-length formula.

$$L_{\text{drawn}} = L_{\text{actual}} \times \text{RF}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$L_{\text{drawn}} = 8 \times 10 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** The gear pin will be drawn as a **80 mm diameter circle** on the sheet, making tiny features clearly visible and measurable.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Isometric Length Correction Factor

**Given:** Isometric projection uses axes at 30° to the horizontal. The true projected length of a line along an isometric axis is shorter than the actual length.

**Step 1:** An isometric view is formed by rotating the object 45° about the vertical axis and tilting it 35.264° from the horizontal.

**Step 2:** The foreshortening factor is the cosine of the tilt angle.

$$f = \cos(35.264°) \approx 0.816$$

**Step 3:** So any line drawn along the isometric axis must be scaled by 0.816 to represent its true length.

**Step 4:** For an actual edge of **100 mm**:

$$L_{\text{iso}} = 100 \times 0.816 = 81.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** For convenience, draftsmen often draw at 1:1 along the isometric axes (called **isometric drawing**, not *projection*) and simply rely on the visual approximation.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Drawing Methods

| Parameter | Free-Hand Sketching | Manual Drawing | CAD Drawing |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Tool/Medium** | Pencil + paper | Drafting board + instruments | Software (AutoCAD, SolidWorks) |
| **Precision** | Low (proportion-only) | High (instrument-grade) | Very high (up to 0.001 mm) |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Slowest | Moderate (initial setup slow) |
| **Revision/Editing** | Redraw from scratch | Redraw with eraser | Easy (undo, parametric) |
| **Storage** | Paper files (physical) | Paper files (physical) | Digital files (cloud/local) |
| **Distribution** | Photocopy/scan | Photocopy/scan | Email, cloud, print directly |
| **Cost** | Very low | Moderate (equipment) | High (software + hardware) |
| **Skill Required** | Visual estimation | Draftsmanship | Software + drafting knowledge |
| **Use Case** | Ideation, brainstorming | Exam, learning fundamentals | Industry, manufacturing |
| **KTU Relevance** | Viva + design notebooks | End-semester lab exam | Lab CAD exercises + 3D modelling |
| **Output Type** | Sketch | 2D drawing | 2D drawing + 3D model + assembly |

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU 2024 PCRUL307 Lab:** Students will perform **manual drawing** in the record and **CAD modelling + drawing extraction** in the computer session. Free-hand sketching appears in the **viva voce**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define *Representative Fraction (RF)*. A machine component of actual length **240 mm** is drawn at a scale of **1:4**. Calculate the length of the component as it appears on the drawing.

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Representative Fraction (RF)* is the ratio of the *drawn length* of an object to its *actual length*, expressed as a dimensionless fraction.
>
> $$\text{RF} = \frac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}$$
>
> **Calculation (1.5 marks):**
> Given: $L_{\text{actual}} = 240$ mm, Scale = 1:4 → RF = 1/4
>
> $$L_{\text{drawn}} = L_{\text{actual}} \times \text{RF} = 240 \times \frac{1}{4} = 60 \text{ mm}$$
>
> **The component will appear as 60 mm on the drawing.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List any **six types of lines** used in engineering drawing as per **BIS SP 46:1988**, stating the specific application of each.

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 marks per line type):**
>
> | # | Line Type | Application |
> |---|-----------|-------------|
> | 1 | Continuous Thick Line | Visible edges and outlines of objects |
> | 2 | Continuous Thin Line | Dimension lines, extension lines, hatching, leader lines |
> | 3 | Dashed Thin Line | Hidden edges not visible in the view |
> | 4 | Chain Thin Line with Thick Ends | Cutting plane lines and viewing direction for sections |
> | 5 | Chain Thin Line with Thin Ends | Centre lines, axes of symmetry, pitch circles |
> | 6 | Phantom Thin Line | Alternative positions, repeated detail, adjacent parts |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principles of orthographic projection** as applied in **first-angle projection**. With a neat sketch, describe the arrangement of the **six principal views** on a drawing sheet. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Distinguish between **First-Angle Projection** and **Third-Angle Projection**. State which system is followed in **India as per BIS** and justify why. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Principles of Orthographic Projection in First-Angle (7 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Orthographic projection is a method of representing a 3D object using parallel projectors perpendicular to the projection plane, producing a 2D view that preserves true shape and size.
>
> **Principles (3 marks):**
> - The object is imagined to be placed in the **first quadrant** of the projection system (between the observer and the plane of projection).
> - Projection planes are placed *behind* the object.
> - Each plane gives one view: VP → Front View, HP → Top View, PP → Left/Right Side View.
> - The planes are then **unfolded** (rotated 90°) to lie in a single plane (the paper).
> - Lines are drawn using the **"projection line"** method — projectors travel from one view to another.
>
> **Six Principal Views Arrangement (2 marks):**
> In first-angle projection, the arrangement on the sheet is:
> - **Top View** is placed *below* the Front View.
> - **Bottom View** is placed *above* the Front View.
> - **Left Side View** is placed on the *right* of the Front View.
> - **Right Side View** is placed on the *left* of the Front View.
> - **Rear View** is placed on the *extreme right or left* (typically extreme right).
>
> ---
>
> **(b) First-Angle vs Third-Angle Projection (7 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | First-Angle Projection | Third-Angle Projection |
> |-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
> | **Symbol** | Truncated cone with small end towards observer | Truncated cone with small end away from observer |
> | **Object Placement** | Between observer and projection plane | Projection plane between observer and object |
> | **Quadrant Used** | First quadrant | Third quadrant |
> | **Top View Position** | Below Front View | Above Front View |
> | **Left Side View Position** | On the right of FV | On the left of FV |
> | **Standard Adopted** | ISO Standard, BIS (India, Europe, Asia) | ANSI Standard (USA, Canada) |
> | **KTU / Indian Standard** | **Adopted** | Not used in India |
>
> **Justification (2 marks):** India follows **BIS SP 46:1988**, which is harmonized with **ISO 128**. The ISO standard mandates **first-angle projection** for all engineering drawings, hence it is the official system used in Indian engineering colleges, public sector industries, and design offices.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** What is a **scale** in engineering drawing? Explain the **types of scales** with examples. A line of actual length **125 m** is to be represented on a drawing at a scale of **1:2500**. Find the drawn length in **mm**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **standard drawing sheet sizes** as per **BIS/ISO**, with the derivation of the A0 sheet dimensions. List the recommended scale ratios (both enlargement and reduction) as per BIS. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Scale in Engineering Drawing (7 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** A *scale* is the ratio between the linear dimensions of the drawing and the actual dimensions of the object. It is expressed as the **Representative Fraction (RF)**.
>
> $$\text{RF} = \frac{\text{Drawing Length}}{\text{Actual Length}}$$
>
> **Types of Scales (3 marks):**
>
> | Type | Definition | Example |
> |------|------------|---------|
> | **Full Size Scale** | Drawing = Actual Size; RF = 1:1 | 1:1 |
> | **Reduction Scale** | Drawing smaller than object; RF < 1 | 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:50, 1:100 |
> | **Enlargement Scale** | Drawing larger than object; RF > 1 | 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1 |
>
> **Calculation (2 marks):**
> Given: Actual length = 125 m = 125,000 mm; Scale = 1:2500
>
> $$L_{\text{drawn}} = L_{\text{actual}} \times \text{RF} = 125{,}000 \times \frac{1}{2500}$$
>
> $$L_{\text{drawn}} = \frac{125{,}000}{2500} = 50 \text{ mm}$$
>
> **The drawn length is 50 mm.**
>
> ---
>
> **(b) Standard Sheet Sizes & Derivation (7 marks):**
>
> **A-Series Rules (2 marks):**
> - The A-series is based on an aspect ratio of $1 : \sqrt{2}$.
> - A0 has an area of **1 m²**.
> - Each successive size is obtained by **halving the long side**.
>
> **A0 Derivation (3 marks):**
> Let short side = $a$, long side = $a\sqrt{2}$.
> Area: $a \times a\sqrt{2} = 1{,}000{,}000$ mm²
>
> $$a^2 = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.414} \approx 707{,}107$$
>
> $$a \approx 841 \text{ mm}; \quad a\sqrt{2} \approx 1189 \text{ mm}$$
>
> Therefore, **A0 = 1189 mm × 841 mm**.
>
> **BIS Recommended Scales (2 marks):**
>
> **Reduction:** 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:5000
> **Enlargement:** 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 50:1, 100:1

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Examiner's Eye View:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "mm" rule:** BIS allows omission of the "mm" symbol after dimension values, but students often write "mm" inside the dimension line — half-mark cut.
> 2. **Confusing first-angle and third-angle symbols:** The truncated cone symbol in the title block is a 1-mark question — students draw it backwards. *First-angle: small end towards observer.*
> 3. **Wrong scale interpretation:** Writing "1:5" as "5:1" or vice-versa causes full-marks loss in calculation problems.
> 4. **No units in RF:** RF is a ratio, but in KTU valuation, students who write "RF = 1:5 mm" are penalized for implying a unit to a dimensionless number.
> 5. **Missing title block information:** Name, Roll No, Sheet No, Scale, Date, Institution, and Drawing Title are compulsory. Missing two = 1 mark cut.
> 6. **Hatching errors:** Different parts in a sectional view must have hatching in the **same direction and spacing**. Different materials have *different* symbols, but **same material parts** must look identical.
> 7. **Centerline omission:** Every circle, hole, and cylindrical feature must have a centerline (chain thin line). Missing centerline = 0.5 mark per feature.
> 8. **Dimensioning on hidden lines:** Dimensions should be placed on visible views whenever possible; dimensioning on hidden lines is penalized unless unavoidable.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**
>
> - **ED = Universal Language of Engineers**, governed by **BIS SP 46:1988** and **ISO 128**.
> - **India uses First-Angle Projection**; **USA uses Third-Angle Projection**.
> - **Mnemonic for First-Angle:** *"Top Goes Below, Left Goes Right."*
> - **A0 Sheet = 1189 × 841 mm**, area = 1 m², ratio = $1:\sqrt{2}$.
> - **RF formula:** $\text{RF} = L_{\text{drawn}} / L_{\text{actual}}$.
> - **Six Line Types:** Continuous thick, Continuous thin, Dashed thin, Chain thick-end, Chain thin-end, Phantom thin.
> - **Line Weight Ratio:** 2:1 (thick:thin) — e.g., 0.7 mm : 0.35 mm.
> - **Hatching:** 45°, 1–3 mm spacing, continuous thin lines, *same part = same direction*.
> - **Standard Scales (Reduction):** 1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:5000.
> - **Standard Scales (Enlargement):** 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 50:1, 100:1.
> - **Isometric axes angle = 120° between axes**; foreshortening factor = **0.816**.
> - **Free-Hand = Ideation**, **Manual = Precision 2D**, **CAD = 2D + 3D + Parametric**.
> - **CAD Workflow:** Sketch → 2D Draft → 3D Model → Assembly → Drawing → Dimensioning → Title Block → Plot/Export.
> - **Title block must contain:** Name, Roll No, Sheet No, Scale, Date, Title, Institution.
> - **Letter height:** ≥ 2.5 mm; capital letters preferred; BIS font.
> - **Three pillars for KTU PCRUL307 lab success:** (1) Manual drawing record, (2) CAD solid model submission, (3) Viva on free-hand + principles.
> - **Always state units only in the title block** (e.g., "All dimensions in mm") — never on every dimension.
> - **Dimensioning rule of thumb:** Dimensions should be placed *between the two views they belong to* whenever possible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing: BIS Specifications, Line Types, Dimensioning, Sectional Views, Welding Symbols, Riveted Joints, Keys, and Fasteners (Bolts, Nuts, Screws)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing: BIS Specifications, Line Types, Dimensioning, Sectional Views, Welding Symbols, Riveted Joints, Keys, and Fasteners (Bolts, Nuts, Screws)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — CODE OF PRACTICE FOR ENGINEERING DRAWING

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing** refers to the unified set of rules, conventions, and graphical standards published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — primarily codified under **IS 696 (1972)** "Code of Practice for General Engineering Drawing" and supplemented by **SP 46:1988** "Engineering Drawing Practice for Schools and Colleges." These standards govern the preparation, reading, and interpretation of engineering drawings so that a drawing prepared in one part of the country (or world) can be unambiguously understood and manufactured in any other location.

For machine drawing specifically, the relevant sub-standards include:

- **IS 696** – General engineering drawing principles
- **SP 46:1988** – Drawing practice (educational/professional)
- **IS 813** – Welding symbols
- **IS 2709** – Riveting (proportions of rivets and riveting)
- **IS 1363 / IS 1364** – Hexagon head bolts, screws and nuts
- **IS 4218** – ISO Metric Screw Threads
- **IS 2292 / IS 2302** – Keys and Keyways
- **IS 9608** – Countersunk head bolts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** For PCRUL307, students must demonstrate drawings that *strictly* follow BIS conventions. A drawing using incorrect line weights, missing centerlines, or wrongly placed dimension lines will be penalized heavily in the lab viva and final assessment.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Grammar of Engineering"

Think of an engineering drawing as a **language** and the BIS Code of Practice as its **grammar and dictionary**.

- **Alphabet** = Different line types (thick, thin, dashed, center)
- **Vocabulary** = Symbols (welding, fasteners, surface finish)
- **Grammar** = Dimensioning rules (where to place dimensions, alignment, units)
- **Punctuation** = Sectional views, hatching, breaks

Just as two people speaking different dialects cannot communicate, two engineers using different drawing conventions cannot share a design. BIS standards act as the universal "English" of Indian engineering graphics.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

1. **Lab Component (Continuous Assessment):** Every drawing produced in the lab must conform to BIS. Marks are deducted for non-conformance.
2. **Viva Questions:** Examiners frequently ask students to recite line types, BIS codes, or proportions of standard machine elements.
3. **Industry Readiness:** Indian industries (ISRO, BHEL, L&T, Tata Motors) follow BIS drawings for production. Fluency in these conventions is a placement skill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants/Standards to Memorize:**
> - Drawing sheet sizes: **A0 (1189 × 841 mm), A1, A2, A3, A4** (per IS 10711)
> - Default line thickness: **0.6 mm for thick lines, 0.25 mm for thin lines** (ratio 2:1)
> - Default text height: **5 mm (titles), 3.5 mm (dimensions), 2.5 mm (notes)** per SP 46
> - Drawing scale: must be indicated in the title block
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — BIS DRAWING RULES

## 2.1 Line Types (IS 696, Type of Lines)

The fundamental building block of any engineering drawing. Each line carries a specific meaning and must be drawn consistently.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Lines

| Line Type | Appearance (Group) | Application | Thickness |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Type 01 — Visible Outline** | Continuous thick | Visible edges, outlines of objects | Thick (0.6 mm) |
| **Type 02 — Hidden Line** | Continuous thin, short dashes | Concealed edges | Thin (0.25 mm) |
| **Type 03 — Center Line** | Long dash, short dash, long dash | Axes of symmetry, centers of circles | Thin |
| **Type 04 — Construction Line** | Continuous thin | Auxiliary construction | Thin |
| **Type 05 — Section Line (Cutting Plane)** | Long dash, dot, long dash | Indicates cutting plane in sectioning | Thick (chain thin) |
| **Type 06 — Short Break Line** | Freehand thick | Short breaks in objects | Thick |
| **Type 07 — Long Break Line** | Straight thin with zig-zag | Long breaks | Thin |
| **Type 08 — Phantom Line** | Long dash, double dot | Alternate positions, repetitive detail | Thin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centerlines must extend slightly beyond the feature** they identify (about 3 mm gap-free on each side). The intersection of two centerlines uses a **small cross (×)** drawn with thick lines for clarity, NOT a 90° junction of dashes.

### 2.1.2 Line Hierarchy (Priority)

When multiple lines coincide at one location, the priority (highest first) is:
1. Visible outline (thick continuous)
2. Hidden line (thin dashed)
3. Cutting plane line
4. Centerline
5. Dimension / extension / leader lines

## 2.2 Dimensioning (IS 696, Cl. 18–22)

Dimensioning is the act of **describing the size and location** of features on a drawing. It is not arbitrary — every dimension must be:

- **Necessary** (no duplicate dimensions)
- **Correct** (numerically accurate)
- **Readable** (clear, no crossing)
- **Toleranced** (when required, in a later session)

### 2.2.1 Elements of Dimensioning

| Element | Style | Notes |
|---------|-------|-------|
| **Dimension line** | Thin continuous, with arrowheads at ends | Drawn between extension lines |
| **Extension line** | Thin continuous, offset 1–2 mm from feature | Must not touch the outline |
| **Leader line** | Thin continuous, with arrowhead or dot at one end | For notes, surface finish, holes |
| **Dimension text** | Numerals above or in a break in the dimension line | Readable, no ambiguity |

### 2.2.2 Rules of Dimensioning (KTU Favorite Viva Topic)

1. Dimensions should be placed **outside the view** wherever possible, between the view and its adjacent view.
2. **Aligned dimensioning:** text aligned with the dimension line (used in linear dimensions).
3. **Unidirectional dimensioning:** text reads horizontally on the sheet, regardless of the dimension line's angle (preferred per IS 696 for engineering drawings).
4. **Diameters** must be prefixed with the symbol **⌀**.
5. **Radii** must be prefixed with **R** and the center marked with a small cross.
6. **Avoid dimensioning to hidden lines** whenever possible.
7. Dimensions should be placed on the view that shows the **characteristic shape** of the feature.

## 2.3 Sectional Views (IS 696, Cl. 14–17)

A section view shows what would be seen if the object were **cut** along a chosen plane and the near portion removed. It is used to reveal **internal features** that are otherwise hidden behind outer surfaces.

### 2.3.1 Components of a Sectional View

1. **Cutting Plane Line (CPL):** Long-dash-dot chain line, thick; indicates where the imaginary cut is made. Labeled with letters (A–A) and arrows showing viewing direction.
2. **Hatching (Section Lines):** Thin continuous parallel lines drawn at **45°** to the principal outline. Spacing approximately 3–5 mm apart. The **same component, when sectioned, must have the same hatching direction and spacing** in all views.
3. **Section View Label:** "SECTION A–A" placed above the view.

### 2.3.2 Conventions and Rules

- **Different parts in assembly** have hatching at **different angles** (e.g., 45° and 135°) or with **different spacing** to distinguish materials.
- **Ribs, webs, spokes, and bolts/nuts** in a sectional view are **NOT hatched** even though the cutting plane passes through them (per IS 696 Cl. 15.2).
- **Holes, shafts, keys, pins, rivets** are shown in section without hatching when the cut is **longitudinal** (along the axis).
- **Transverse cuts** through these elements DO show hatching.
- Hatching must **stop short of the outline** by about 1 mm.

### 2.3.3 Types of Sectional Views

| Section Type | Application |
|--------------|-------------|
| Full Section | Cutting plane passes completely through object |
| Half Section | Symmetrical objects; one half in section, other half in view |
| Offset Section | Cutting plane offset to pass through multiple features |
| Aligned Section | Angled features rotated into the cutting plane |
| Removed Section | Section drawn outside the view, attached by leader |
| Revolved Section | Section drawn on the view itself (e.g., rib profile) |

## 2.4 Welding Symbols (IS 813:1986)

IS 813 is the Indian Standard (technically aligned with **AWS A2.4 / ISO 2553**) that defines the symbolic representation of welds on engineering drawings.

### 2.4.1 Anatomy of a Welding Symbol

A complete weld symbol has **eight reference positions**, but only a few are used commonly. The basic structure is:

```
           ├─── Fillet / Groove reference
    ───────┼────── 
           └─── Supplementary
   Tail ─── Reference line ─── Arrow
            (above)            (points to joint)
           └─── Supplementary (below)
```

| Element | Location | Meaning |
|---------|----------|---------|
| **Arrow line** | Connects reference line to joint | Points to the location of the weld |
| **Reference line** | Horizontal | Carries all weld information |
| **Weld symbol** | Below the arrow side of reference line | Indicates type of weld (fillet, butt, etc.) |
| **Dimensions / length / pitch** | To the right of weld symbol | E.g., 6 (throat), 50 (length) |
| **Tail** | Left of reference line | Carries supplementary info (process, specification) |

### 2.4.2 Common Welding Symbols

| Symbol | Weld Type |
|--------|-----------|
| **△** (filled triangle) | Fillet weld |
| **⌵** (open V) | Single V butt weld |
| **⌶** (open square) | Square butt weld |
| **>** (open V tilted) | Single bevel butt weld |
| **U** | U-groove butt weld |
| **⏉** | Plug or slot weld |
| **⌒** | Spot weld |
| **⌇** | Seam weld |

> [!NOTE]
> **Arrow side vs. Other side rule:**
> - Weld symbol **below** the reference line = weld on the **arrow side** (the side the arrow points to).
> - Weld symbol **above** the reference line = weld on the **other side** (away from arrow).
> - Symbol on **both sides** (above and below) = weld on **both sides**.

## 2.5 Riveted Joints (IS 800 / IS 2709)

A **rivet** is a permanent mechanical fastener — a ductile metal pin with a head on one end, inserted through overlapping/butting plates and the other end formed (headed) to clamp the joint.

### 2.5.1 Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Pitch (p)** | Distance between centers of two adjacent rivets in a row |
| **Back pitch (p_b)** | Distance between rows of rivets (perpendicular to the load) |
| **Diagonal pitch (p_d)** | Distance between two diagonal rivets in adjacent rows |
| **Gauge (g)** | Transverse distance between adjacent rows |
| **Edge distance (e)** | Distance from center of rivet hole to nearest edge of plate |
| **Margin (m)** | Distance from edge of plate to center of rivet hole (= e + hole_radius) |
| **Lap (L)** | Amount of overlap in lap joint |
| **Tearing/Crushing efficiency** | Ratio of strength of riveted section to strength of plate |

### 2.5.2 Types of Riveted Joints

**By function:**
- **Lap joint** (single, double) — plates overlap
- **Butt joint** (single plate strap, double plate strap) — plates meet end-to-end

**By arrangement of rivets:**
- **Chain riveting** — rivets in each row are in straight line
- **Zig-zag riveting** — rivets in adjacent rows are staggered

### 2.5.3 Standard Proportions (Unwin's Formula and BIS Recommendations)

For **Unwin's empirical formula** (commonly used in KTU textbooks):

$$
d = 6 \sqrt{t}
$$

where **d** = nominal rivet diameter (mm), **t** = thickness of the thinnest plate (mm).

Standard pitches and edge distances (per IS 800:2007, Cl. 10.2.3):

$$
p = 2.5d \quad \text{(minimum, for chain riveting in tension members)}
$$

$$
p = 1.5d \quad \text{(minimum, for zig-zag riveting)}
$$

$$
e = 1.5d \quad \text{(edge distance for sheared/hand-cut edges)}
$$

$$
e = 1.25d \quad \text{(edge distance for rolled/machine-cut edges)}
$$

Maximum pitch: $p_{max} = 32t$ or **300 mm**, whichever is less (in tension members).

## 2.6 Keys (IS 2292, IS 2302)

A **key** is a small metal piece inserted between a shaft and a hub (e.g., gear, pulley) to prevent relative rotational motion.

### 2.6.1 Types of Keys

| Type | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Sunk key** | Embedded partially in shaft and hub |
| **Parallel sunk key** | Uniform cross-section (rectangular), used for heavy duty |
| **Taper sunk key** | Tapered on top (1:100 slope), for tight fit |
| **Woodruff key** | Half-disc shaped, self-aligning |
| **Gib-head key** | Tapered with a head for easy removal |
| **Saddle key** | Sits in a depression on the shaft, no shear load capability |
| **Round key** | Circular cross-section pin |

### 2.6.2 Standard Proportions of Parallel Sunk Key (per IS 2292)

For shaft diameter $d$ (mm):

$$
w = \frac{d}{4}
$$

$$
h = \frac{7d}{24} \quad \approx \frac{d}{3.43}
$$

The key length $L$ is typically chosen between **1.5d to 2d**, subject to hub length.

The **keyway depth in shaft** is $t_1 = h/2 = 7d/48$.

The **keyway depth in hub** is $t_2 = h - t_1 = 7d/48$.

## 2.7 Fasteners — Bolts, Nuts, Screws (IS 1363, IS 1364, IS 4218)

These are the most common detachable fasteners in machine assemblies.

### 2.7.1 Types of Thread Profiles

| Thread | Standard | Application |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **ISO Metric (M)** | IS 4218 (ISO 261, 724) | General purpose, the KTU default |
| **BSP / BSW / BSF** | IS 554, 6151 | Legacy / plumbing |
| **Acme / Trapezoidal** | IS 7002 | Power transmission (lead screws) |
| **Square thread** | — | Heavy load, low efficiency (e.g., jacks) |

### 2.7.2 ISO Metric Thread — Coarse Pitch Series (Partial Table)

| Nominal ⌀ d (mm) | Pitch P (mm) | Minor ⌀ d₁ (mm) | Pitch ⌀ d₂ (mm) | Stress area A_s (mm²) |
|------------------|--------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| **M3** | 0.5 | 2.459 | 2.675 | 5.03 |
| **M5** | 0.8 | 4.019 | 4.480 | 14.20 |
| **M6** | 1.0 | 4.773 | 5.350 | 20.10 |
| **M8** | 1.25 | 6.466 | 7.188 | 36.60 |
| **M10** | 1.5 | 8.160 | 9.026 | 58.00 |
| **M12** | 1.75 | 9.853 | 10.863 | 84.30 |
| **M16** | 2.0 | 13.546 | 14.701 | 157.00 |
| **M20** | 2.5 | 16.933 | 18.376 | 245.00 |
| **M24** | 3.0 | 20.319 | 22.051 | 353.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The nominal diameter d in "M8 × 1.25" indicates a metric thread of 8 mm nominal diameter and 1.25 mm pitch. The basic triangle is an equilateral triangle with apex angle **60°**.

### 2.7.3 Geometric Relationships for ISO Metric Threads

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P
$$

$$
d_1 = d - 1.0825 \, P
$$

$$
h = 0.8660 \, P \quad \text{(fundamental triangle height)}
$$

$$
H = \frac{5}{8} H_{1} = 0.5413 \, P \quad \text{(actual thread depth)}
$$

### 2.7.4 Hexagonal Head Bolt (IS 1363)

For bolt of nominal diameter $d$:

| Parameter | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| Width across flats (A/F) | $1.5d + 3$ mm (min) |
| Width across corners (A/C) | $1.5d + 3$ / $\cos 30°$ ≈ $1.732 \, (1.5d + 3)$ |
| Head height (k) | $0.7d$ |
| Washer face diameter (dw) | $1.5d$ |
| Fillet radius at head-shank junction | $0.1d$ to $0.2d$ |
| Length of thread (b) | $2d + 6$ mm (for $L \le 125$ mm); $2d + 12$ mm (for $L \le 200$ mm) |

### 2.7.5 Hexagonal Nut (IS 1363)

| Parameter | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| Width across flats (A/F) | $1.5d + 3$ mm (same as bolt head) |
| Nut thickness (m) | $0.8d$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Thread Representation Conventions (per IS 4218, IS 696):**
> - **External threads (bolt):** Major diameter = continuous thick line; Minor diameter = continuous thin line (broken at ~30° of a circle in end view).
> - **Internal threads (nut/tapped hole):** Major diameter = thin line; Minor diameter = thick line. Reverse of external threads.
> - In a sectional view, hatching lines **pass through the minor diameter (thin) line** of internal threads and **stop at the major diameter (thick) line** of external threads.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Rapid Revision Table

| # | Topic | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Rivet diameter (Unwin) | $d = 6\sqrt{t}$ | mm | $t$ = thinner plate thickness |
| 2 | Pitch (chain) | $p = 2.5d$ | mm | IS 800 |
| 3 | Pitch (zigzag) | $p = 1.5d$ | mm | IS 800 |
| 4 | Edge distance | $e = 1.5d$ | mm | Sheared edge |
| 5 | Key width | $w = d/4$ | mm | IS 2292 |
| 6 | Key height | $h = 7d/24$ | mm | IS 2292 |
| 7 | Thread pitch ⌀ | $d_2 = d - 0.6495P$ | mm | ISO metric |
| 8 | Thread minor ⌀ | $d_1 = d - 1.0825P$ | mm | ISO metric |
| 9 | Thread depth | $H = 0.5413P$ | mm | ISO metric |
| 10 | Hex A/F | $1.5d + 3$ | mm | IS 1363 |
| 11 | Hex bolt head ht | $k = 0.7d$ | mm | IS 1363 |
| 12 | Hex nut thickness | $m = 0.8d$ | mm | IS 1363 |
| 13 | Counterbore ⌀ | $1.5d$ | mm | Socket head |
| 14 | Counterbore depth | $h$ | mm | Equals head height |
| 15 | Hole clearance | $1.05d$ | mm | Clearance hole |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (CNC, Tooling):** ISO metric thread tables are used to program lathes and CNC mills. M-code tap cycles rely on pitch and depth data.
- **Structural Engineering (Steel Structures):** IS 800 uses rivet-pitch and edge-distance formulas to certify steel buildings, bridges, and towers.
- **Automotive & Aerospace:** Hex bolt and nut proportions in the KTU tables match shop-floor drawings, ensuring global procurement compatibility.
- **Maintenance and Repair:** A field technician can replace any bolt on a Tata or Mahindra vehicle by referring only to the A/F dimension ($1.5d + 3$) and wrench size.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Unwin's Rivet Diameter Formula

### Background

Empirically, **David Unwin** (1880) studied riveted plate joints in boilers and pressure vessels and observed that the diameter of the rivet must increase with plate thickness, but **not linearly**. Trials showed that shear stress and bearing stress gave acceptable values only when $d \propto \sqrt{t}$.

### Step 1: Set up the core hypothesis

The rivet diameter $d$ should be proportional to the square root of the plate thickness $t$:

$$
d = k \sqrt{t}
$$

### Step 2: Calibrate the constant $k$ from test data

For a mild steel plate of thickness $t = 10$ mm, an experimental optimum rivet diameter was approximately $d = 19$ mm. Substituting:

$$
19 = k \sqrt{10}
$$

$$
k = \frac{19}{\sqrt{10}} \approx \frac{19}{3.162} \approx 6.01
$$

### Step 3: Adopt the integer form for design

Rounding to a design-friendly integer:

$$
k \approx 6
$$

### Step 4: Final form

$$
\boxed{\, d = 6 \sqrt{t} \,} \quad \text{(in mm)}
$$

**Verification for $t = 10$ mm:** $d = 6 \sqrt{10} = 18.97 \approx 19$ mm ✓

> [!NOTE]
> This empirical formula is widely used in textbook problems but is superseded in **IS 800:2007** by table-based design based on calculated shear and bearing capacities. The KTU syllabus, however, still expects familiarity with this formula.

## 3.2 Derivation — Thread Geometry for ISO Metric Threads

### Step 1: Define the fundamental triangle

The ISO metric thread is based on an **equilateral triangle** of side length $P$ (pitch) with apex angle **60°**. The height of this triangle is:

$$
H_1 = \frac{P}{2} \tan 30° \cdot 2 = P \tan 30° = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.8660 \, P
$$

### Step 2: Apply the truncation rule

In practice, the thread is **truncated at 1/8 of $H_1$** at both crest and root (i.e., 1/8 from top, 1/8 from bottom, leaving 5/8 in between). This gives:

$$
H = \frac{5}{8} H_1 = \frac{5}{8} \times 0.8660 \, P = 0.5413 \, P
$$

### Step 3: Derive the pitch diameter $d_2$

The pitch diameter is the **midway** diameter between major and minor diameters:

$$
d_2 = d - \frac{2 \times \frac{3}{8} H_1}{1} = d - \frac{3}{4} H_1 = d - \frac{3}{4} \times 0.8660 \, P
$$

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495 \, P
$$

### Step 4: Derive the minor diameter $d_1$

$$
d_1 = d - 2H = d - 2 \times 0.5413 \, P = d - 1.0825 \, P
$$

### Step 5: Verification with M10 × 1.5

Given $d = 10$ mm, $P = 1.5$ mm:

$$
d_2 = 10 - 0.6495 \times 1.5 = 10 - 0.974 = 9.026 \text{ mm} \checkmark
$$

$$
d_1 = 10 - 1.0825 \times 1.5 = 10 - 1.624 = 8.376 \text{ mm (table shows 8.160 due to rounding)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The minor discrepancy is because the **BIS tables round up $d_1$** to the nearest convenient dimension (8.160 mm vs computed 8.376 mm) for manufacturing convenience.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — All Standard Machine Element Proportions

| Element | Parameter | Formula | Worked Example (d = 20 mm, t = 12 mm) |
|---------|-----------|---------|--------------------------------------|
| **Rivet** | Diameter | $d_r = 6\sqrt{t}$ | $d_r = 6\sqrt{12} = 20.78 \approx 21$ mm |
| **Rivet** | Pitch (chain) | $p = 2.5 d_r$ | $p = 2.5 \times 21 = 52.5$ mm |
| **Rivet** | Edge distance | $e = 1.5 d_r$ | $e = 1.5 \times 21 = 31.5$ mm |
| **Key** | Width | $w = d/4$ | $w = 20/4 = 5$ mm |
| **Key** | Height | $h = 7d/24$ | $h = 7 \times 20/24 = 5.83$ mm |
| **Key** | Shaft keyway depth | $t_1 = h/2$ | $t_1 = 5.83/2 = 2.92$ mm |
| **Key** | Hub keyway depth | $t_2 = h/2$ | $t_2 = 2.92$ mm |
| **Bolt (Hex)** | A/F flats | $1.5d + 3$ | $1.5 \times 20 + 3 = 33$ mm |
| **Bolt (Hex)** | A/C corners | $(1.5d+3)/\cos 30°$ | $33 / 0.866 = 38.1$ mm |
| **Bolt (Hex)** | Head height | $k = 0.7d$ | $0.7 \times 20 = 14$ mm |
| **Nut (Hex)** | Thickness | $m = 0.8d$ | $0.8 \times 20 = 16$ mm |
| **Bolt (Sq)** | Side | $1.5d$ | $1.5 \times 20 = 30$ mm |
| **Bolt (Sq)** | Height | $k = 0.7d$ | $14$ mm |
| **Counterbore** | Diameter | $1.5d$ | $30$ mm |
| **Counterbore** | Depth | $k$ | $14$ mm |
| **Thread M20 × 2.5** | Pitch ⌀ $d_2$ | $d - 0.6495P$ | $20 - 1.624 = 18.376$ mm |
| **Thread M20 × 2.5** | Minor ⌀ $d_1$ | $d - 1.0825P$ | $20 - 2.706 = 17.294$ mm |
| **Thread M20 × 2.5** | Depth $H$ | $0.5413P$ | $0.5413 \times 2.5 = 1.353$ mm |

## 3.4 Dimensioning Practice — KTU Common Pattern

### Example Problem: A shaft of diameter 30 mm has a keyway. Provide the dimensional callouts per IS 696.

**Given:** Shaft Ø30, parallel sunk key per IS 2292.

**Step 1 — Compute the key dimensions:**

$$
w = \frac{d}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \text{ mm (round to standard } 8 \text{ mm)}
$$

$$
h = \frac{7d}{24} = \frac{7 \times 30}{24} = 8.75 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
t_1 = t_2 = \frac{h}{2} = 4.375 \text{ mm} \approx 4.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose a standard key (from IS 2292 table):**

For $d = 30$ mm, standard key is $w \times h = 8 \times 8$ mm with length 28–70 mm.

**Step 3 — Layout dimensions on the drawing:**

- Outer shaft diameter: **⌀30**
- Keyway width: **8**
- Keyway depth in shaft: **4.4** (dimensioned from the top of shaft surface to the bottom of keyway, OR given as $t_1$)
- Key length: **L** (per application)

**Step 4 — Apply line conventions:**

- Visible top surface of shaft: **continuous thick line**
- Bottom of keyway: **continuous thick line**
- Bottom of keyway: **continuous thick line**
- Centerline of shaft: **long-short-long center line**

## 3.5 Sectional View — Worked Example for a Hex Bolt in a Tapped Hole

**Step 1:** Draw the bolt and the tapped hole side by side or in assembly.

**Step 2:** Convert one half of the assembly to a sectional view (since the assembly is symmetrical).

**Step 3:** Apply hatching rules:

- The **tapped plate (nut/hub)** is hatched at 45°.
- The **bolt itself** is **NOT hatched** (per IS 696 Cl. 15.2 — fasteners in longitudinal section are shown without hatching).
- Hatching lines **pass through the minor (thin) diameter** of the internal thread.
- Hatching lines **stop at the major (thick) diameter** of the external thread.

**Step 4:** Label the section as "SECTION A–A."

**Step 5:** Provide dimensions: bolt diameter M, length L, depth of engagement, hole depth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often hatch the bolt body in a longitudinal section. This is **wrong** and leads to mark deduction. The bolt body should remain **unhatched**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Code of Practice Framework

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the BIS Code of Practice for Engineering Drawing is hierarchically organized. This block-level functional architecture maps the interactions between the various sub-standards under the umbrella of IS 696.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[BIS CODE OF PRACTICE] --> B1[DRAWING CONVENTIONS]
    A0 --> B2[MACHINE ELEMENTS]
    A0 --> B3[FASTENERS]
    A0 --> B4[PROCESS SYMBOLS]

    B1 --> C1[Line Types IS 696]
    B1 --> C2[Dimensioning IS 696]
    B1 --> C3[Sectioning IS 696]
    B1 --> C4[Sheet Sizes IS 10711]
    B1 --> C5[Scales SP 46]

    B2 --> C6[Riveted Joints IS 800 2709]
    B2 --> C7[Keys and Keyways IS 2292 2302]
    B2 --> C8[Shafts and Bearings]

    B3 --> C9[Bolts and Nuts IS 1363 1364]
    B3 --> C10[Screws IS 4218 4219]
    B3 --> C11[Threaded Inserts]
    B3 --> C12[Washers IS 2016]

    B4 --> C13[Welding Symbols IS 813]
    B4 --> C14[Surface Finish IS 3073]
    B4 --> C15[Tolerance Symbols IS 919]

    C1 --> D1[Thick 0.6 mm Continuous]
    C1 --> D2[Thin 0.25 mm Continuous]
    C1 --> D3[Dashed Hidden]
    C1 --> D4[Centerline Long Short Long]
    C1 --> D5[Cutting Plane Long Dash Dot]

    C9 --> E1[Hex Head Bolt]
    C9 --> E2[Square Head Bolt]
    C9 --> E3[Hex Nut]
    C9 --> E4[Lock Nut]

    C6 --> F1[Unwin Formula d equal 6 root t]
    C6 --> F2[Pitch 2.5 d chain]
    C6 --> F3[Edge Distance 1.5 d]

    C7 --> G1[Parallel Sunk]
    C7 --> G2[Taper Sunk]
    C7 --> G3[Woodruff]
    C7 --> G4[Gib Head]

    C13 --> H1[Fillet Weld]
    C13 --> H2[Butt Weld V Square Bevel U]
    C13 --> H3[Spot and Plug Weld]
    C13 --> H4[Symbol Placement Arrow Side Other Side]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Drawing Decision Workflow for Threaded Fastener Assembly

This block-level functional architecture flowchart demonstrates the logical sequence a draftsman follows when converting a verbal description into a fully dimensioned orthographic sectional drawing of a bolted joint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[START Read the Problem Statement] --> S1[Identify Bolt Diameter d and Length L]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Pitch P from ISO Table]
    S2 --> S3[Compute A/F using 1.5 d plus 3]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Head Height k equals 0.7 d]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Nut Thickness m equals 0.8 d]
    S5 --> S6[Draw Front View with Hex Heads]
    S6 --> S7[Convert to Half Section or Full Section per IS 696]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Hatching Rules Halt at Bolt Body]
    S8 --> S9[Add Thread Lines Thick Major and Thin Minor]
    S9 --> S10[Add Centerlines Long Short Long]
    S10 --> S11[Add Dimension Lines Extension Lines Leader Lines]
    S11 --> S12[Add Title Block and Scale Indicator]
    S12 --> S13[END Validate Against IS 696 and IS 1363]

    S7 --> S8a{Drawing is a Single Fastener or Assembly}
    S8a --> S8b[Single Fastener Show End View of Hex]
    S8a --> S8c[Assembly Show Both Components in Half Section]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Welding Symbol Anatomy Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Tail Supplementary Info] --> P1[Reference Line Horizontal]
    P1 --> P2[Arrow Line Points to Joint]
    P1 --> P3[Below Arrow Side Weld Symbol]
    P1 --> P4[Above Other Side Weld Symbol]
    P3 --> P5[Type of Weld Fillet Butt Spot]
    P3 --> P6[Size of Weld Throat Leg]
    P3 --> P7[Length of Weld]
    P3 --> P8[Pitch of Weld for Intermittent]
    P4 --> P9[Same Codes Apply on Other Side]
    P5 --> P10[Completed Welding Callout on Drawing]
    P6 --> P10
    P7 --> P10
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any six different line types used in engineering drawing as per IS 696, and state the relative thickness (thick or thin) and one application of each.**

**Model Answer (Six marks worth of content compressed for 3-mark scope):**

The six line types as per IS 696 are:

1. **Visible outline (continuous thick line, 0.6 mm):** Used for all visible edges of an object.
2. **Hidden line (continuous thin line, short dashes):** Used to represent edges that are not directly visible.
3. **Centerline (thin line, long-short-long dashes):** Used to indicate axes of symmetry, bolt circles, and centers of circular features.
4. **Cutting plane line (long-dash dot, thick):** Used to indicate the plane along which a sectional view is taken.
5. **Dimension/extension line (continuous thin):** Used to indicate the extent and direction of a dimension.
6. **Break line (freehand thick for short breaks, zig-zag thin for long breaks):** Used to indicate that a portion of the object has been removed to save space.

**[Self-score: 0.5 marks per correctly described line × 6 = 3 marks]**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the standard across-flats dimension of a hexagonal bolt of nominal diameter 20 mm? State the relevant BIS code.**

**Model Answer:**

For a hexagonal head bolt as per **IS 1363**, the across-flats (A/F) dimension is given by:

$$
A/F = 1.5d + 3 \text{ mm}
$$

For $d = 20$ mm:

$$
A/F = 1.5 \times 20 + 3 = 33 \text{ mm}
$$

The relevant BIS code is **IS 1363:2002** (for steel hexagon bolts and nuts).

**[Self-score: 1.5 marks for correct formula + 1.5 marks for correct substitution and answer]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student is offered an internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Compulsory]

**(a)** Sketch the standard profiles of the following machine elements on a single A4 sheet, with all BIS conventional lines and at least four dimensions each: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Hexagonal head bolt M16 × 50 (full sectional view of bolt in tapped hole).
   (ii) Hexagonal nut M16 (with standard chamfers).

**(b)** A mild steel plate of thickness **12 mm** is to be joined using a lap riveted joint. Design the joint using Unwin's formula and determine: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Rivet diameter
   (ii) Pitch for chain and zig-zag riveting
   (iii) Edge distance and margin
   (iv) Sketch the lap joint (one rivet pair) with all dimensions.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Drawing Steps for M16 × 50 Bolt in Tapped Hole:**

1. **Compute standard dimensions** for M16 bolt (using formulas):
   - Pitch $P = 2$ mm (from ISO table)
   - Pitch diameter $d_2 = 16 - 0.6495 \times 2 = 14.701$ mm
   - Minor diameter $d_1 = 16 - 1.0825 \times 2 = 13.835$ mm
   - A/F flats = $1.5 \times 16 + 3 = 27$ mm
   - A/C corners = $27 / \cos 30° = 31.18$ mm
   - Head height $k = 0.7 \times 16 = 11.2$ mm
   - Thread length $b = 2d + 6 = 38$ mm

2. **Draw the front view** of the bolt inserted into a tapped hole of depth ≈ 1.5 × bolt diameter + clearance.

3. **Convert front view to half section** (cut through the central axis):
   - The bolt and nut remain **unhatched**.
   - The tapped plate is hatched at 45° (continuous thin lines, ~3 mm spacing).
   - Hatching lines pass through the **minor diameter (thin line)** of the internal thread.
   - Hatching lines stop at the **major diameter (thick line)** of the external thread.

4. **Draw the end view** of the bolt head as a regular hexagon (inscribed in a circle of diameter 31.18 mm, A/F 27 mm).

5. **Add dimensions:**
   - Overall bolt length **50**, with thread length **38**.
   - Thread designation **M16** with the pitch callout.
   - Head height **11.2** (or labelled as $0.7d$).
   - A/F **27**.
   - Tapped hole depth.

6. **Add title block** with drawing title, scale, name, date, sheet number, and IS code reference (IS 1363, IS 4218).

**[Marking distribution: 1.5 marks for front view + 1.5 marks for end view + 1 mark for sectioning convention + 1.5 marks for dimensioning + 1 mark for title block and line quality = 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Rivet diameter using Unwin's formula:**

$$
d = 6\sqrt{t} = 6\sqrt{12} = 6 \times 3.464 = 20.78 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt the next higher standard size: **d = 22 mm** (per IS 1148 rivet sizes: 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36 mm).

**Step 2 — Pitch:**

For chain riveting: $p = 2.5d = 2.5 \times 22 = 55$ mm

For zig-zag riveting: $p = 1.5d = 1.5 \times 22 = 33$ mm

**Step 3 — Edge distance and margin:**

Edge distance: $e = 1.5d = 1.5 \times 22 = 33$ mm

Margin: $m = e + d/2 = 33 + 11 = 44$ mm

**Step 4 — Sketch description:**

Draw a lap joint (two 12 mm thick plates overlapping by a length L = 2p + 2e + clearance). One rivet near each end of the lap. Show the hole diameter slightly larger than the rivet (clearance for hot riveting expansion), show the head as a filled circular shape (snap or pan head — for hot riveting, use **snap head** = a hemisphere).

**[Marking distribution: 2 marks for rivet diameter + 2 marks for pitch (chain & zig-zag) + 1.5 marks for edge/margin + 1.5 marks for sketch and clarity = 7 marks]**

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Compulsory Alternative]

**(a)** Explain the BIS conventions for representing **external and internal threads** in **(i) end view** and **(ii) longitudinal section view**. Sketch the M12 × 1.75 thread in both views, clearly showing major and minor diameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of diameter **40 mm** is keyed to a gear hub using a **parallel sunk key** as per IS 2292. Compute: **(7 marks)**
   (i) Key width and height
   (ii) Keyway depth in shaft and hub
   (iii) Recommended key length range
   (iv) State the shear and crushing stress formulae for the key (do not derive; just write).

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**End view (looking along the axis of the bolt):**

- A circle of diameter **d** (major diameter) drawn with a **continuous thick line** — this is the crest of the thread.
- A **three-quarter (3/4) thin circle** of diameter **d₁** (minor diameter, the root), with a **30° gap** at the top quadrant. The thin circle is the only representation of the minor diameter in end view.
- Centerlines drawn with long-short-long dashes through the center.

**Longitudinal (front) view:**

- The major diameter (crest) is shown as a **continuous thick line**.
- The minor diameter (root) is shown as a **continuous thin line**.
- The thread does NOT have to be drawn as a zigzag; the convention is two parallel lines (one thick, one thin) representing the crest and root.

**For internal threads (tapped hole):**

- The minor diameter (the actual hole drilled, the **root** of the internal thread) is drawn with a **continuous thick line**.
- The major diameter (the **crest** of the internal thread, the imaginary larger cylinder) is drawn with a **continuous thin line**.
- In end view, the thick circle has the 30° gap at the top, and the thin circle is a complete 360°.

**For M12 × 1.75 thread:**

- Major diameter $d = 12$ mm
- Pitch diameter $d_2 = 12 - 0.6495 \times 1.75 = 10.863$ mm
- Minor diameter $d_1 = 12 - 1.0825 \times 1.75 = 10.106$ mm

Sketch dimensions: thick line at 12 mm, thin line at 10.106 mm.

**[Marking distribution: 2 marks for end view of external thread + 1.5 marks for front view of external thread + 1.5 marks for internal thread conventions + 1 mark for sketch quality and dimensions + 1 mark for M12 calculations = 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Key width and height (per IS 2292):**

$$
w = \frac{d}{4} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
h = \frac{7d}{24} = \frac{7 \times 40}{24} = 11.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Keyway depth in shaft and hub:**

For a parallel sunk key (per IS 2292), $t_1 = t_2 = h/2$:

$$
t_1 = t_2 = \frac{11.67}{2} = 5.83 \text{ mm}
$$

However, standard IS 2292 tables typically round $h$ to standard values. For $d = 40$, the standard key is $w \times h = 10 \times 11$ mm with $t_1 = 5$ mm and $t_2 = 6$ mm (a common proportional pair).

**Step 3 — Recommended key length:**

$$
L = 1.5d \text{ to } 2d = 60 \text{ mm to } 80 \text{ mm}
$$

Choose a standard length from IS 2292: **63, 70, or 80 mm** depending on hub length.

**Step 4 — Shear and crushing stress formulae:**

The key is subjected to a tangential force $F$ at the shaft surface (radius $d/2$). This force is resisted by:
- **Shear** in the key across its width $w$ at the shaft-hub interface.
- **Crushing (bearing)** on the **half-height** of the key in the hub ($h/2$).

**Shear stress in key:**

$$
\tau = \frac{2F}{w \cdot L}
$$

where $F$ is the tangential force, $w$ is the key width, $L$ is the key length. The factor of 2 is because the key is in **double shear** when only one key is used (no, it's actually single shear in this case — the resisting area is $w \times L$).

> *Correction:* For a single sunk key, the shear area is $w \times L$, so:

$$
\tau = \frac{F}{w \cdot L}
$$

**Crushing (bearing) stress in key:**

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{2F}{h \cdot L}
$$

where the bearing area is the projected area $(h/2) \times L \times 2$ (the force pushes on half-height from each side — or simply, the projected area is $h \cdot L / 2$ ... the most common form is $\sigma_c = 2F / (h \cdot L)$).

**[Marking distribution: 2 marks for key dimensions + 1.5 marks for keyway depth + 1.5 marks for length + 2 marks for stress formulas = 7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module Experiment 2:**
>
> 1. **Hatching the bolt body in a longitudinal section (1–2 marks lost):** Bolts, nuts, rivets, keys, pins, and shafts in **longitudinal section are NEVER hatched**. Only transverse sections of these elements are hatched.
>
> 2. **Reversing thread representation (1–2 marks lost):** External thread = thick line OUTSIDE (major diameter), thin line INSIDE (minor diameter). Internal thread = reverse. Mixing these is a guaranteed deduction.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong line weight (0.5–1 mark lost):** All visible outlines must be in **thick continuous** line (~0.6 mm). Dimension lines, centerlines, hidden lines, and hatching must be in **thin** line (~0.25 mm). Mixing weights = unprofessional.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the A/F formula for hex bolt (1 mark lost):** The exam expects $A/F = 1.5d + 3$, not the nominal diameter $d$. Drawing a hex head with side equal to $d$ is incorrect.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to extend centerlines (0.5 mark lost):** Centerlines must extend **3 mm beyond the circle/feature** they identify. Truncated centerlines look amateurish.
>
> 6. **Missing the 30° gap in thread end view (1 mark lost):** The minor diameter circle in end view of an external thread has a 30° (or 90°) gap. Drawing a complete 360° circle is a common error.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the chamfer on the head and nut (0.5 mark lost):** Hex bolts and nuts have a **chamfer of 0.15d to 0.2d** on the top edges of the head. Drawing sharp corners = mark deduction.
>
> 8. **Incorrect welding symbol placement (1–2 marks lost):** Weld symbol **below** reference line = arrow side. **Above** = other side. Many students reverse this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
>
> ✅ **BIS Codes to Remember:**
> - IS 696 → General engineering drawing
> - SP 46:1988 → Engineering drawing practice
> - IS 813 → Welding symbols
> - IS 2709 / IS 800 → Riveting
> - IS 2292 / IS 2302 → Keys
> - IS 1363 / IS 1364 → Hex bolts and nuts
> - IS 4218 → ISO Metric threads
> - IS 10711 → Drawing sheet sizes
>
> ✅ **Memorize These Six Formulas:**
> - Rivet: $d = 6\sqrt{t}$
> - Pitch: $p = 2.5d$ (chain), $p = 1.5d$ (zig-zag)
> - Edge: $e = 1.5d$
> - Key: $w = d/4$, $h = 7d/24$
> - Thread: $d_2 = d - 0.6495P$, $d_1 = d - 1.0825P$
> - Hex: A/F = $1.5d + 3$, head $h = 0.7d$, nut $m = 0.8d$
>
> ✅ **Sectioning Golden Rules:**
> - Bolt, nut, rivet, key, pin, shaft in **longitudinal section → NOT HATCHED**
> - Different parts in assembly = hatching at different angles (45° and 135° or different spacing)
> - Same part, multiple sections = same hatching direction and spacing
> - Hatching stops ~1 mm short of the outline
> - Cutting plane line is **thick, long-dash-dot**
>
> ✅ **Dimensioning Golden Rules:**
> - Dimension between extension lines (1–2 mm gap from feature)
> - Arrows at the ends of dimension lines
> - Diameters prefixed with **⌀**, radii with **R**
> - Avoid dimensioning to hidden lines
> - Use **unidirectional** text orientation
>
> ✅ **Welding Symbol Placement:**
> - Below reference line = **arrow side**
> - Above reference line = **other side**
> - Tail carries supplementary info (process, electrode)
> - Fillet weld symbol: filled triangle
> - Butt weld symbol: V, square, bevel, U depending on edge prep
>
> ✅ **Thread Representation:**
> - External: thick line = major dia, thin line = minor dia
> - Internal: thin line = major dia, thick line = minor dia (REVERSED)
> - End view: thick circle has 30°/90° gap, thin circle is complete
> - In section: hatching through thin line, stops at thick line
>
> ✅ **Standard Proportions Table (Memorize for Lab & Exam):**
> - Hex bolt head: A/F = $1.5d + 3$, height = $0.7d$
> - Hex nut: A/F = $1.5d + 3$, thickness = $0.8d$
> - Square bolt head: side = $1.5d$, height = $0.7d$
> - Counterbore (socket head): ⌀ = $1.5d$, depth = head height
> - Clearance hole: ⌀ = $1.05d$ (normal fit) or $1.10d$ (loose fit)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conventional Representation of Welded Joints, Bolts and Nuts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Conventional Representation of Welded Joints, Bolts and Nuts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Conventional Representation** refers to the standardized simplified symbolic and pictorial methods prescribed by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** to depict **welded joints, threaded fasteners (bolts, nuts, screws, studs), rivets, and keys** in engineering drawings without showing their true complex geometry. It is governed by **IS 696 (Engineering Drawing Practice)**, **IS 813 (Scheme of Symbols for Welding)**, and **IS 800 (General Construction in Steel)**.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PCRUL307 – Machine Drawing & Solid Modelling Lab), this topic forms the foundation of **Module Experiment 2**, where students are expected to draw and identify the conventional symbols and simplified orthographic views of these machine elements.

### 1.2 Why Conventional Representation Exists — The Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are sending a *handwritten* letter to a friend in Japan. If both of you don't agree on the meaning of the *same* symbols (like ☀️ for "sunny" or ❤️ for "love"), the message will fail. **Conventional representation is the universal "language" of engineering drawings** — a pre-agreed set of symbols that every engineer, fabricator, and machinist across the world can read *identically*.

A full sectional view of a hexagonal bolt and nut is complex (showing chamfers, thread profiles, undercuts, etc.). However, on a manufacturing drawing, we don't need all that detail — we just need to convey: *type, size, position, and material*. Conventions do this in **seconds**, saving hours of drafting time.

### 1.3 Categories Covered in This Module

| S.No. | Element | Governing BIS Code | Use Case |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Welded Joints (Fillet, Butt, Spot) | **IS 813: 1986** | Fabrication drawings, structural steel |
| 2 | External Threads (Bolts, Screws) | **IS 1367 (Part 7)** | General machine assembly |
| 3 | Internal Threads (Tapped Holes) | **IS 1367 (Part 7)** | Castings, drilled & tapped components |
| 4 | Hexagonal Nuts & Bolts | **IS 1363, IS 1364** | Most common machine fasteners |
| 5 | Square Nuts & Bolts | **IS 2585, IS 1654** | Rough, low-cost assemblies |
| 6 | Slotted Screws (Cheese, CSK) | **IS 1365** | Clamping operations |
| 7 | Studs | **IS 1889** | Permanent + detachable joints (cylinder heads) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** In lab exams, students often confuse *thread conventions* (external vs internal) and *weld symbols*. Always remember: **External threads** show **two solid lines + two thin lines** (the minor diameter); **Internal threads** in section show **two thin lines crossing into solid lines**.

---

### 1.4 Physical & Geometric Standards to Remember

- **BIS Sheet Sizes:** A0 = 1189 × 841 mm, A1 = 841 × 594 mm, A2 = 594 × 420 mm
- **First Angle Projection** is the **KTU-mandated projection system** (per IS 1502)
- **Line types used:** Continuous thick (object), continuous thin (hidden/hatching), short chain thin (centre), thin chain thick at ends (cutting plane).
- **Standard metric coarse thread pitch** for **M10** bolt = **1.5 mm**; for **M12** = **1.75 mm**; for **M20** = **2.5 mm** (per IS 1891).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Philosophy Behind Conventions

Conventional representation is rooted in the **Principle of Simplification with Adequacy**. A drawing must contain *just enough* information for:

1. **Manufacture** (what to make)
2. **Inspection** (what is acceptable)
3. **Assembly** (how it fits)
4. **Communication** (universal understanding)

A hexagonal nut drawn in full sectional view would require **12 visible edges, 6 hidden edges, 3 thread pitch lines, and a chamfer** — roughly **20+ lines** for a single component. A conventional representation reduces this to **2–4 lines** while preserving 100% of the engineering meaning.

### 2.2 Welded Joint Representation (The Core of This Module)

#### 2.2.1 Anatomy of a Welding Symbol (Per IS 813)

> [!NOTE]
> A welding symbol is **not a single symbol** — it is a *combination* of **8 elements** arranged in a specific order along a **reference line**.

The 8 elements in order are:

1. **Arrow line** → points to the joint
2. **Reference line** → horizontal line on which the weld symbol sits
3. **Weld symbol** (triangular for fillet, etc.) → drawn on the reference line
4. **Supplementary symbol** (contour, flush, etc.)
5. **Size of weld** (e.g., leg length in mm)
6. **Length of weld**
7. **Pitch (centre-to-centre distance)** for intermittent welds
8. **Number / process specification**

**Position rules:**
- Weld symbol **below** reference line = weld on **arrow side** (near side)
- Weld symbol **above** reference line = weld on **other side** (far side)
- Symbol **on the line** = weld on **arrow side** (most common)

#### 2.2.2 The Four Fundamental Weld Types

| Weld Type | BIS Symbol | Real-World Application | Geometry |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Fillet Weld** | Right-angled triangle ▽ | T-joints in frames, brackets | Triangular cross-section in the corner |
| **Butt Weld** | Rectangle with cross lines | Plate-to-plate, pipe joints | Full penetration across the joint |
| **Spot Weld** | Circle ● | Sheet metal (automobile panels) | Circular nugget formed by resistance |
| **Seam Weld** | Circle with two parallel lines | Hermetic sealing (fuel tanks) | Continuous linear weld |

#### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Welding Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **BIS/IS 800:2007** standard formulas and are directly applicable to lab viva questions and KTU 2024 university examinations.

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Effective throat thickness ($t_e$) | $t_e = 0.7 \times s$ | For equal-leg fillet weld of leg size $s$ | mm |
| Throat area (per unit length) | $A_t = 0.7 \times s \times 1$ | Used in strength calculations | mm² |
| Strength of fillet weld (shear) | $P = 0.7 \times s \times L \times \tau_{allow}$ | $L$ = length, $\tau_{allow}$ from IS 800 | N |
| Weld size vs plate thickness | $s \leq t_{min} - 1.5 \text{ mm}$ | Maximum permissible leg size | mm |
| Minimum weld size (IS 800) | $s_{min}$ depends on $t_{thick}$ | 3 mm for $t \leq 10$ mm; 5 mm for $10 < t \leq 20$ | mm |
| Butt weld strength | $P = t \times L \times \sigma_t$ | Full penetration = plate thickness | N |
| Intermittent weld length $l$ | $l_{min} = 4 \times s$ or $40$ mm, whichever greater | For lap joint | mm |
| Pitch of intermittent welds | $p = 16 \times t$ for compression, $24 \times t$ for tension | $t$ = thinner plate | mm |

### 2.3 Bolts, Nuts and Threaded Fasteners — Deep Theory

#### 2.3.1 The Two-View Logic

A bolt or nut is conventionally drawn using **two views** (typically front view + side view) with:
- **One full view** showing the actual head shape (hexagon/square)
- **One view** showing only the conventional thread (two solid + two thin lines)

The hexagonal head is shown in true shape in **one view** and in a **simplified circular shape** in the adjacent view (using 4 thin lines crossing at 60°/120° to indicate the hex flats).

#### 2.3.2 Thread Conventions (BIS IS 1367)

**External Thread (e.g., on a bolt shank):**
- **Major diameter (outer):** Two **continuous thick** lines spaced by $d$ (nominal)
- **Minor diameter (inner):** Two **continuous thin** lines spaced by $d - 1.2268 \times p$ where $p$ is pitch

**Internal Thread (e.g., tapped hole):**
- **In sectional view:** Two **continuous thin** lines for the major (tap drill) diameter, two **continuous thick** lines for the minor diameter
- **Hatching** of the parent material **stops** at the major (thin) line, not at the minor (thick) line

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Hatching crosses the thread in internal sections. **Correct rule:** Hatching is **terminated at the major diameter (thin line)** in internal threads. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction in KTU exams if forgotten.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Standard Proportions for Hexagonal Bolt/Nut

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it is the **single most frequently asked content** in KTU 2024 machine drawing lab viva.

| Element | Proportional Formula | Standard Reference |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Nominal bolt diameter | $d$ | Given |
| Hex head across corners | $A_c = 2 \times d$ (i.e., $1.93d \approx 2d$) | IS 1363 |
| Hex head across flats | $A_f = 1.5 \times d + \text{or} - 0.5$ (≈$1.732d$) | IS 1363 |
| Hex head height | $h = 0.7 \times d$ | IS 1363 |
| Nut thickness | $m = 0.8 \times d$ | IS 1363 |
| Chamfer on head/nut | $c = 0.15 \times d$ to $0.2 \times d$ | IS 1363 |
| Washer thickness | $t_w = 0.1 \times d$ (typically) | IS 2016 |
| Thread length on bolt | $l_t = 2d + 6$ (for $l \leq 100$); $2d + 12$ for longer | IS 1367 |
| Drilled hole for clearance | $d_h = d + 1.5$ mm (medium fit) | IS 800 |
| Tap drill diameter | $d_{tap} = d - p$ | IS 1891 |
| Minor diameter | $d_c = d - 1.0825p$ | ISO 68 |
| Tapping drill (approx.) | $d_{tap} \approx d - p$ | IS 1891 |

> **Note (Table):** All dimensions are in mm; $p$ is the standard pitch for the metric coarse series (IS 1891).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fabrication industry:** Welded joint symbols are non-negotiable in structural steel (bridges, buildings) and pressure vessel drawings.
- **Automotive industry:** Every car body has ~5000 spot welds; their symbol must be unambiguous.
- **Aerospace:** Threaded fasteners in aircraft engines are double-safety-critical — conventions eliminate ambiguity.
- **Maintenance:** A maintenance engineer can order the exact replacement bolt (M10 × 1.5 × 50, grade 8.8) from a single conventional representation.
- **CNC/3D printing:** A correct conventional drawing is the first input to CAD → CAM → CNC workflow.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throat Thickness of a Fillet Weld

A fillet weld has a triangular cross-section with equal legs of size $s$. The **effective throat** is the **shortest distance from the root of the weld to its hypotenuse face** — this is the dimension that carries the load.

Let us derive it:

> **Step 1 — Geometry of the isoceles right triangle:**
> The two legs are perpendicular, each of length $s$. The hypotenuse forms the outer face of the weld.
>
> **Step 2 — Identify the perpendicular distance from the root (right-angle corner) to the hypotenuse:**
> This perpendicular is the **effective throat** $t_e$.
>
> **Step 3 — Apply Pythagoras theorem to the right triangle formed by $t_e$, half of hypotenuse, and one leg:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Hypotenuse, } H &= \sqrt{s^2 + s^2} = s\sqrt{2} \\
> \text{Area of triangle} &= \frac{1}{2} \times s \times s = \frac{s^2}{2} \\
> \text{Also, Area} &= \frac{1}{2} \times H \times t_e = \frac{1}{2} \times s\sqrt{2} \times t_e \\
> \frac{s^2}{2} &= \frac{s\sqrt{2}}{2} \times t_e \\
> t_e &= \frac{s^2}{2} \times \frac{2}{s\sqrt{2}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{2}} \\
> t_e &= \frac{s}{1.414} \approx 0.707 \, s \\
> \boxed{t_e \approx 0.7 \, s}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** The effective throat thickness is approximately **70%** of the leg size. This 0.7 factor is **the single most important constant** in fillet weld design per IS 800:2007.

### 3.2 Derivation: Hex Head "Across Corners" (AC) = 2d

> **Step 1 — A regular hexagon has 6 equilateral triangles** inscribed within it, each with side = across flats ($A_f$).
>
> **Step 2 — Across corners** ($A_c$) of a regular hexagon = **2 × apothem** for a regular hexagon? No — actually $A_c = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \times A_f$ for a regular hexagon.
>
> **Step 3 — For a standard hex head per IS 1363**, the designed relationship is:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A_f &= 1.5 \, d + \text{or} - 0.5 \text{ mm} \\
> A_c &= 1.93 \, d \approx 2 \, d \\
> \text{Cross-check:} \quad \frac{A_c}{A_f} &= \frac{2.0}{1.732} = 1.155 \\
> \text{Theoretical ratio for regular hexagon:} \quad \frac{2/\sqrt{3}}{1} &= 1.1547
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> This **confirms** the 2.0d approximation is consistent with a regular hexagon, and IS 1363 has rounded the constant for practical drafting.

### 3.3 Derivation: Tap Drill Diameter for Internal Threads

> **Step 1 — The nominal diameter $d$ is the major diameter** of the external thread (the bolt).
>
> **Step 2 — When tapping a hole, the tap removes material** to form threads up to a minor diameter $d_c$.
>
> **Step 3 — Standard approximation for tap drill** (per IS 1891 and ISO 2306):
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> d_c &= d - 1.0825 \, p \\
> d_{tap} &= d - p \quad \text{(simplified, for steel)} \\
> \text{For M10 (p = 1.5 mm):} \quad d_{tap} &= 10 - 1.5 = 8.5 \text{ mm} \\
> \text{For M12 (p = 1.75 mm):} \quad d_{tap} &= 12 - 1.75 = 10.25 \text{ mm}
> \end{aligned}$$
