> **Verification:** A standard tap drill size for M10 is **8.5 mm** in drill size tables — confirms the formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Welded vs Bolted Joints

| Criterion | Welded Joint | Bolted Joint |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Strength-to-weight ratio** | High (continuous joint) | Moderate (stress concentration at holes) |
| **Permanent vs detachable** | Permanent | Detachable (advantage for maintenance) |
| **Skill required** | Skilled welder needed | Semi-skilled labour sufficient |
| **Inspection difficulty** | Difficult (NDT needed) | Easy (visual + torque check) |
| **Cost for small batch** | High (setup) | Low (standard parts) |
| **Thermal distortion** | Significant problem | None |
| **Fatigue resistance** | Lower (notch effect) | Higher |
| **Typical KTU application** | Frames, brackets, tanks | Machine assembly, flanges |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A 100 mm × 10 mm plate is lap-jointed to another plate using a **single transverse fillet weld of leg size 8 mm on both sides**. Calculate the **shear strength** of the joint. Take allowable shear stress in weld as **108 MPa**.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Leg size, } s &= 8 \text{ mm} \\
> \text{Effective throat, } t_e &= 0.7 \times s = 0.7 \times 8 = 5.6 \text{ mm} \\
> \text{Number of welds} &= 2 \text{ (both sides)} \\
> \text{Length of each weld, } L &= 100 \text{ mm} \\
> \text{Total throat area, } A &= 2 \times t_e \times L = 2 \times 5.6 \times 100 = 1120 \text{ mm}^2 \\
> \text{Shear strength, } P &= A \times \tau_{allow} = 1120 \times 108 = 120960 \text{ N} \\
> \boxed{P \approx 120.96 \text{ kN}}
> \end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The 8-Element Welding Symbol Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **anatomical structure of a welding symbol** as defined by IS 813:1986.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Arrow Line] --> A1[Points to the joint line on the part]
    A1 --> A2[Reference Line]
    A2 --> A3[Horizontal baseline of the symbol]
    A3 --> A4[Weld Type Symbol]
    A4 --> A5["Fillet: Right Triangle"]
    A4 --> A6["Butt: Rectangle with cross"]
    A4 --> A7["Spot: Filled Circle"]
    A3 --> A8[Supplementary Symbol]
    A8 --> A9["Flush / Convex / Concave"]
    A3 --> A10[Weld Size in mm]
    A3 --> A11[Effective Length in mm]
    A3 --> A12[Pitch in mm for intermittent]
    A3 --> A13[Number of welds]
    A3 --> A14[Process: SMAW / TIG / MIG]
    P0[Position Rule] --> P1["Below line: Arrow side"]
    P0 --> P2["Above line: Other side"]
    P0 --> P3["On the line: Arrow side default"]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Threaded Fasteners

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Threaded Fasteners per IS 1367]
    F0 --> F1[Bolts]
    F0 --> F2[Nuts]
    F0 --> F3[Screws]
    F0 --> F4[Studs]
    F1 --> F1a[Hex Head Bolt]
    F1 --> F1b[Square Head Bolt]
    F1 --> F1c[Slotted Head Bolt]
    F2 --> F2a[Hex Nut Standard]
    F2 --> F2b[Square Nut]
    F2 --> F2c[Lock Nut]
    F2 --> F2d[Cap Nut Dome]
    F3 --> F3a[Cheese Head Slotted]
    F3 --> F3b[Countersunk CSK Slotted]
    F3 --> F3c[Pan Head Slotted]
    F4 --> F4a[Stud with two chamfered ends]
    F4 --> F4b[Stud with one end for tapping]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: How Conventional Representation Flows from Sketch to Manufacture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Conceptual Sketch] --> S2[Orthographic Projection Drawing]
    S2 --> S3["Apply BIS Conventions: IS 696"]
    S3 --> S4["Welds: IS 813 Symbols"]
    S3 --> S5["Threads: IS 1367 Convention"]
    S3 --> S6["Bolt Heads: IS 1363 Dimensions"]
    S4 --> S7[Dimensioned Production Drawing]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[CAD Model Solid Modelling]
    S8 --> S9[Engineering Approval]
    S9 --> S10[Workshop Fabrication]
    S10 --> S11[Final Manufactured Component]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Architecture: Weld vs Bolt Joint Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WeldJoint [Welded Joint Logic]
        W1[Two Plates in Contact] --> W2[Heat Source Applied]
        W2 --> W3[Filler Material Melts]
        W3 --> W4[Metallurgical Fusion]
        W4 --> W5[Continuous Permanent Joint]
    end
    subgraph BoltJoint [Bolted Joint Logic]
        B1[Drilled Hole in Plates] --> B2[Bolt Inserted]
        B2 --> B3[Nut Threaded on]
        B3 --> B4[Pre-load Torque Applied]
        B4 --> B5[Clamping Force via Friction]
        B5 --> B6[Detachable Joint Formed]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** The above Mermaid blocks represent the **functional architecture** and classification logic of welded/bolted joints. The actual *physical* drawings of these joints (with specific dimensions) are best rendered in a CAD environment such as AutoCAD, SolidWorks, or CATIA, as specified by your KTU 2024 lab manual.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define conventional representation of threads as per **IS 1367**. With the help of a neat sketch, show the **two-line convention** for an **external metric thread** of diameter 20 mm with pitch 2.5 mm.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Conventional representation of threads is the simplified symbolic method of depicting threads on engineering drawings without drawing the actual helical profile. It is governed by IS 1367 and uses standardized line types.
>
> **Conventions for External Thread (1.5 marks):**
> - Two **continuous thick lines** at the major diameter (nominal) = 20 mm apart
> - Two **continuous thin lines** at the minor diameter = $d - 1.2268 \times p = 20 - 1.2268 \times 2.5 = 16.93$ mm apart
> - Thin lines drawn **across the end view** showing ¾ circle on the visible side
> - In end view, the major diameter circle is **complete** (thick); the minor diameter circle is **3/4 thin** (gap in the front quadrant to show visibility)
>
> **Real-world use (0.5 mark):** Saves drafting time while communicating thread type, size, and fit to a manufacturer unambiguously.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
What is the significance of a **welding symbol** per IS 813? List the **essential elements** of a complete welding symbol.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Significance (1 mark):** A welding symbol is a standardized graphical language that conveys the type, size, length, location, and process of a weld on an engineering drawing. It eliminates ambiguity in fabrication and is mandatory per IS 813:1986.
>
> **Essential Elements (2 marks):**
> 1. **Arrow line** — points to the joint
> 2. **Reference line** — horizontal baseline
> 3. **Weld symbol** (e.g., fillet triangle ▽, butt rectangle)
> 4. **Supplementary symbol** (flush, contour, etc.)
> 5. **Weld size** (numerical, in mm)
> 6. **Weld length** (in mm)
> 7. **Pitch** (centre-to-centre distance for intermittent welds)
> 8. **Process and number of welds** (e.g., "2 SMAW" for two arc welds)
>
> **Position rule (bonus):** Symbol below the line = arrow side; above the line = other side.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has internal choice (a OR b). Below, both options are provided for thorough practice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **conventional representation of a hexagonal head bolt and nut assembly** for an **M16 × 80 bolt** of grade 4.6 connecting two plates, each 12 mm thick. Show:
- Hex head with chamfer
- Nut with chamfer
- Threaded portion of length 32 mm
- Spring washer

Provide a sectional front view and an end view. All dimensions to be calculated per IS 1363.

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

> **Step 1 — Identify standard dimensions for M16 bolt (1 mark):**
> - Nominal diameter $d = 16$ mm
> - Pitch $p = 2$ mm (standard coarse pitch per IS 1891)
> - Hex head across corners $= 2d = 32$ mm
> - Hex head across flats $= 1.5d + 0.5 = 24.5$ mm
> - Hex head height $h = 0.7d = 11.2$ mm
> - Nut thickness $m = 0.8d = 12.8$ mm
> - Chamfer $c = 0.15d$ to $0.2d$ → $c = 2.5$ mm (approx.)
> - Major thread diameter (solid lines) = 16 mm
> - Minor thread diameter (thin lines) $= d - 1.2268p = 16 - 2.45 = 13.55$ mm
> - Length of bolt = 80 mm; threaded portion $l_t = 2d + 6 = 38$ mm
>
> **Step 2 — Sectional Front View (3 marks):**
> - Draw the bolt in full section with two plates
> - Upper plate 12 mm thick, lower plate 12 mm thick
> - Hex head sits on top of upper plate
> - Spring washer between plate and nut
> - Thread length should extend through both plates + washer + nut
> - Show chamfers at 30° on head and nut top
> - Show thread convention: two solid + two thin lines
>
> **Step 3 — End View (2 marks):**
> - Hex head shown as a hexagon with 24.5 mm across flats
> - Bolt shank end shown as a circle of 16 mm diameter (thick line)
> - Inside the circle, a 3/4 circle of 13.55 mm diameter (thin line) with a gap in the front
>
> **Step 4 — Dimensioning (1 mark):**
> - All dimensions per IS 1363, with "M16" designation
> - Tolerances on bolt length noted (±1 mm)
> - Grade "4.6" marked on the drawing

**Drawing Layout (ASCII representation for student reference):**

```
   ┌─── 24.5 ───┐
   │   ◯ Hex    │ ← Hex head, height 11.2
   ├────────────┤
   │  Plate 1   │ ← 12 mm
   ├────────────┤
   │            │
   │  Shank     │ ← Thread from here, length 38 mm
   │ ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │   (solid + thin lines)
   │ ░░░░░░░░░░ │ ← Internal thread in lower plate
   │  Plate 2   │ ← 12 mm
   ├────────────┤
   │  ~~~ ~~~   │ ← Spring washer
   ├────────────┤
   │   ◯ Nut    │ ← Hex nut, thickness 12.8
   └────────────┘
```

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **welding symbols** for:
(i) A **fillet weld** of leg size 10 mm on the arrow side
(ii) A **butt weld** with double-V preparation
(iii) A **continuous seam weld**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **(i) Fillet weld — arrow side, 10 mm (2.5 marks):**
> - Reference line (horizontal)
> - Right-angled triangle ▽ drawn **below** the reference line (arrow side)
> - Numerical "10" written to the left of the triangle (weld size)
> - Arrow line connects the reference line to the joint
>
> **(ii) Butt weld with double-V preparation (2.5 marks):**
> - Reference line
> - Rectangle with two converging lines inside (V-shape on both sides) drawn **on** the reference line
> - The "V" symbol is symmetric
> - Plate preparation angle (typically 60°) shown if needed
>
> **(iii) Continuous seam weld (2 marks):**
> - Reference line
> - Circle with two parallel horizontal lines passing through it (resembling a "lens" or "I-shape" inside a circle)
> - Symbol on reference line = arrow side
> - "C" or "CONT" written to indicate continuous (not intermittent)

**Why these symbols matter (1 mark bonus):** They translate into specific welding procedures, electrode types, and inspection methods in the fabrication shop.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 200 mm × 12 mm plate is to be butt-welded to another 200 mm × 10 mm plate using a **single-V butt weld with 60° included angle**. Calculate:
(i) The effective throat thickness
(ii) The cross-sectional area of the weld per unit length
(iii) The tensile strength of the joint if the allowable tensile stress in the weld metal is 150 MPa.

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 — Geometry of the single-V butt weld (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{For a single-V butt weld, the effective throat} &= t_{plate} \text{ (the thinner plate)} \\
> \text{Since the plates are 12 mm and 10 mm,} \quad t &= 10 \text{ mm} \\
> \text{Effective throat, } t_e &= t = 10 \text{ mm}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area per unit length (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> A &= t_e \times 1 = 10 \times 1 = 10 \text{ mm}^2/\text{mm of length}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Tensile strength (2 marks):**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> P &= A \times \sigma_{allow} = 10 \times 200 \times 150 \\
> P &= 10 \times 200 \times 150 = 300000 \text{ N} \\
> \boxed{P = 300 \text{ kN}}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Note on V-preparation (1 mark):**
> The 60° included angle ensures full penetration; if the included angle is less than 60°, incomplete penetration may occur. The reinforcement (extra weld metal above the plate) is typically neglected in design calculations per IS 800.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the conventional representation of the following, with neat sketches:
(i) **Square head bolt and nut**
(ii) **Countersunk slotted head screw (CSK screw)**
(iii) **Stud** (threaded on both ends)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

> **(i) Square head bolt and nut (2.5 marks):**
> - Square head: 4 sides visible in front view, square shape in end view
> - Head height $h = 0.7d$ (same as hex)
> - Across corners of square $= d\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414d$
> - Nut thickness $m = 0.8d$
> - Thread convention same as hex bolt
>
> **(ii) Countersunk slotted head screw (2.5 marks):**
> - Head tapers at 90° (or 60° per IS) from the surface of the parent material
> - In a section view, the conical head is shown with two thick lines forming a V above the surface
> - A slot is shown in the top view as a single thick line across the diameter
> - Threaded shank shown with standard thread convention
> - Head sinks flush with the surface of the joined part
>
> **(iii) Stud (2 marks):**
> - Cylindrical rod, threaded on both ends
> - One end (the "permanent" end) is screwed into a tapped hole of the lower component
> - Other end receives a nut
> - Middle portion is **unthreaded** (smooth shank)
> - In the drawing, both ends show thread convention; the middle shows as a plain shaft
> - Two chamfers at both ends (per IS 1889)

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU 2024 Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to stop hatching at the major (thin) line of internal threads** — guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction. *Fix:* Hatching ALWAYS stops at the thin line in internal thread sections.
> 2. **Drawing weld symbol on the wrong side of the reference line** — 2 marks lost. *Fix:* Default is BELOW the line for "arrow side" welds.
> 3. **Confusing the chamfer angle (30° on hex head, 60° on CSK screw)** — 1 mark lost. *Fix:* Memorize: Hex head chamfer = 30°; Countersunk = 90° included angle (45° from horizontal).
> 4. **Using 2nd angle projection instead of 1st angle** — KTU 2024 mandates 1st angle. Marks deducted if not followed.
> 5. **Missing the chamfer on bolt head and nut in the end view** — The chamfer appears as a small circle inside the hex (per IS 1363).
> 6. **Calculating weld strength using the leg size $s$ instead of throat $0.7s$** — gives a 30% inflated answer; loses 2–3 marks for conceptual error.
> 7. **Writing dimensions without units (mm)** — IS 9606 mandates mm for all decimal values, with no unit symbol; values > 1 m use "m".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conventional Representation (Welds, Bolts, Nuts):**
>
> - [ ] **BIS codes to remember:** IS 696, IS 813, IS 800, IS 1363, IS 1367, IS 1891
> - [ ] **1st angle projection** is mandatory in KTU 2024 scheme
> - [ ] **External thread:** 2 solid (major) + 2 thin (minor); thin line gap of $1.2268p$
> - [ ] **Internal thread:** In section, 2 thin (major, tap drill) + 2 solid (minor); hatching stops at thin line
> - [ ] **Hex head across corners = 2d**, across flats = **1.5d**, height = **0.7d**
> - [ ] **Nut thickness = 0.8d**, chamfer = **0.15d to 0.2d**
> - [ ] **Fillet weld throat = 0.7s**, where $s$ = leg size
> - [ ] **Butt weld throat = plate thickness** (full penetration assumed)
> - [ ] **Weld symbol position rule:** below line = arrow side; above line = other side
> - [ ] **Welding symbol has 8 elements** in a fixed order
> - [ ] **Spot weld symbol = filled circle ●**
> - [ ] **Seam weld symbol = circle with two parallel lines**
> - [ ] **CSK screw head angle = 90° included (45° from horizontal)**
> - [ ] **Hex head chamfer = 30°**
> - [ ] **Standard pitch values:** M10 → 1.5 mm; M12 → 1.75 mm; M16 → 2 mm; M20 → 2.5 mm
> - [ ] **Tap drill = $d - p$** (approximation for steel)
> - [ ] **Clearance hole = $d + 1.5$ mm** (medium fit per IS 800)
> - [ ] **Always dimension in mm**; no unit symbol needed (IS 9606)
> - [ ] **Hatching spacing** = 2 to 3 mm (uniform across the section)
> - [ ] **Centre lines** are chain (long-short-long) thin lines extending slightly beyond the object
> - [ ] **Hidden lines** are short-dash thin lines; **omitted for threads** in most cases
> - [ ] **Stainless steel, brass, bronze** follow the same convention as mild steel

> 📌 **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Lab Exam:** *If in doubt, follow IS 1363 for fasteners and IS 813 for welds. The examiner will always reward the answer that quotes the correct standard.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to 2D Assembly Drawings

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
A **2D Assembly Drawing** is an orthographic engineering drawing that depicts the spatial arrangement, mating relationships, and functional integration of multiple machine components when they are fully assembled. It serves as the primary communication medium between design, manufacturing, assembly, and inspection departments. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Code: **PCRUL307**), assembly drawings of standard machine elements such as the **Stuffing Box** and the **Screw Jack** are mandatory laboratory experiments to develop proficiency in interpreting and producing multi-view engineering documentation following **BIS SP 46:1988** and **IS 696:1972** drafting standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module Experiment 2**
> The 2D assembly drawing experiment evaluates the student's ability to:
> 1. Interpret the orthographic views of disassembled parts.
> 2. Apply standard conventions for sectional views, hatching, and hidden lines.
> 3. Generate a fully dimensioned assembly drawing with a Bill of Materials (BOM).
> 4. Sketch any **one** orthographic view (part drawing) of any **one** component.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you have just dismantled a household **water tap (faucet)**. You see the body, the spindle, the washer, the gland nut, and the packing rings scattered on a table. An **assembly drawing** is the "instruction manual in picture form" that tells the plumber *exactly* where each of these parts goes, in what order, and how they fit together to make the tap work. The **2D** part means it is drawn on a flat sheet of paper using standard orthographic projection — just like a map, but for a machine.
> 
> The **Stuffing Box** is essentially the sealing mechanism of a tap/valve/pump that *stops* fluid from leaking out along a moving shaft. The **Screw Jack** is the lifting mechanism under your car when you change a tyre — it converts rotational motion (turning the handle) into linear motion (lifting the car).

### Why These Two Assemblies Are Chosen (Pedagogical Rationale)

| Assembly | Engineering Concept Illustrated | KTU Skill Outcome (CO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Stuffing Box** | Sealing, packing, gland adjustment, sliding contact | CO2: Reading & Interpreting Assembly Drawings |
| **Screw Jack** | Power screw, thrust bearing, load lifting, self-locking | CO3: Drafting Assembly & Part Drawings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard to Remember (Bold for Emphasis):**
> - All dimensions in **millimetres (mm)**.
> - First-angle projection (**IS 696:1972**) is the BIS and KTU standard.
> - All assembly drawings must show a **front view (sectioned)**, **top view**, and **side view (half/half section where applicable)**.

---

## 1.2 The Stuffing Box — Definition and Function

### Technical Definition
A **Stuffing Box** (also called a **packing gland assembly** or **packing box**) is a cylindrical chamber fitted around a reciprocating or rotating shaft, designed to prevent the leakage of fluid (liquid or gas) along the shaft's path of travel. It accomplishes this through the use of **compressible packing rings** (traditionally asbestos, modern PTFE/graphite) that are axially compressed by a **gland follower** to form a tight seal against the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example You Already Know:**
> The stem of your bathroom tap — the part where water comes out. Inside the tap body, the packing (or in modern taps, an O-ring) wraps around the spindle shaft, and the small nut on top (the gland nut) tightens to compress the packing. That entire arrangement is a **stuffing box**.

### Main Components of a Stuffing Box (List Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The stuffing box typically consists of **8 to 10 main parts**. Memorize the following list — KTU questions often ask for component identification:
> 
> 1. **Body (Stuffing Box Body)** — The main housing, contains the bore for the shaft and the stuffing chamber.
> 2. **Gland (Gland Follower / Packing Gland)** — A ring-shaped component that presses down on the packing.
> 3. **Gland Studs (2 nos.)** — Threaded rods that pull the gland down onto the packing.
> 4. **Gland Nuts (2 nos.)** — Hex nuts that tighten the studs.
> 5. **Packing Rings (Lantern Ring + Soft Packing Rings)** — Compressible sealing material.
> 6. **Lantern Ring (or Bull Ring / Splash Ring)** — A perforated ring that allows lubricant to enter.
> 7. **Shaft (or Spindle)** — The rotating/reciprocating member.
> 8. **Stuffing Box Cover / Cap** — Sometimes a top cover is provided.
> 9. **Joint Gasket** — Seals between the body and mating flange.
> 10. **Hex Nuts on Studs (for body-to-machinery fastening)** — Secures the assembly to the pump/valve casing.

---

## 1.3 The Screw Jack — Definition and Function

### Technical Definition
A **Screw Jack** is a mechanical device that uses a **power screw** (helical screw thread) to convert rotational motion (applied through a lever or tommy bar) into linear motion, thereby lifting heavy loads with a mechanical advantage. The screw jack typically incorporates a **thrust bearing** at the head of the screw to reduce friction and a **swiveling cup (or cap)** at the top of the spindle to support the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example You Already Know:**
> The **bottle jack** or **scissor jack** that comes with your car. You insert a metal bar (tommy bar) into holes on the head, and as you rotate it, the screw turns and the saddle on top rises to lift the car. That is a screw jack in its simplest portable form.

### Main Components of a Screw Jack (List Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard **screw jack assembly** consists of **7 to 9 principal parts**. Know them well:
> 
> 1. **Base / Body (Cast Iron)** — A heavy hollow cylinder that houses the nut and supports the assembly.
> 2. **Nutt (Internal Threaded Bush)** — A bronze or gunmetal nut fixed inside the base; the screw rotates within it.
> 3. **Screw / Spindle (External Threaded Rod)** — The main lifting screw with ACME or square threads.
> 4. **Cup / Saddle (Swivel Cap)** — The top contact surface that holds the load; it pivots to self-align.
> 5. **Cup Pin (Saddle Pin)** — A small pin allowing the cup to swivel but not detach.
> 6. **Thrust Bearing (Ball Bearing or Taper Roller)** — Located at the head of the screw to reduce rotational friction.
> 7. **Lock Nut / Check Nut** — Prevents the screw from being pushed out under load.
> 8. **Tommy Bar (Lever)** — Inserted into the head of the screw to apply torque.
> 9. **Head of the Screw** — A forged head with holes for the tommy bar.

---

## 1.4 Standard Conventions Used in Both Assemblies (BIS SP 46:1988)

| Convention | Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hatching (Section Lining)** | Same part = same direction & spacing; different parts at 45° and at different spacings | Internal cross-section views |
| **Thread Representation** | Two parallel lines: outer = major diameter, inner = minor diameter | Screw, studs, nut threads |
| **Hidden Lines** | Dashed lines (short dashes) for features not in direct view | Internal bores, hidden holes |
| **Centerlines** | Long-dash-short-dash lines through axes of symmetry | All cylindrical features |
| **Dimensioning** | Aligned (unidirectional) system preferred; in **mm** | All numerical dimensions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Using the same hatching direction and spacing for two adjacent parts. According to **BIS SP 46**, when two adjacent parts are sectioned, the hatching must be drawn in **opposite directions** OR at **different angles/spacings** to visually distinguish them.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background of Stuffing Box Design

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The stuffing box operates on the principle of **progressive axial compression of packing material** to create a labyrinth seal. The detailed sequence is:

1. The **shaft** passes through the central bore of the body and is in relative motion (rotating or sliding) with the fluid.
2. **Soft packing rings** (typically 3 to 5 rings of square cross-section) are stacked in the annular space between the shaft and the stuffing box body.
3. The **lantern ring** (a perforated ring) sits in the middle of the packing stack and serves two purposes:
   - Allows **lubricant** (oil or grease) to be injected via a side port to cool and lubricate the shaft.
   - Acts as a "vent" for any leakage that bypasses the inner packing rings.
4. The **gland follower** sits above the topmost packing ring.
5. As the **gland nuts** are tightened on the **gland studs**, the gland follower descends axially, compressing the packing rings uniformly around the shaft circumference.
6. This compression creates a radial interference seal between the packing and the shaft, effectively **stopping fluid leakage outward** along the shaft.

### The "Why" Behind Each Component

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reasoning (Why this design works):**
> - *Why compression?* — A material under axial compression expands radially (Poisson's effect), creating the seal.
> - *Why a lantern ring?* — A single seal would overheat; the lantern ring distributes lubricant and breaks the pressure gradient.
> - *Why a gland follower?* — Direct axial force cannot be applied to soft packing without crushing it unevenly; the rigid gland distributes load evenly.

### Design Equations for Stuffing Box (KTU Reference)

The key dimensional relationships are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter | $d$ | Given | $20$–$100$ mm |
| Packing radial thickness | $t$ | $t = (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \cdot \sqrt{d}$ | in mm |
| Stuffing box bore diameter | $D$ | $D = d + 2t$ | in mm |
| Packing axial length (per ring) | $L_{ring}$ | $L_{ring} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \cdot d$ | in mm |
| Number of packing rings | $n$ | $n = 3 \text{ to } 5$ (typical) | — |
| Total stuffing chamber length | $L_{total}$ | $L_{total} = n \cdot L_{ring}$ | in mm |

### Real-World Utility

Stuffing boxes are universally used in:
- **Centrifugal and reciprocating pumps** (sealing the pump shaft).
- **Valve stems** (gate valves, globe valves).
- **Marine propeller shafts** (the largest stuffing boxes in the world).
- **Hydraulic and pneumatic cylinders**.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Background of Screw Jack Design

### Working Principle

The screw jack operates on the principle of a **simple machine — the screw and nut pair** (a type of inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder). Rotational input torque is converted into linear lifting force through the thread engagement.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Force Transmission

1. The **load** $W$ rests on the **swivel cup** at the top of the spindle.
2. The **spindle** is threaded externally (e.g., **Acme thread** or **square thread**) with pitch $p$ and lead $l$.
3. The **nut** is fixed inside the **base** and has matching internal threads.
4. The **thrust bearing** at the head of the screw allows the screw to rotate freely without the load rotating with it.
5. When the **tommy bar** of length $R$ is pushed with effort $P$, a torque $T = P \cdot R$ is applied to the screw.
6. The screw rotates one full turn → advances by **lead $l$** → raises the load by exactly $l$ mm.
7. The **lock nut** (if present) prevents axial ejection under shock load.

### The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reasoning:**
> - *Why a thrust bearing?* — Without it, the full load $W$ would have to be rotated with the screw, creating enormous frictional torque and wear.
> - *Why a swivel cup?* — A load is rarely perfectly flat. The swivel cup self-aligns to ensure the load is purely axial, preventing bending moment on the screw.
> - *Why Acme or square threads (not V-threads)?* — They have higher efficiency and are easier to manufacture. V-threads self-lock better but waste more energy as friction.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Screw Jack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE — It is the most-tested topic in KTU for Screw Jack problems.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load | $W$ | Given in N | — |
| Effort | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot \tan(\phi + \alpha)}{1 - \mu_{c} \cdot \tan(\phi + \alpha)}$ or simplified $P = \dfrac{W}{2\pi R} \cdot (p + \pi \mu d_{m})$ | Effort at tommy bar |
| Mean diameter of screw | $d_{m}$ | $d_{m} = \dfrac{d_{o} + d_{i}}{2}$ | where $d_o$ = outer, $d_i$ = core |
| Helix angle | $\alpha$ | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{l}{\pi \cdot d_{m}}$ | Lead $l$, single-start: $l = p$ |
| Friction angle | $\phi$ | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | where $\mu \approx 0.10$–$0.15$ for steel-on-bronze |
| Mechanical advantage (ideal) | $MA$ | $MA = \dfrac{2 \pi R}{l}$ | Ignoring friction |
| Velocity ratio | $VR$ | $VR = \dfrac{2 \pi R}{l}$ | — |
| Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\phi + \alpha)}$ | For square thread, no collar friction |
| Self-locking condition | — | $\alpha < \phi$ (or $\alpha < 4°$ to $5°$ in practice) | Screw will not back-drive |
| Maximum load (crushing of nut) | $W_{max}$ | $W_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (d_{o}^{2} - d_{i}^{2}) \cdot \sigma_{b}$ | Bearing/crushing stress on nut threads |

### Explanation of the Self-Locking Condition (Real-World Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why self-locking matters in engineering:**
> A car jack **must not** unwind when you remove your hand. The condition $\alpha < \phi$ (i.e., the helix angle is smaller than the friction angle) ensures that friction alone can hold the load. This is why lead screws in **jacks, presses, and clamps** are designed with small helix angles (typically $2°$ to $4°$) — *not* fast-pitch screws.

### Real-World Applications of Screw Jacks
- **Automotive jacks** (bottle jack, scissor jack, trolley jack).
- **Workshop presses** (hydraulic & mechanical).
- **Stage lifts** in theatres.
- **Adjustable table legs** and **hospital beds**.
- **Formwork supports** in civil construction.

---

## 2.3 Common Drafting Conventions — Assembly Drawing Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 Golden Rules of Assembly Drawing (BIS SP 46:1988):**
> 1. The assembly is shown in **functional position** (working orientation).
> 2. One view is **sectioned** (usually the front view) to show internal mating.
> 3. The **section plane passes through the axis of symmetry** of the assembly.
> 4. Adjacent parts in section have **opposite hatching directions** (or different spacings).
> 5. **Standard parts** (nuts, bolts, washers, bearings, keys) are shown in section without hatching if cut *longitudinally* (along the axis).
> 6. A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **parts list** is provided in the top-right corner of the drawing sheet.
> 7. Overall dimensions (length × width × height) are given on the assembly drawing; component-level dimensions are reserved for the part drawings.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Stuffing Box Assembly Procedure

The derivation of an assembly drawing is not a mathematical one but a **logical step-by-step drafting procedure**. The KTU exam asks students to "draw the assembly drawing of a Stuffing Box given the orthographic views of its parts." Below is the exhaustive step-by-step analytical method.

### Analytical Matrix — Assembly of Stuffing Box

**Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Statement):**
- Shaft diameter: $d = 40$ mm
- Stuffing box bore: $D = 64$ mm
- Packing ring thickness (axial): $L_{ring} = 20$ mm
- Number of packing rings: $n = 4$
- Gland stud diameter: $M16$
- Gland follower outer diameter: $D_{gland} = 70$ mm
- Body outer diameter: $D_{body} = 100$ mm
- Total body length: $L = 140$ mm

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Method (KTU Examiner's Expected Approach):**
> 
> **Step 1 — Setup the Layout:**
> Using **first-angle projection** (BIS standard), allocate the drawing area:
> - Front view (sectioned): $200$ mm × $140$ mm
> - Top view: $200$ mm × $100$ mm
> - Side view (half section): $100$ mm × $140$ mm
> 
> **Step 2 — Draw the Centerlines:**
> 
> $$\text{Horizontal CL for shaft axis} = \text{center of all views}$$
> 
> **Step 3 — Draw the Shaft (Component 1):**
> Front view: a horizontal rectangle of width = visible length (e.g., $200$ mm) and height = $d = 40$ mm.
> Top view: a rectangle of width $200$ mm and height equal to shaft length projected.
> 
> **Step 4 — Draw the Body (Component 2):**
> Center the body on the shaft axis. The body is a hollow cylinder:
> - Outer diameter: $D_{body} = 100$ mm (in front view: $100$ mm total height)
> - Inner bore: $D = 64$ mm
> - Length: $L = 140$ mm
> 
> **Step 5 — Section the Front View:**
> Apply **section hatching** to the body (45° lines, one direction, spacing = $3$ mm).
> 
> **Step 6 — Place the Packing Rings (Component 3):**
> In the annular space between the shaft and the body bore, draw 4 packing rings, each of axial length $L_{ring} = 20$ mm. Total packing axial length: 
> 
> $$L_{packing,total} = n \cdot L_{ring} = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}$$
> 
> **Step 7 — Place the Lantern Ring (Component 4):**
> The lantern ring goes in the *middle* of the packing stack (after ring 2):
> - Diameter: slightly less than $D = 64$ mm (e.g., $D_{lantern} = 62$ mm).
> - Has 4 to 6 small radial holes for lubricant passage.
> - Axial length: $10$ mm.
> 
> **Step 8 — Place the Gland Follower (Component 5):**
> - Outer diameter: $D_{gland} = 70$ mm.
> - Sits *above* the topmost packing ring.
> - Has 2 holes for gland studs to pass through.
> - Hatching at 45° in **opposite direction** to the body.
> 
> **Step 9 — Draw the Gland Studs and Nuts (Component 6 & 7):**
> - 2 studs, $M16$, located at $P_{CD} = 50$ mm (pitch circle diameter).
> - Pass through clearance holes in the gland and thread into the body.
> - Hex nuts on top of the gland.
> - *Longitudinal section:* studs and nuts are **not hatched** (BIS rule).
> 
> **Step 10 — Add the Joint Flange (Component 8):**
> A base flange with bolt holes for mounting to the pump casing.
> - 4 to 6 mounting holes.
> - PCD = $130$ mm.
> 
> **Step 11 — Dimension the Assembly Drawing:**
> Provide only **overall dimensions** and **functional dimensions**:
> - Overall length: $200$ mm
> - Overall height: $160$ mm
> - Shaft diameter: $\phi 40$ mm
> - PCD of gland studs: $\phi 50$ mm
> - PCD of mounting holes: $\phi 130$ mm
> 
> **Step 12 — Draw the Bill of Materials (BOM) Table:**
> Top right corner, with columns: Sl. No. | Part Name | Material | Quantity.

### Final Assembled Layout (Exhaustive Schematic)

```
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                          STUFFING BOX                             |
|                                                                   |
|    FRONT VIEW (Sectioned at Center Plane)                         |
|                                                                   |
|         Gland Nut        Gland                                   |
|         ┌───┐             ┌──────┐                                |
|         │ * │═════════════│      │  ← Gland Stud (M16)            |
|         │   │     [Gland] │      │                                |
|         │   │═════════════│      │                                |
|         └───┘  Packing    │      │  ←  Gland Follower            |
|                Ring 1     ├──────┤                                |
|         ███████           │      │  ←  Lantern Ring              |
|         █      █  Ring 2  │      │                                |
|         █ BODY █          │ BODY │                                |
|         █      █  Ring 3  │      │                                |
|         ███████           │      │                                |
|                Ring 4     │      │                                |
|         ███████           │      │                                |
|         █      █  ←Shaft→ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│                               |
|         █ BODY █   (d=40) │      │                                |
|         █      █          │      │                                |
|         ███████           │      │                                |
|         └──┬──┘═══════════└──────┘                                |
|     Mounting Flange (4 holes)                                    |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hatching Note:** Body is hatched at 45° (one direction), Gland is hatched at 45° in the **opposite** direction (i.e., 135°). Shaft is **not hatched** in longitudinal section.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Screw Jack Assembly Procedure

### Given Data (Standard KTU Problem Statement)

- Screw outer diameter: $d_{o} = 50$ mm
- Screw core diameter: $d_{i} = 38$ mm
- Pitch: $p = 8$ mm (single-start, Acme thread)
- Lead: $l = p = 8$ mm
- Mean diameter: $d_{m} = \dfrac{d_{o} + d_{i}}{2} = \dfrac{50 + 38}{2} = 44$ mm
- Base outer diameter: $D_{base} = 110$ mm
- Base height: $H_{base} = 100$ mm
- Total screw length: $L_{screw} = 250$ mm
- Tommy bar length: $R = 350$ mm
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.12$
- Load: $W = 20$ kN

### Step-by-Step Assembly Procedure

**Step 1 — Calculate the Helix Angle $\alpha$:**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{l}{\pi \cdot d_{m}} = \frac{8}{\pi \times 44} = \frac{8}{138.23} = 0.0579$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.0579) = 3.314° \approx 3.3°$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Friction Angle $\phi$:**

$$\tan \phi = \mu = 0.12$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.843° \approx 6.84°$$

**Step 3 — Verify Self-Locking Condition:**

Since $\alpha = 3.3°$ and $\phi = 6.84°$, the condition $\alpha < \phi$ is satisfied.

$$\alpha < \phi \implies 3.3° < 6.84° \quad \checkmark \text{ (Self-locking achieved)}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Effort $P$ at the Tommy Bar:**

The torque required to lift the load is:

$$T = \frac{W \cdot d_{m}}{2} \cdot \tan(\phi + \alpha)$$

$$T = \frac{20{,}000 \times 0.044}{2} \times \tan(6.84° + 3.31°)$$

$$T = 440 \times \tan(10.15°)$$

$$T = 440 \times 0.1790 = 78.77 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Effort at the Tommy Bar:**

$$P = \frac{T}{R} = \frac{78.77}{0.350} = 225.06 \text{ N} \approx 225 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the Efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\phi + \alpha)} = \frac{\tan(3.31°)}{\tan(10.15°)} = \frac{0.0579}{0.1790} = 0.3235$$

$$\eta \approx 32.35\%$$

**Step 7 — Calculate the Mechanical Advantage (Ideal, no friction):**

$$MA_{ideal} = \frac{2 \pi R}{l} = \frac{2 \pi \times 350}{8} = \frac{2199.1}{8} = 274.9$$

**Step 8 — Calculate the Actual Mechanical Advantage:**

$$MA_{actual} = \frac{W}{P} = \frac{20{,}000}{225.06} = 88.9$$

**Step 9 — Drafting the Assembly:**

The screw jack is shown in **functional position** with the screw vertical. The front view is sectioned through the axis of symmetry.

| Component | Drawing Feature | Section Hatching |
|---|---|---|
| Base | Hollow cylindrical body with internal nut | 45° (one direction) |
| Nut (internal) | Inside the base bore | 45° (opposite direction) |
| Screw | External threads shown as two parallel lines | Not hatched (long. section) |
| Cup/Saddle | Topmost cap with a domed contact surface | 45° (opposite to base) |
| Tommy bar | A horizontal rod in a hole in the screw head | Not hatched |
| Thrust bearing | Standard part shown simplified | Not hatched |

**Step 10 — Final Dimensioning:**

- Total height (lowest position): $350$ mm
- Total height (lifted position): $350 + l = 358$ mm (showing one rotation)
- Base diameter: $\phi 110$ mm
- Screw outer diameter: $\phi 50$ mm
- Tommy bar length: $350$ mm
- PCD of mounting holes: $\phi 130$ mm

## 4.3 Assembly Sequence Diagram — Construction Order

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as Step 1
    participant S2 as Step 2

    S3->>S4: Place the moving component (Shaft for stuffing box / Screw for jack)
    S4->>S5: Add sealing or load-bearing elements (Packing/Gland OR Cup/Bearing)
    S5->>S1: Add fastening elements (Studs, Nuts) and dimension the assembly
    Note over S1,S5: Apply BIS SP 46 hatching rules at each step
    Note over S1,S5: Generate part drawing of ONE component
```

## 4.4 Component Hierarchy Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Machine Element Assembly] --> B[Stuffing Box]
    A --> C[Screw Jack]
    
    B --> B1[Body]
    B --> B2[Gland Follower]
    B --> B3[Gland Studs 2 nos]
    B --> B4[Gland Nuts 2 nos]
    B --> B5[Packing Rings 3-5 nos]
    B --> B6[Lantern Ring]
    B --> B7[Shaft]
    B --> B8[Mounting Flange]
    
    C --> C1[Base with Internal Nut]
    C --> C2[Screw with External Thread]
    C --> C3[Swivel Cup and Pin]
    C --> C4[Thrust Bearing]
    C --> C5[Lock Nut]
    C --> C6[Head with Tommy Bar Hole]
    C --> C7[Tommy Bar]
    
    style B fill:#bbf
    style C fill:#bfb
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Model Question Paper — Module Experiment 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Assessment Pattern for PCRUL307:**
> - **Continuous Evaluation (CE) — 50 Marks:** Lab performance + Record + Viva.
> - **End Semester Exam (ESE) — 50 Marks:** Drawing examination, **4 hours**, **one full imperial-size sheet (A1)** to be drawn by hand.
> - **ESE Question Pattern:** Usually **two questions** (one from each experiment of the cycle), student answers **one** (internal choice).

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *List any **six** main components of a Stuffing Box and state the function of the lantern ring.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The six main components of a stuffing box are:
> 1. **Body** — Houses the packing and supports the gland.
> 2. **Gland follower** — Compresses the packing axially.
> 3. **Gland studs and nuts** — Apply axial force on the gland.
> 4. **Packing rings** — Provide the radial seal against the shaft.
> 5. **Lantern ring** — Distributes lubricant and breaks pressure gradient.
> 6. **Shaft** — The rotating/reciprocating member being sealed.
> 
> **Function of the lantern ring:** It is a perforated ring placed in the middle of the packing stack. It allows lubricant to be injected from a side port to cool and lubricate the shaft, and it also vents any small amount of leakage that bypasses the inner packing rings.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *Define a Screw Jack. State the condition for its self-locking.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Screw Jack** is a mechanical device that uses a power screw and nut mechanism to lift heavy loads by converting rotational motion (applied through a tommy bar) into linear motion.

**Self-locking condition:** A screw jack is self-locking when the **helix angle $\alpha$ is less than the friction angle $\phi$**, i.e., $\alpha < \phi$.

Mathematically:

$$\alpha < \phi \iff \tan \alpha < \mu \iff \frac{l}{\pi d_m} < \mu$$

In practice, $\alpha$ is kept below **$4°$ to $5°$** to ensure positive self-locking.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module Experiment 2]**
> *Figure 1 shows the orthographic views of the dismantled parts of a **Stuffing Box**. Draw the **2D Assembly Drawing** showing the front view (sectioned), top view, and side view. Provide **six** overall dimensions and prepare a **Bill of Materials** for all parts. Also draw the **orthographic views** of the **stuffing box body** with dimensions.*
> *(14 Marks — sub-parts as below)*

#### Sub-Part (a) — Assembly Drawing of Stuffing Box (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Set up first-angle projection layout.** Place front view (sectioned) in the top-left, top view below it, and side view to the right of the front view. Reserve top-right corner for the BOM table.

2. **Draw the centerlines** — one horizontal CL through the shaft axis in all three views, and one vertical CL in the front view and side view.

3. **Front view (sectioned):**
   - Draw the **body** as a rectangular section, outer dia $\phi 100$ mm, total length $140$ mm, with flange on the left.
   - Bore diameter $\phi 64$ mm, shaft $\phi 40$ mm.
   - Hatching the body at 45° to the right.
   - Place 4 packing rings of $20$ mm each in the bore.
   - Place lantern ring in the middle of the packing stack.
   - Place gland follower on top with two stud clearance holes.
   - Draw the two M16 gland studs vertically, each with a hex nut.
   - Hatch gland at 45° to the left (opposite direction to body).

4. **Top view:**
   - Show the body as a rectangle with the shaft running through it.
   - Show two gland stud positions (small circles on PCD $50$ mm).
   - Show the mounting flange with 4 bolt holes on PCD $130$ mm.

5. **Side view (half section preferred):**
   - Show the body in section on the left half and external view on the right half.
   - Show one stud, one nut, and the shaft in section.

6. **Dimensioning (six overall dimensions):**
   - Overall length: $200$ mm
   - Overall height: $160$ mm
   - Body outer dia: $\phi 100$ mm
   - Shaft dia: $\phi 40$ mm
   - PCD of gland studs: $\phi 50$ mm
   - PCD of mounting holes: $\phi 130$ mm

7. **Bill of Materials Table:**

| Sl. No. | Part Name | Material | Qty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stuffing box body | Cast iron | 1 |
| 2 | Gland follower | Cast iron | 1 |
| 3 | Gland stud | MS | 2 |
| 4 | Gland nut | MS | 2 |
| 5 | Packing ring | Asbestos / PTFE | 4 |
| 6 | Lantern ring | Brass | 1 |
| 7 | Shaft | EN8 steel | 1 |
| 8 | Mounting flange | Cast iron | 1 |

#### Sub-Part (b) — Part Drawing of Stuffing Box Body (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

Draw **three orthographic views** of the body alone:

1. **Front view (sectioned):** Show the body as a stepped cylinder. Provide dimensions:
   - Total length: $140$ mm
   - Flange thickness: $15$ mm
   - Stuffing chamber length: $80$ mm
   - Bore diameter: $\phi 64$ mm
   - Outer diameter: $\phi 100$ mm
   - Flange diameter: $\phi 130$ mm
   - $4 \times \phi 15$ mounting holes on PCD $\phi 110$ mm
   - $2 \times M16$ tapped holes for gland studs on PCD $\phi 50$ mm
   - Surface finish (e.g., $\nabla\nabla$ on the bore, $\nabla$ on the outside).

2. **Top view:** Concentric circles showing the flange, body OD, bore, and mounting holes.

3. **Side view:** Show the half-section profile.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Stuffing Box:**
> - **2 marks lost** if hatching directions are the same for two adjacent parts in section.
> - **1 mark lost** if threads on studs are not represented by the standard two-line convention.
> - **1 mark lost** if the BOM table is missing Part Numbers and Material column.
> - **1 mark lost** if dimensions are placed on the body (in part drawing) instead of on the assembly drawing (overall only).
> - **2 marks lost** if the part drawing is of a wrong component.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module Experiment 2]**
> *Figure 2 shows the orthographic views of the dismantled parts of a **Screw Jack**. Draw the **2D Assembly Drawing** showing the front view (full section), top view, and side view. Provide all **functional dimensions** and a **Bill of Materials**. Also draw the **orthographic views** of the **base with the nut** with all manufacturing dimensions.*
> *(14 Marks — sub-parts as below)*

#### Sub-Part (a) — Assembly Drawing of Screw Jack (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

1. **Layout in first-angle projection.** Front view (full section) in top-left, top view below, side view on the right.

2. **Front view (sectioned through axis):**
   - Draw the **base** as a hollow cylinder: outer dia $\phi 110$ mm, inner bore with internal thread $\phi 50$ mm, height $100$ mm. Flange at the bottom with 4 mounting holes.
   - Inside the base, show the **bronze nut** (internal thread). Hatch the base at 45° right, nut at 45° left.
   - Draw the **screw** vertical, passing through the nut. Screw OD $\phi 50$ mm, core $\phi 38$ mm. Threads represented by two parallel lines.
   - At the top of the screw, draw the **thrust bearing** (standard part, not hatched).
   - Above the bearing, draw the **swivel cup** with a domed contact surface.
   - In the screw head, show the **tommy bar** as a horizontal rod, with the bar's ends visible.
   - The lock nut sits just above the bearing.

3. **Top view:**
   - Concentric circles: outer mounting flange, base OD, screw OD, cup OD.
   - Two diametrically opposite holes for the tommy bar.

4. **Side view:**
   - Half section: show the screw, bearing, and cup; external view of the base and flange.

5. **Functional dimensions (minimum six):**
   - Total height (in lowest position): $350$ mm
   - Lift per turn: $8$ mm (lead)
   - Base outer diameter: $\phi 110$ mm
   - Screw outer diameter: $\phi 50$ mm
   - Tommy bar length: $350$ mm
   - PCD of mounting holes: $\phi 130$ mm

6. **Bill of Materials:**

| Sl. No. | Part Name | Material | Qty |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base with internal nut | CI + Bronze bush | 1 |
| 2 | Screw (spindle) | EN8 steel | 1 |
| 3 | Thrust ball bearing | Std. part | 1 |
| 4 | Swivel cup | Cast steel | 1 |
| 5 | Cup pin | MS | 1 |
| 6 | Lock nut | MS | 1 |
| 7 | Tommy bar | MS | 1 |

#### Sub-Part (b) — Part Drawing of Base with Nut (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

Draw **three orthographic views** of the base + nut:

1. **Front view (sectioned):**
   - Outer dia: $\phi 110$ mm
   - Total height: $100$ mm
   - Flange thickness: $15$ mm, flange dia: $\phi 130$ mm
   - Internal thread (bronze bush): major dia $\phi 50$ mm, minor dia $\phi 38$ mm
   - Nut height: $60$ mm
   - 4 mounting holes: $4 \times \phi 15$ on PCD $\phi 110$ mm
   - $2 \times M8$ tapped holes for the lock nut retainer
   - Surface finish symbols on the threaded bore.

2. **Top view:** Concentric circles, mounting holes, and the bore.

3. **Side view:** Profile view, showing the flange thickness and overall height.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Screw Jack:**
> - **2 marks lost** if the Acme thread profile is shown as a V-thread (must be **trapezoidal/Acme**).
> - **1.5 marks lost** if the helix angle $\alpha$ is not calculated and the self-locking is not verified.
> - **1 mark lost** if the tommy bar is missing from the head of the screw.
> - **1.5 marks lost** if the swivel cup is drawn as a fixed (non-rotating) cap.
> - **1 mark lost** if the BOM does not specify the material for the nut (must be **gunmetal/bronze**, not cast iron).

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Must memorize before the exam.**

### Stuffing Box — Quick Recall
- ✅ **Primary function:** Prevent leakage of fluid along a moving shaft.
- ✅ **Number of main parts:** 8 to 10 (body, gland, studs, nuts, packing rings, lantern ring, shaft, flange).
- ✅ **Lantern ring position:** In the **middle** of the packing stack.
- ✅ **Lantern ring function:** Lubricant distribution + pressure gradient breaker.
- ✅ **Number of packing rings:** 3 to 5 (typical = 4).
- ✅ **Hatching direction:** Adjacent parts in section must be at **opposite 45° angles**.
- ✅ **Longitudinal section rule:** Studs, nuts, and shafts are **not hatched** when cut along the axis.
- ✅ **Packing material (modern):** PTFE, graphite, or braided carbon fibre (asbestos is obsolete).
- ✅ **Dimensioning:** Only overall dimensions on assembly; all manufacturing dimensions on part drawing.

### Screw Jack — Quick Recall
- ✅ **Primary function:** Convert rotation → linear motion to lift heavy loads.
- ✅ **Number of main parts:** 7 to 9 (base, nut, screw, cup, cup pin, bearing, lock nut, head, tommy bar).
- ✅ **Thread type preferred:** **Acme** (trapezoidal) or square thread — not V-thread.
- ✅ **Helix angle formula:** $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{l}{\pi d_m}$
- ✅ **Self-locking condition:** $\alpha < \phi$ (i.e., $\alpha < 4°$ to $5°$).
- ✅ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\phi + \alpha)}$
- ✅ **Tommy bar length:** $R$ — appears in $MA = 2\pi R / l$.
- ✅ **Swivel cup purpose:** Self-aligns the load; prevents bending moment on the screw.
- ✅ **Thrust bearing purpose:** Allows the screw to rotate under load without rotating the load itself.
- ✅ **Lock nut purpose:** Prevents the screw from being ejected under shock loading.

### Common Drafting Rules (BIS SP 46:1988)
- ✅ **Projection method:** **First-angle** projection (BIS and KTU standard).
- ✅ **Units:** **Millimetres (mm)**, no units shown on dimensions.
- ✅ **Hidden lines:** Dashed, short dashes.
- ✅ **Centerlines:** Long-dash, short-dash pattern.
- ✅ **Section hatching:** $45°$, uniform spacing ($3$ to $5$ mm), opposite directions for adjacent parts.
- ✅ **Thread convention:** Two parallel lines — outer line = major dia, inner line = minor dia.
- ✅ **BOM location:** Top-right corner of the drawing sheet.

### Examiner's Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU ESE)

| Drawing Element | Approx. Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct orthographic layout and projection | 2 |
| Accurate proportions and component placement | 3 |
| Section hatching and standard conventions | 2 |
| Dimensioning (6 overall dimensions) | 2 |
| BOM table (complete with 8 parts) | 1 |
| Part drawing of one component (3 views) | 4 |
| **Total** | **14** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Practice drawing both assemblies **at least 3 times** before the exam. Speed and accuracy are crucial because the ESE is a **4-hour** exam. Use a **sharp 2H pencil**, a **cleaner**, and an **A1 imperial sheet**. Start with centerlines, build the geometry in light strokes, then darken the final lines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to AutoCAD: Basic Commands, Keyboard Shortcuts, Coordinate and Unit Setting, Drawing, Editing, Measuring, Dimensioning, Plotting Commands, and Layering Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to AutoCAD: Basic Commands, Keyboard Shortcuts, Coordinate and Unit Setting, Drawing, Editing, Measuring, Dimensioning, Plotting Commands, and Layering Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**AutoCAD** is a computer-aided design (CAD) and drafting software application developed by **Autodesk** that enables engineers, architects, and designers to create precise 2D drawings and 3D models of physical components, assemblies, and systems. In the context of **KTU PCRUL307 – Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling Laboratory**, AutoCAD serves as the primary tool for preparing orthographic projections, isometric views, sectional views, and assembly drawings of machine components in accordance with **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) – SP 46:2003** drafting conventions.

For KTU 2024 Scheme, the Module Experiment 2 is designed to familiarize students with the **Graphical User Interface (GUI)**, **command-line interface**, **coordinate input systems**, **draw-modify-query workflow**, **layer organization**, and **plotting/layout management** of AutoCAD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AutoCAD commands are categorized into **Draw toolbar**, **Modify toolbar**, **Dimension toolbar**, **Layer Properties Manager**, and **Plot/Publish dialogs**. Mastery of keyboard shortcuts is mandatory for the lab continuous evaluation (CE) and end-semester practical examination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a draftsman working at a **traditional drawing board** (drafting table). On this board:
- The **sheet of paper** corresponds to the **Model Space / Layout** in AutoCAD.
- Your **T-square, set squares, and compass** correspond to **Draw commands** (Line, Circle, Arc, Polyline).
- Your **eraser and correction fluid** correspond to **Modify commands** (Erase, Move, Copy, Trim, Extend).
- The **pencil grading (H, 2H, HB)** corresponds to **Layers** with different colors, linetypes, and lineweights.
- The **scale ruler and divider** correspond to **Measure commands** (Distance, Area, Radius).
- The **title block stamp** corresponds to the **Layout / Paper Space** for plotting.
- The **act of speaking commands aloud to an assistant** corresponds to typing shortcuts in the **Command Line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition – Workspace:** The AutoCAD workspace consists of three primary areas: **Model Space** (where geometry is created at 1:1 scale), **Paper Space / Layout** (where scaled viewports and title blocks are arranged for printing), and the **Command Line** (text-based input interface).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Defaults
- **Drawing Units (Default):** Decimal (millimeters in KTU Mechanical/Civil context). Configurable via `UNITS` command.
- **Drawing Limits:** Default grid extends to a standard **A0 / A1 / A2 sheet** equivalent (e.g., 1189 × 841 mm for A1 landscape).
- **Angle Measurement:** Default = **0 degrees** at the positive X-axis, counter-clockwise positive (mathematical convention). Configurable via `UNITS` → Angle → Direction.
- **Precision:** Decimal, **0 (zero decimal places)** for architectural, **0.00 (two decimals)** for engineering mechanical drafting.
- **Linetypes:** Continuous, Dashed (`DASHED`), Center (`CENTER`), Hidden (`HIDDEN`), Phantom (`PHANTOM`) — each loaded via the `LINETYPE` command.
- **Lineweights:** Display in **millimeters** (e.g., 0.25 mm for thin lines, 0.50 mm for thick object lines, **0.70 mm** for visible outline as per BIS SP 46).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "what are the default drawing units in AutoCAD?", the expected answer is **Decimal Units** with a **Millimeter** base — explicitly state that for machine drawing, BIS SP 46 mandates metric units with mm as the base.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The AutoCAD Command Execution Model
AutoCAD follows a **command-driven paradigm** where every action begins with a command, followed by prompts for selection or input. The four universal principles are:

1. **Command Initiation** – Either by typing in the Command Line, clicking a toolbar icon, or using a keyboard shortcut.
2. **Object Selection** – After command initiation, AutoCAD prompts: *"Select Objects:"* Use pickbox click, window selection, crossing selection, or `PREVIOUS` / `ALL` keywords.
3. **Coordinate Input** – Geometry requires either point picks (mouse clicks) or precise coordinate entry (Absolute, Relative, or Polar).
4. **Command Termination** – Pressing **Enter** or **Spacebar** completes or repeats the command.

### 2.2 Coordinate Systems — The Foundational Concept
AutoCAD uses two principal coordinate entry methods:

- **Absolute Cartesian Coordinates** — Format: `X, Y` measured from the **World Coordinate System (WCS) origin (0,0)**. Example: `100,50` means 100 mm right, 50 mm up.
- **Relative Cartesian Coordinates** — Format: `@X, Y` measured from the **last entered point**. Example: `@50,30` means 50 mm right and 30 mm up from the previous point.
- **Relative Polar Coordinates** — Format: `@Distance<Angle`. Example: `@40<45` means 40 mm long at 45° from the last point.
- **Direct Distance Entry** — Move cursor in direction, type distance, press Enter (no `@` symbol needed).

> [!NOTE]
> **WCS vs UCS:** The **World Coordinate System (WCS)** is fixed with X horizontal, Y vertical, and Z out of the screen. The **User Coordinate System (UCS)** can be relocated and reoriented using the `UCS` command — essential for drawing inclined surfaces in machine components.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Command Cheat Sheet

| Category | Command | Keyboard Shortcut | Function | KTU Exam Weightage |
|----------|---------|-------------------|----------|---------------------|
| File | NEW | `CTRL + N` | Create new drawing | Low |
| File | OPEN | `CTRL + O` | Open existing DWG | Low |
| File | SAVE | `CTRL + S` | Save current drawing | Low |
| File | PLOT | `CTRL + P` | Print / plot drawing | **High** |
| Draw | LINE | `L` | Draw straight segment | **High** |
| Draw | CIRCLE | `C` | Draw circle (6 options) | **High** |
| Draw | ARC | `A` | Draw arc (11 options) | **High** |
| Draw | RECTANGLE | `REC` | Draw rectangle | Medium |
| Draw | POLYLINE | `PL` | Connected line/arc segments | **High** |
| Draw | POLYGON | `POL` | Regular polygon | Medium |
| Draw | ELLIPSE | `EL` | Draw ellipse | Medium |
| Draw | HATCH | `H` | Fill closed boundary with pattern | **High** |
| Draw | TEXT | `T` or `DT` | Add single/multi-line text | Medium |
| Modify | ERASE | `E` | Delete selected objects | **High** |
| Modify | COPY | `CO` or `CTRL + C` | Duplicate objects | **High** |
| Modify | MOVE | `M` | Relocate objects | **High** |
| Modify | ROTATE | `RO` | Rotate about base point | **High** |
| Modify | SCALE | `SC` | Enlarge/reduce uniformly | **High** |
| Modify | MIRROR | `MI` | Mirror about a line | **High** |
| Modify | OFFSET | `O` | Create parallel copy at distance | **High** |
| Modify | TRIM | `TR` | Cut objects at cutting edges | **High** |
| Modify | EXTEND | `EX` | Extend to boundary edges | **High** |
| Modify | FILLET | `F` | Round sharp corners | **High** |
| Modify | CHAMFER | `CHA` | Bevel corner | **High** |
| Modify | ARRAY | `AR` | Rectangular / polar / path array | **High** |
| Modify | BREAK | `BR` | Break object at point(s) | Medium |
| Modify | STRETCH | `S` | Stretch crossed portion | Medium |
| Modify | EXPLODE | `X` | Break compound object to lines | Medium |
| Query | DIST | `DI` | Measure distance/angle between points | **High** |
| Query | AREA | `AA` | Compute area & perimeter | **High** |
| Query | LIST | `LI` | Display object properties | Medium |
| Query | ID | `ID` | Show X,Y,Z of a picked point | Low |
| Dimension | DIMLINEAR | `DLI` | Linear dimension (horizontal/vertical) | **High** |
| Dimension | DIMALIGNED | `DAL` | Aligned dimension (parallel to object) | **High** |
| Dimension | DIMANGULAR | `DAN` | Angular dimension | **High** |
| Dimension | DIMRADIUS | `DRA` | Radius dimension | **High** |
| Dimension | DIMDIAMETER | `DDI` | Diameter dimension | **High** |
| Dimension | DIMCONTINUE | `DCO` | Continue dimension chain | Medium |
| Dimension | DIMBASELINE | `DBA` | Baseline dimension chain | Medium |
| Layer | LAYER | `LA` | Open Layer Properties Manager | **High** |
| Layer | LINETYPE | `LT` | Load and set linetypes | **High** |
| Format | UNITS | `UN` | Set drawing units & precision | **High** |
| Format | LIMITS | `LIMITS` | Set drawing area / grid limits | Medium |
| View | ZOOM | `Z` | Magnify/reduce view | **High** |
| View | PAN | `P` | Move view without changing scale | **High** |
| Misc | ORTHO | `F8` | Toggle orthogonal mode | **High** |
| Misc | OSNAP | `F3` | Toggle object snap | **High** |
| Misc | POLAR | `F10` | Toggle polar tracking | Medium |
| Misc | GRID | `F7` | Toggle grid display | Medium |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
In **production engineering** and **mechanical design**, AutoCAD commands underpin:
- **Tolerance stack-up analysis** using `DIMLINEAR` and `DIMALIGNED`.
- **CNC tool path preparation** via clean polyline boundaries.
- **Sheet metal development** using `OFFSET` for parallel bends.
- **BOM (Bill of Materials) generation** through `LIST` and `DATAEXTRACTION`.
- **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing)** using the Dimension Style Manager (`DIMSTYLE`).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Procedure: Setting Up a New Drawing for Machine Component

**Step 1 — Initialize Units**
Type `UNITS` in the command line. The Drawing Units dialog appears.

$$\text{Insertion Scale Units} = \text{Millimeters}$$

Set **Length Type = Decimal**, **Precision = 0.00**, **Angle Type = Decimal Degrees**, **Precision = 0.00**. Set **Clockwise** to **OFF** (counter-clockwise default).

**Step 2 — Set Drawing Limits**
Type `LIMITS`. The command prompts:
```
Specify lower left corner or [ON/OFF] <0.0000,0.0000>:
Specify upper right corner <420.0000,297.0000>: 594,420
```
This sets the limits for an **A2 sheet (594 × 420 mm)** in landscape.

**Step 3 — Load Required Linetypes**
Type `LT` → Linetype Manager → **Load** → select `CENTER`, `HIDDEN`, `DASHED`, `PHANTOM` from the acadiso.lin file.

**Step 4 — Configure Object Snap (OSNAP)**
Type `OSNAP` → enable **Endpoint, Midpoint, Center, Intersection, Quadrant, Perpendicular, Tangent, Node, Nearest** as running modes. This is critical for precise KTU machine drawings.

**Step 5 — Create Layers**

| Layer Name | Color | Linetype | Lineweight (mm) | Purpose |
|------------|-------|----------|------------------|---------|
| 0 (Default) | White | Continuous | 0.25 | Construction / Centerlines reference |
| OUTLINE | Red | Continuous | **0.50** | Visible object lines |
| HIDDEN | Yellow | HIDDEN | 0.25 | Hidden lines |
| CENTER | Cyan | CENTER | 0.25 | Centerlines / axes |
| DIM | Green | Continuous | 0.25 | Dimension lines and text |
| HATCH | Magenta | Continuous | 0.25 | Section hatching |
| TEXT | Blue | Continuous | 0.25 | Text / annotations |
| BORDER | White | Continuous | 0.70 | Sheet border & title block |

**Step 6 — Save the Template**
Use `SAVEAS` → `A2_Machine_Drawing_Template.dwt` to preserve all settings.

### 3.2 Procedure: Drawing a Circle Using the 6 Options

The `CIRCLE` command in AutoCAD provides **6 construction options**. Each is described below:

1. **Center, Radius** (default): Pick center → type radius.
2. **Center, Diameter** (keyword `D`): Pick center → type diameter.
3. **2 Points** (keyword `2P`): Pick two diametrically opposite points.
4. **3 Points** (keyword `3P`): Pick any three points on the circumference.
5. **Tan, Tan, Radius** (keyword `TTR`): Pick two tangent objects → enter radius.
6. **Tan, Tan, Tan** (keyword `TTT`): Pick three mutually tangent objects (creates inscribed circle).

**Worked Example — Tan, Tan, Radius Construction:**
```
Command: C ↵
CIRCLE Specify center point for circle or [3P/2P/Ttr (tan tan radius)]:
T ↵
Specify point on object for first tangent of circle:
[Click on first line]
Specify point on object for second tangent of circle:
[Click on second line]
Specify radius of circle <50.0000>: 25 ↵
```

### 3.3 Procedure: Linear Dimensioning Workflow

The KTU end-semester exam mandates the use of **Aligned and Linear dimensions** for orthographic projections.

**Step 1 — Set Dimension Style**
Type `DIMSTYLE` → New → name "KTU_MACHINE" → set:
- **Text height = 2.5 mm**
- **Arrow size = 1.5 mm** (closed filled arrow)
- **Extension line offset = 1.0 mm**
- **Primary units = Decimal, 0.00 precision**
- **Text style = Standard, font = txt.shx**

**Step 2 — Apply Linear Dimension**
```
Command: DLI ↵
DIMLINEAR Specify first extension line origin or <select object>:
[Pick first point]
Specify second extension line origin:
[Pick second point]
Specify dimension line location or [Mtext/Text/Angle/Horizontal/Vertical/Rotated]:
[Pick above the object]
Dimension text = 50.00
```

**Step 3 — Apply Aligned Dimension for Inclined Features**
Use `DAL` for dimensions parallel to the actual feature direction (e.g., on a 30° inclined surface of a machine bracket).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Object Snap Override Codes

| Object Snap | Code | Use Case in Machine Drawing |
|-------------|------|------------------------------|
| Endpoint | `END` | Endpoints of lines, arcs |
| Midpoint | `MID` | Center of a line segment |
| Center | `CEN` | Center of circles, arcs |
| Quadrant | `QUA` | Top/bottom/left/right of circle |
| Intersection | `INT` | Where lines/circles cross |
| Perpendicular | `PER` | Foot of perpendicular |
| Tangent | `TAN` | Tangent point on arc/circle |
| Node | `NOD` | Point objects |
| Insertion | `INS` | Block insertion point |
| Nearest | `NEA` | Nearest point on object |
| Apparent Intersection | `APP` | Visual intersection in 3D |
| Parallel | `PAR` | Parallel to source object |
| Extension | `EXT` | Implied extension path |
| Temporary Track Point | `TT` | Acquisition point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Procedural Logic — Editing Order:** For any KTU machine drawing, the recommended workflow is: **(1)** Draw rough geometry → **(2)** Apply modifications (TRIM, EXTEND, FILLET, CHAMFER) → **(3)** Apply OSNAP-precise corrections → **(4)** Dimension → **(5)** Hatch sectioned regions → **(6)** Add text annotations → **(7)** Layer freeze/thaw and final plotting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
