<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q1. List any six commonly used Draw commands in AutoCAD with their keyboard shortcuts. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Command | Shortcut | Function |
|---|---------|----------|----------|
| 1 | LINE | L | Draws a straight line segment between two points |
| 2 | CIRCLE | C | Draws a circle using center-radius, 2P, 3P, TTR, or TTT |
| 3 | ARC | A | Draws an arc using 11 different construction methods |
| 4 | RECTANGLE | REC | Draws a rectangle with optional chamfer/fillet |
| 5 | POLYLINE | PL | Draws connected line and arc segments as a single object |
| 6 | HATCH | H | Fills a closed boundary with a hatch pattern |

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q2. Explain the difference between Absolute, Relative Cartesian, and Relative Polar coordinate systems in AutoCAD with suitable examples. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Absolute Cartesian Coordinates** are measured from the **World Coordinate System (WCS) origin (0,0)**. Format is `X,Y`. Example: Typing `100,50` places the point exactly 100 mm along X and 50 mm along Y from origin.

- **Relative Cartesian Coordinates** are measured from the **last entered point**, prefixed with `@`. Example: After drawing a line to `100,50`, typing `@40,30` extends the line 40 mm right and 30 mm up from the previous endpoint.

- **Relative Polar Coordinates** specify distance and angle from the last point, prefixed with `@`. Format is `@Distance<Angle`. Example: `@50<45` draws a 50 mm segment at 45° from the positive X-axis of the previous point.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse the `<` (less-than) symbol with the `\` or `[` symbol. Always use `<` for polar angle notation. Also, students forget the `@` symbol for relative coordinates, which causes coordinates to snap to the absolute origin — this is a **favourite deduction point** for examiners.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a) Explain the Layer concept in AutoCAD. Describe any six properties that can be assigned to a layer, with their significance in machine drawing as per BIS SP 46. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Layer** in AutoCAD is a logical grouping mechanism that allows the draftsman to organize drawing entities (lines, circles, text, dimensions) into separate, controllable categories — analogous to **transparent overlay sheets** stacked on a physical drafting board, where each sheet contains only one type of information (e.g., one sheet for visible lines, another for hidden lines, another for centerlines).

**Six Key Layer Properties:**

1. **Layer Name** — A unique identifier (e.g., `OUTLINE`, `HIDDEN`, `CENTER`). Naming follows project conventions for clarity in multi-user environments.

2. **Color** — AutoCAD provides an indexed color palette (1–255) and True Color. BIS SP 46 conventions:
   - **White (7)** = visible object outlines
   - **Red (1)** = visible outlines in color plots
   - **Yellow (2)** = hidden lines
   - **Cyan (4)** = centerlines
   - **Green (3)** = dimensioning

3. **Linetype** — Defines the dash pattern. Common linetypes: `Continuous`, `CENTER` (long-short-long), `HIDDEN` (short dashes), `DASHED`, `PHANTOM`, `DOT`, `DASHDOT`.

4. **Lineweight** — Physical thickness of the line in mm or inches when plotted. BIS SP 46 specifies:
   - **0.25 mm** — thin lines (dimension, hatching, hidden, center)
   - **0.50 mm** — thick lines (visible object outline for medium components)
   - **0.70 mm** — thick lines for main visible outlines of large components

5. **On/Off State** — A turned-off layer is **invisible and ignored** in regeneration. Useful for temporary suppression.

6. **Freeze/Thaw** — A **frozen** layer is invisible AND excluded from regeneration, improving performance. A **thawed** layer is active. Critical for large assembly drawings with thousands of components.

7. **Lock/Unlock** — A **locked** layer is visible but **cannot be edited** — prevents accidental modification of reference geometry.

**Significance in Machine Drawing:**
Layer-based organization allows the drafter to **isolate the part drawing from dimensions**, **hide dimensions during 3D modelling visualization**, **print only specific layers for different deliverables** (e.g., a workshop drawing without dimensions, a GA drawing with section hatching, a parts list with text only).

---

**b) Explain the workflow to set up drawing units, limits, and linetypes in AutoCAD for a standard A2 size machine drawing. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Set Drawing Units**
Type `UNITS` in the command line. The **Drawing Units** dialog box opens. Configure:
- **Length Type = Decimal**
- **Length Precision = 0.00** (two decimal places)
- **Angle Type = Decimal Degrees**
- **Angle Precision = 0.00**
- Set **Clockwise direction = OFF** (counter-clockwise is the AutoCAD default and matches mathematical convention)

Click **OK**. The status bar at the bottom updates to reflect the new unit settings.

**Step 2 — Set Drawing Limits**
Type `LIMITS`. AutoCAD prompts:

```
Command: LIMITS ↵
Reset Model space limits:
Specify lower left corner or [ON/OFF] <0.0000,0.0000>: ↵
Specify upper right corner <420.0000,297.0000>: 594,420 ↵
```

For an **A2 sheet (594 × 420 mm)** in landscape orientation, upper right corner is `594,420`. Then enable limit checking:
```
Command: LIMITS ↵
Specify lower left corner or [ON/OFF] <0.0000,0.0000>: ON ↵
```

**Step 3 — Load Linetypes**
Type `LT` to open the **Linetype Manager**. Click **Load** to open the **Load or Reload Linetypes** dialog. From the `acadiso.lin` file (international metric linetypes), select:
- `CENTER` (long-short-long dash)
- `HIDDEN` (short dashes)
- `DASHED` (medium dashes)
- `PHANTOM` (long-short-short)
- `DASHDOT` and `DOT`

Click **OK** → **OK** to confirm.

**Step 4 — Set Current Linetype**
Type `-LINETYPE` (with hyphen for command-line version), or use the Linetype Control dropdown on the **Layers toolbar / ribbon**. Set current linetype to `CENTER` when drawing centerlines.

**Step 5 — Configure Status Bar Aids**
- Press **F7** to toggle **GRID** display on
- Press **F9** to toggle **SNAP** mode
- Press **F8** to toggle **ORTHO** mode
- Press **F3** to enable **OSNAP** with required snap points

**Step 6 — Save as Template**
Use `SAVEAS` → set **Files of type = AutoCAD Drawing Template (*.dwt)** → name the file `A2_Machine_Drawing_Template.dwt`. This template is reused for all subsequent KTU machine drawing submissions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**a) Explain any eight Modify (Edit) commands in AutoCAD with their functions, keyboard shortcuts, and typical applications in machine drawing. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Modify toolbar** provides commands that alter existing geometry. Eight essential commands are:

| # | Command | Shortcut | Function | Machine Drawing Application |
|---|---------|----------|----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | ERASE | E | Removes selected entities from the drawing | Cleaning up construction lines after dimensioning |
| 2 | COPY | CO | Duplicates selected objects at one or multiple locations | Drawing multiple identical bolt holes on a flange |
| 3 | MOVE | M | Relocates selected objects to a new base point | Positioning a sub-assembly in a GA drawing |
| 4 | ROTATE | RO | Rotates objects about a specified base point | Rotating a view to align an inclined feature |
| 5 | MIRROR | MI | Creates a mirror image across a specified mirror line | Symmetric halves of a shaft or housing |
| 6 | OFFSET | O | Creates a parallel copy at a specified distance | Concentric circles for hole diameters, slot offsets |
| 7 | TRIM | TR | Removes portions of objects that extend beyond a cutting edge | Cleaning up lines that extend past a boundary |
| 8 | EXTEND | EX | Lengthens objects to meet an existing boundary edge | Completing a shaft profile to a shoulder face |
| 9 | FILLET | F | Rounds sharp corners with a specified radius | Adding fillets at internal/external corners per design |
| 10 | CHAMFER | CHA | Bevels corners with two specified distances | Adding chamfers for assembly clearance |
| 11 | SCALE | SC | Enlarges/reduces objects uniformly about a base point | Not typically used in machine drawing (scaling is done in layout) |
| 12 | ARRAY | AR | Creates multiple copies in rectangular, polar, or path patterns | Bolt circle patterns, repeated features |
| 13 | STRETCH | S | Lengthens/shortens objects crossed by a selection window | Modifying a slot length after design change |
| 14 | EXPLODE | X | Breaks compound objects (blocks, polylines) into individual entities | Editing imported geometry from other CAD systems |

**Command Syntax Example — OFFSET:**
```
Command: O ↵
OFFSET
Specify offset distance or [Through/Erase/Layer] <Through>: 20 ↵
Select object to offset or [Exit/Undo] <Exit>:
[Click on the original circle]
Specify point on side to offset:
[Click outside the circle to create a concentric circle 20 mm larger]
```

**Command Syntax Example — MIRROR with Text Correction:**
```
Command: MI ↵
MIRROR
Select objects: 1 found
Select objects: ↵
Specify first point of mirror line: [Click point 1]
Specify second point of mirror line: [Click point 2]
Erase source objects? [Yes/No] <N>: N ↵
```
> When mirroring, set `MIRRTEXT = 0` to prevent text from being mirrored — critical for dimension readability.

---

**b) Explain the procedure to create Linear and Aligned dimensions in AutoCAD. Discuss the Dimension Style Manager and its significance. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**The Dimension Style Manager (`DIMSTYLE` command):**
A **Dimension Style** is a named collection of dimension settings (text height, arrow size, line spacing, color, units, etc.) that controls how dimensions appear. The **Dimension Style Manager** is invoked by typing `DIMSTYLE` or `D` in the command line. It allows:
- Creating new styles (e.g., `KTU_MACHINE`)
- Modifying existing styles
- Comparing styles
- Setting the current style

**Significance in KTU Examinations:** Every KTU machine drawing must conform to **BIS SP 46:2003** conventions. A properly configured dimension style ensures uniformity across all submissions and avoids manual adjustment of every dimension.

**Key Settings under Modify Dimension Style → Lines tab:**
- **Dimension line color = Black**
- **Dimension line spacing = 6.25 mm** (gap between dimension line and text)
- **Extend beyond ticks = 1.25 mm**

**Text tab:**
- **Text style = Standard** with **txt.shx** font
- **Text height = 2.5 mm** (BIS standard for A2 sheet)
- **Text color = Black**

**Primary Units tab:**
- **Unit format = Decimal**
- **Precision = 0.00**

**Procedure to Apply Linear Dimension:**

```
Command: DLI ↵
DIMLINEAR
Specify first extension line origin or <select object>:
[Pick first point on the geometry]
Specify second extension line origin:
[Pick second point on the geometry]
Specify dimension line location or
[Mtext/Text/Angle/Horizontal/Vertical/Rotated]:
[Pick a point above the geometry to place the dimension line]
```

**Procedure to Apply Aligned Dimension (for inclined features):**
```
Command: DAL ↵
DIMALIGNED
Specify first extension line origin or <select object>:
[Pick first point on the inclined surface]
Specify second extension line origin:
[Pick second point on the inclined surface]
Specify dimension line location or [Mtext/Text/Angle]:
[Pick a point along the inclined surface to place the dimension]
```

**Why Aligned over Linear for Inclined Features:**
A `DIMLINEAR` always produces a horizontal or vertical dimension. For a 30° inclined surface, this would misrepresent the actual feature length. `DIMALIGNED` rotates the dimension line to be **parallel to the measured feature**, providing the **true length** of the inclined line.

**Dimensioning Rules (BIS SP 46:2003):**
1. Dimensions are placed **outside the view** wherever possible.
2. Dimensions are read from the **bottom or right side** of the drawing.
3. Avoid dimensioning to hidden lines.
4. Centerlines must extend slightly beyond the circle (typically 2–3 mm beyond the circumference).
5. Dimension lines should be spaced 8–10 mm apart to avoid crowding.
6. Use **aligned dimensions for inclined features**; use **linear dimensions for horizontal and vertical** features.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for 14-mark questions:**
> 1. **Coordinate entry confusion** — Students often write `X = 100, Y = 50` and lose 1 mark; correct format is just `100,50`.
> 2. **Forgetting the `@` symbol** for relative coordinates — coordinate shifts to absolute origin.
> 3. **Wrong linetype spelling** — `CENTERLINE` (incorrect) vs `CENTER` (correct). BIS uses `CENTER`.
> 4. **Confusing `MIRROR` with `ROTATE`** — mirror needs a line, rotate needs a base point and angle.
> 5. **Explode misuse** — exploding dimensions destroys their associative nature; never explode dimensions before plotting.
> 6. **Plot scale errors** — for A2 sheet with mm units, the plot scale is `1:1` in mm; the layout viewport scale is what is adjusted (e.g., `1:2` viewport scale prints a 200 mm part as 100 mm on paper).
> 7. **Layer property mismatch** — assigning lineweight 0.5 mm to hidden lines violates BIS (hidden lines must be 0.25 mm).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **AutoCAD defaults to Decimal units and 0° at +X axis (mathematical convention)** — explicit setting via `UNITS` is mandatory before any KTU drawing.
- [x] **The `L` shortcut for LINE is the most-used command** — practice typing instead of clicking the toolbar (saves exam time).
- [x] **Absolute coordinates** use `X,Y` from origin; **Relative coordinates** use `@X,Y` from the last point; **Polar coordinates** use `@Distance<Angle`.
- [x] **6 circle options** = Center-Radius (default), Center-Diameter, 2P, 3P, TTR, TTT — TTR and TTT are most useful for machine component tangency.
- [x] **BIS SP 46 lineweights**: 0.25 mm (thin) for dimension/centerline/hidden, 0.50 mm (medium) for visible outlines, 0.70 mm (thick) for main outlines.
- [x] **BIS linetypes** = `CENTER`, `HIDDEN`, `DASHED`, `PHANTOM`, `DOT`, `DASHDOT` — load via `LT` from `acadiso.lin`.
- [x] **Object Snaps (OSNAP / F3)** — always enable Endpoint, Midpoint, Center, Intersection, Quadrant, Perpendicular, Tangent for machine drawings.
- [x] **F8 = ORTHO, F7 = GRID, F9 = SNAP, F3 = OSNAP, F10 = POLAR** — these are exam-critical toggles.
- [x] **`OFFSET` is preferred over `COPY`** for concentric features (holes, shaft steps) — preserves geometric relationship.
- [x] **`TRIM` and `EXTEND` use the same selection set paradigm** — pick cutting/boundary edges first, then objects to modify.
- [x] **`FILLET` radius = 0 produces a sharp corner** — useful when you want to chain fillet and sharp-corner creation in one command.
- [x] **`MIRRTEXT = 0`** must be set before mirroring to prevent text from being mirrored.
- [x] **`DIMLINEAR` for H/V features, `DIMALIGNED` for inclined features** — examiner will deduct marks if wrong dimension type is used.
- [x] **Layer ON/OFF affects visibility; FREEZE/THAW affects visibility AND regeneration; LOCK/UNLOCK affects editability but not visibility.**
- [x] **Layout space (Paper Space)** is where viewports, title block, and border are placed; **Model space** is where 1:1 geometry lives.
- [x] **Plot command workflow** = `Page Setup` → choose printer/PDF → select paper size (A2/A1) → set plot area = Layout → set scale = 1:1 mm → set style = monochrome.ctb → preview → plot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2D Assembly Drawings with Bill of Materials: Lathe Tailstock and Universal Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 2D Assembly Drawings with Bill of Materials: Lathe Tailstock and Universal Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Assembly Drawing** is a technical engineering drawing that depicts the relationship, spatial arrangement, and functional interaction between two or more individual machine components when they are brought together in their working position. In the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PCRUL307 – Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling Laboratory**, an assembly drawing is distinguished from a part drawing by showing the *integrated system* rather than isolated components.

A **Bill of Materials (BOM)** is the formalized, numbered tabular document — conventionally placed in the upper-right corner of the drawing sheet — that lists every individual part required to manufacture or assemble the parent machine, along with its quantity, material, and part number. The BOM is governed by **BIS SP:46 (2003)** and **ISO 128** drafting conventions adopted by Indian engineering practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Intent):** *An assembly drawing prepared in First-Angle Projection (used in India per BIS) showing the assembled position of components, with at least one sectional view, center lines, hidden lines removed in section, and a complete BOM — forms the core assessment output of Module Experiment 2.*

## 1.2 The Two Machine Sub-Assemblies in this Experiment

### (a) Lathe Tailstock
The **tailstock** (also called *dead stock* or *footstock*) is the movable support assembly mounted on the *rear* of the lathe bed ways. It carries the **dead center** which supports the free end of a long workpiece, thereby preventing deflection and chatter during turning. It consists of a **base**, **body**, **sleeve (quill)**, **spindle (dead center)**, **handwheel**, **screw**, **nut**, and **clamping bolt**.

### (b) Universal Joint (Hooke's Joint)
A **universal coupling** is a mechanical linkage that transmits rotational motion (and torque) between two non-collinear, intersecting shafts. The angle between the shafts can vary during operation. It consists of two **yokes (forks)**, a **spider (cross/cruciform)**, and **four needle bearings** seated in the yoke eyes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Human Analogy:**
> - **Assembly Drawing** is like a photograph of your bicycle with all its parts *fitted together* — pedals on the crank, chain on the sprocket, wheels on the frame. You see how they *interact*, not how each part is made in isolation.
> - **Lathe Tailstock** is the *second human hand* holding the far end of a chisel while the main hand cuts wood — it provides *reactive support*.
> - **Universal Joint** is the *knuckle of your finger* — it allows two bones (shafts) to rotate freely even when they are not perfectly straight.

## 1.3 Standard Drafting Conventions (BIS SP:46)

| Convention | Standard Rule | KTU 2024 Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Projection System | First-Angle (India) | Mandatory |
| Hatching Angle | 45° parallel, opposite for adjacent parts | Mandatory in section |
| Hidden Lines | Omitted in sectioned view | Mandatory in full section |
| Center Lines | Extended to assembly boundaries | Mandatory for all rotational parts |
| Scale | 1:1 or 1:2 preferred | Standard for lab record |
| Sheet Size | A2 (594×420 mm) recommended | Standard for assembly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics for this experiment:**
> - **Standard A2 Sheet** = **594 mm × 420 mm** (Drawing area = 520 mm × 380 mm with 30 mm margin).
> - **First-Angle Projection Symbol** is mandatory on every sheet.
> - **Number of components in Lathe Tailstock** ≈ **8 to 11 parts**.
> - **Number of components in Universal Joint** ≈ **6 to 8 parts** (excluding individual needle rollers).
> - **Total Mark Weightage for Module Exp 2** = **15 Marks** (Continuous Evaluation in lab).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Assembly Drawing

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Assembly Drawings
An engineering system (e.g., a tailstock) is a *machine* — meaning a combination of resistant bodies arranged to transmit force/energy. To:
1. **Manufacture** the individual parts (need part drawings).
2. **Assemble** the parts in the shop floor (need assembly drawing).
3. **Maintain/Sell spare parts** (need BOM and part numbers).

The assembly drawing answers the question: *"How do all the individual pieces fit together to perform the intended function?"*

### 2.1.2 The Hierarchical Logic
The drafting logic follows a strict 5-tier hierarchy:

- **Tier 1 — Identification of Parts:** Identify each part by a balloon/leader number.
- **Tier 2 — Functional Grouping:** Group related parts (e.g., sleeve group, screw group).
- **Tier 3 — Mating Surfaces:** Identify where surfaces *mate* (these dimensions must be coordinated).
- **Tier 4 — Motion Arrows:** Show direction of movement (sleeve slides, screw rotates, etc.).
- **Tier 5 — Sectional Revelation:** Choose a section plane that cuts through the *maximum* number of parts to reveal internal mating.

## 2.2 Bill of Materials (BOM) — KTU Reference Table

The BOM table is positioned in the **top-right corner** of the sheet. The format is strictly prescribed.

| Column Header | Width (mm) | Content | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PART NO.** | 25 | Sequential balloon number | 1, 2, 3 ... |
| **NAME** | 60 | Descriptive part name | Hex. Nut, Spindle, etc. |
| **QTY** | 15 | Quantity used per assembly | 1, 2, 4 |
| **MATERIAL** | 50 | Standard designation | EN8, CI, Brass |
| **REMARKS** | 40 | Standard/Bought-out info | Std. M12 Nut, Buy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard BOM Sequence (Bottom-Up Logic):** Parts are numbered in the order they would be disassembled — i.e., the *last* part to be fitted (often the smallest, like a key) gets the *lowest* number, and the *base/body* (first to be fitted) gets the *highest* number. This is the inverse of the assembly sequence.

## 2.3 Standard Materials Used in These Assemblies

| Part | Material Designation | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Lathe Tailstock Body | **Cast Iron (FG 200, IS 210)** | Vibration damping, rigidity |
| Spindle (Dead Center) | **EN 8 / EN 31 (case-hardened)** | Hardness at tip, toughness at shank |
| Screw (Tailstock) | **EN 8 / EN 24** | High tensile strength for axial load |
| Sleeve (Quill) | **EN 8 (ground)** | Smooth sliding fit in bore |
| Universal Joint Yoke | **EN 8 / Forged Steel** | High fatigue resistance |
| Spider (Cross) | **EN 31 (hardened & ground)** | Wear resistance at bearing seats |
| Needle Rollers | **Bearing Steel (52100 / SUJ2)** | Hardness for rolling contact |

## 2.4 Standard Fits and Tolerances

| Mating Pair | Fit Type | Application Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Sleeve in Body Bore | **H7/g6** (Sliding) | Sleeve must slide axially without play |
| Spindle in Sleeve | **H7/h6** (Locational) | Spindle must rotate *and* slide axially |
| Screw in Body Thread | **Medium Class (6H/6g)** | Easy assembly, reusable |
| Bearing on Spider Journal | **H7/k6** (Transition) | Precise concentric rotation |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Dimensioning & Layout

$$ \text{Overall Sheet Length} = 594 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(A2 Size)} $$

$$ \text{Inner Border Distance} = 590 \text{ mm} \times 410 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \text{Title Block Dimensions} = 170 \text{ mm} \times 65 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \text{Number of Parts in Lathe Tailstock (N\textsubscript{LT})} = 8 \text{ to } 11 $$

$$ \text{Number of Parts in Universal Joint (N\textsubscript{UJ})} = 6 \text{ to } 8 $$

$$ \text{Linear Scale Ratio} = \frac{\text{Drawing Length on Sheet}}{\text{True Component Length}} $$

$$ \text{Area of Drawing Sheet} = L \times W = 594 \times 420 = 249{,}480 \text{ mm}^2 $$

$$ \text{Recommended Scale for Module 2} = 1:1 \text{ (preferred)} \quad \text{or} \quad 1:2 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the industry, the BOM is the master input to the **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)** system in ERP software (SAP, Oracle). The part numbers in the BOM directly drive procurement, costing, inventory, and supply chain logistics. A single mistake in the BOM (e.g., wrong material code) can halt a production line.

# 3. Step-by-Step Drafting Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 General Workflow for Module Experiment 2

The drafting of a 2D assembly drawing follows a rigid 12-step procedure. Each step is non-skippable and directly scores marks in the KTU lab evaluation.

### **STEP 1 — Study the Disassembled Parts List**
*Objective:* Read the given part drawings / exploded view. Identify each component, its material, and its function. **Marks Weight: 2**

### **STEP 2 — Determine the Viewing Direction**
*Objective:* Select the principal view such that the **maximum number of parts** appears visible and the **section plane** (vertical centre line through the assembly) cuts through the *core* functional parts.

For the **Lathe Tailstock** → Choose the **front view (elevation)** such that the handwheel is visible on the right, the dead center points to the left, and the section plane passes through the spindle axis.

For the **Universal Joint** → Choose the **front view** such that one yoke is in the plane of the paper and the other is at 90° (or vice versa), with the section plane cutting through one of the yokes and the spider.

### **STEP 3 — Sketch the Construction Lines (Light Pencil)**
*Objective:* Draw the center lines, the base outline rectangle, and the overall bounding box.

$$ L_{\text{base, LT}} \approx 220 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{\text{base, LT}} \approx 95 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{Lathe Tailstock approximate envelope}) $$

$$ L_{\text{base, UJ}} \approx 140 \text{ mm}, \quad H_{\text{base, UJ}} \approx 110 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{Universal Joint approximate envelope}) $$

### **STEP 4 — Block Out the Main Bodies**
*Objective:* Draw the body/base of the assembly as simple rectangles, then progressively add cylindrical/special features.

### **STEP 5 — Position the Spindle/Spider and Yoke(s)**
*Objective:* Using the center lines as a reference, draw the rotational components with proper proportions. Maintain a **gap** of approximately 2 to 3 mm between mating parts on the drawing for visual clarity (this is a drafting convention, not a functional gap).

### **STEP 6 — Add the Sectional Hatching**
*Objective:* Apply **45° parallel hatching** spaced 3 to 4 mm apart, in opposite directions for *adjacent* parts to clearly distinguish them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Rule:** The examiner checks that **no two adjacent parts** in a section have hatching in the *same* direction. This is the single most common cause of mark deduction in lab records.

### **STEP 7 — Draw Hidden Lines (Outside Section)**
*Objective:* In the un-sectioned portions of the assembly, draw the hidden details (threads, internal bores, keyways) using **short-dashed lines** (– – –) of equal length and gap.

### **STEP 8 — Apply Center Lines and Motion Arrows**
*Objective:* All shafts, bores, and rotational features must have a **center line** (long-dash + short-dash, alternating: –·–·–). The sleeve of the tailstock needs a **double-headed arrow** to indicate axial sliding motion.

### **STEP 9 — Dimension the Assembly (Overall + Key)**
*Objective:* Provide:
- **Overall dimensions** (length, width, height) of the assembly.
- **Key functional dimensions** (e.g., center height of tailstock, bore diameter of universal joint).
- **Mating dimensions** (e.g., thread size M20, bore Ø40 H7).

> [!NOTE]
> **Do NOT dimension every part in the assembly drawing** — the part drawings contain part-level dimensions. The assembly drawing carries only *inter-part* and *overall* dimensions.

### **STEP 10 — Add Balloon/Leader Numbers**
*Objective:* Circle a number, connect it to the part with a thin leader line terminating in a **dot** on the part. Numbers are placed *around* the drawing perimeter, never crossing dimension lines.

### **STEP 11 — Draw the BOM Table**
*Objective:* In the top-right corner, draw the 5-column table. Fill in the parts *bottom-up* (last fitted = lowest number). The **base** of the tailstock and the **yoke (forging)** are typically part number 1 because they form the foundation.

### **STEP 12 — Title Block + First-Angle Symbol + Scale**
*Objective:* Bottom-right corner. Contains: Name, Roll No, Branch, Date, Title, Scale (e.g., 1:1), Sheet No, and the **First-Angle Projection symbol** (truncated cone with the smaller circle to the right of the vertical line).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Dimensioning Logic — Lathe Tailstock

The dimensioning strategy for the tailstock is non-trivial. The critical dimensions, in order of importance:

1. **Center Height (H_c)** — Distance from lathe bed surface to dead center tip.
   $$ H_c = \text{lathe bed surface to bottom of tailstock base} + \text{base thickness} + \text{body height to spindle axis} $$

2. **Sleeve Travel (T_s)** — Maximum axial movement of the quill:
   $$ T_s = \text{Total length of quill} - \text{Embedded length in body bore} $$

3. **Spindle Taper (MT\textsubscript{x})** — Morse Taper number (e.g., MT2, MT3, MT4).
   $$ \text{Taper Ratio (TR)} = \frac{D_{\text{major}} - D_{\text{minor}}}{L_{\text{taper}}} $$

4. **Handwheel Pitch Diameter (D\textsubscript{hw})** — For leverage:
   $$ T_{\text{applied}} = F_{\text{axial}} \times \frac{D_{\text{hw}}}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{\text{axial}} = \frac{2 T_{\text{applied}}}{D_{\text{hw}}} $$

5. **Base Slot Width (w_s)** — To engage with the lathe bed:
   $$ w_s = \text{Bed Way Width} + \text{Clearance (0.5 to 1.0 mm)} $$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Dimensioning Logic — Universal Joint

The Hooke's joint has a critical operating parameter — the **angular velocity fluctuation ratio** when the operating angle is non-zero. The KTU 2024 syllabus does not require derivation of the velocity equation, but the dimensional logic is:

$$ \text{Yoke Eye Bore Diameter} = D_e \quad (\text{matches spider journal diameter}) $$

$$ \text{Spider Arm Length} = L_s = \frac{D_e}{2} + \text{Web Thickness (4 to 6 mm)} $$

$$ \text{Operating Angle (}\alpha\text{)} = \arctan\left(\frac{\text{Shaft Offset}}{\text{Coupling Width}}\right) \quad \alpha \in [0^\circ, 45^\circ] $$

$$ \text{Standard Operating Angle (preferred)} = 3^\circ \text{ to } 10^\circ \text{ per shaft} $$

$$ \text{Angular Velocity Ratio} = \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The last equation is the classic **White's coupling ratio** but is beyond the KTU PCRUL307 scope. It is included only for the curious student. The lab evaluation requires only the *drawing* skill, not this calculation.

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls — The "Analytical Matrix" of Errors

| Step | Common Error | Mark Loss | Correction |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Using Third-Angle projection symbol | -2 | Use truncated cone (First-Angle) |
| 2 | Same hatching direction for adjacent parts | -3 | Reverse direction every part |
| 3 | Hidden lines drawn inside the section | -1 | Remove hidden lines within section |
| 4 | Center lines missing on shafts | -2 | Add –·–·– pattern on all rotational axes |
| 5 | Missing motion arrows on sleeve | -1 | Add double-headed axial arrow |
| 6 | BOM placed in wrong corner | -1 | Always top-right |
| 7 | Part numbers placed inside drawing area | -1 | Place on perimeter, not crossing dimensions |
| 8 | Missing scale notation in title block | -1 | State "SCALE 1:1" or "1:2" explicitly |
