# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU lab course **PCRUL307** is evaluated internally. The following questions model the **End-Semester Practical Exam** pattern (Viva + Record) and the **continuous internal evaluation** rubric.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Define "Assembly Drawing" as per BIS SP:46. List four essential features that differentiate it from a part drawing.*

**Model Answer:**

An **assembly drawing** is a technical drawing that depicts two or more machine parts in their *assembled, functional position*, showing their relative arrangement, mating, and motion relationships, conforming to **BIS SP:46 (2003)** and **ISO 128** drafting standards.

Four differentiating features from a part drawing:

1. **Multiple components** are shown together in a single drawing, whereas a part drawing shows only *one* component.
2. **At least one sectional view** is mandatory to reveal internal mating; part drawings show only external views.
3. **A Bill of Materials (BOM)** is attached listing all part numbers, names, quantities, and materials; part drawings do not have a BOM.
4. **Only overall and inter-part (mating) dimensions** are given; individual part dimensions are *omitted* (they are detailed in the part drawings).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the purpose of the Bill of Materials (BOM) in an assembly drawing? State the position and the standard columns of a BOM table as adopted by KTU.*

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose of BOM:** The BOM is the master reference document that itemizes *every part* required to manufacture, assemble, procure spares for, and service the parent machine. It functions as the input to the **MRP/ERP system** in industry and is essential for cost estimation, inventory control, and supply chain management.

**Position on Sheet:** Top-right corner of the drawing sheet, *above* the title block area, with the part-number column closest to the drawing so that leader lines do not cross.

**Standard Columns of BOM (KTU Convention):**

| Part No. | Name | Qty | Material | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sequential balloon number (1, 2, 3 ...) | Descriptive part name (e.g., "Hex Nut M12") | Number of units per assembly (1, 2, 4) | Standard designation (e.g., "EN8", "CI FG200") | "Std. Bought-out" or "Make" |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students often number parts in the *order they appear in the drawing* (e.g., top part = 1). The KTU convention is **bottom-up assembly order** — the *last fitted* part is number 1, the *base* (first fitted) is the highest number. Reversing this loses **1 mark** per occurrence.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

#### **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*(a) Draw the assembled sectional front view of a **Lathe Tailstock** in First-Angle Projection. Show all parts, the section hatching, motion arrows, and balloon numbers. The BOM is mandatory. (7 Marks)*

*(b) With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **function of each numbered part** of the lathe tailstock. State the material of the body and the spindle with justification. (7 Marks)*

---

#### **Solution for (a) — Drafting Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Mark Allocation Breakdown (for examiner's reference):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct First-Angle projection symbol + border + title block | 1.0 |
| Correct sectional view of body, sleeve, spindle, screw, handwheel | 2.0 |
| Hatching correctly applied (alternating direction) | 1.0 |
| Center lines on all rotational axes | 0.5 |
| Hidden lines in un-sectioned portions | 0.5 |
| Balloon numbers correctly placed | 0.5 |
| BOM table complete and cross-referenced | 1.0 |
| Overall neatness and dimensioning | 0.5 |

**Drafting Steps (Textual Substitute for the Drawing):**

1. **Draw the A2 sheet** with a 30 mm margin all around.
2. **Bottom-right corner** → Title block (170 × 65 mm) with: *Title: "ASSEMBLY OF LATHE TAILSTOCK"*, *Scale: 1:1*, *First-Angle symbol*, *Name/Roll No/Date*.
3. **Top-right corner** → BOM table (5 columns, 8 to 11 rows).
4. **Center of sheet** → Front view of tailstock in section.
5. **Construction** (light lines): Draw horizontal center line through the spindle axis. Draw vertical center line through the handwheel axis.
6. **Block out:** A base rectangle (220 × 95 mm), a body trapezoid above it (160 × 140 mm), the sleeve cylinder (Ø60 × 180 mm) protruding to the left, the handwheel disc (Ø140 × 25 mm) on the right.
7. **Detail the section:** Show the internal bore of the body, the screw thread (simplified as two parallel lines + hatching), the spindle Morse taper tip (left end).
8. **Hatching:** 45° lines, 3 mm spacing, alternate direction for: base, body, sleeve, spindle, screw, handwheel, nut, cap.
9. **Center lines:** Through spindle, screw, handwheel, and base slot.
10. **Hidden lines:** Show the keyway on the spindle and the internal thread of the nut.
11. **Motion arrow:** Double-headed arrow (↔) above the sleeve showing axial sliding.
12. **Balloon numbers:** Circle numbers 1 to 10, placed at the perimeter.
13. **Overall dimensions:** Total length (≈ 380 mm), total height (≈ 240 mm), center height (≈ 130 mm), sleeve travel (≈ 100 mm), base width (≈ 220 mm).
14. **Cross-verify** BOM with balloon numbers and the assembly hierarchy.

---

#### **Solution for (b) — Function of Each Part (7 Marks)**

| Part No. | Name | Function | Material | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Nut | Reacts the axial thrust from the screw; prevents screw from moving axially | Brass / Phosphor Bronze | Self-lubricating, absorbs thrust |
| 2 | Cap (Rear) | Closes the rear bore of the body; supports screw tail end | CI | Structural closure |
| 3 | Handwheel | Manual operator; provides leverage for screw rotation | CI (or Alumn. Alloy) | Ergonomic, rigid |
| 4 | Feed Screw (Leadscrew) | Converts rotary motion of handwheel to linear motion of sleeve | EN 8 / EN 24 | High tensile, machinable thread |
| 5 | Key (Woodruff or Parallel) | Locks spindle to screw so they rotate together | EN 8 (Hardened) | Shear strength |
| 6 | Spindle (Dead Center) | Supports the free end of the workpiece; tip is hardened 60-62 HRC | EN 31 (Case-hardened) | Surface hardness, core toughness |
| 7 | Sleeve (Quill) | Holds the spindle; slides axially to advance/retract the dead center | EN 8 (Ground) | Smooth bore, wear-resistant |
| 8 | Set Screws (2 nos.) | Limit the travel of the sleeve to prevent it sliding out | EN 8 | Adjustable, lockable |
| 9 | Body | Main housing; carries the sleeve bore, screw bore, and clamping slot | Cast Iron (FG 200) | Vibration damping, rigidity, low cost |
| 10 | Clamping Bolt + Lever | Locks the tailstock body to the lathe bed ways | EN 8 | High clamping force |
| 11 | Base | Bottom plate that slides on the lathe bed ways | Cast Iron (FG 200) | Wear-resistant sliding surface |

**Material Justification (Critical for 2 marks):**
- **Body in Cast Iron (FG 200):** Cast iron has excellent *damping capacity* (10× that of steel), which prevents chatter during turning. It is also cheap and easily cast into complex shapes.
- **Spindle in EN 31 (case-hardened):** EN 31 is a high-carbon chromium steel. The case-hardening produces a *hard surface* (for wear resistance at the dead center tip) and a *tough core* (to absorb bending loads from the workpiece).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*(a) Draw the assembled front view (one yoke in section) of a **Universal Joint (Hooke's Coupling)** in First-Angle Projection. Show the spider, bearings, yokes, and the BOM. (7 Marks)*

*(b) Explain the **working principle** of the universal joint. Why is the operating angle limited to about 45°? List two practical applications. (7 Marks)*

---

#### **Solution for (a) — Drafting Procedure (7 Marks)**

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct First-Angle projection + border + title block | 1.0 |
| Yoke(s) drawn correctly in front view | 1.5 |
| Spider (cross) drawn with 4 journals | 1.0 |
| Bearings (needle rollers) shown in section in one yoke | 0.5 |
| Hatching (alternating) on yoke and spider | 1.0 |
| Center lines through both shaft axes and spider journals | 0.5 |
| Balloon numbers and BOM | 1.0 |
| Dimensioning and motion arrows | 0.5 |

**Drafting Steps:**

1. Draw the A2 sheet with standard border and title block.
2. Title: "ASSEMBLY OF UNIVERSAL JOINT (HOOKE'S COUPLING)". Scale: 1:1.
3. Draw the two shaft center lines intersecting at the coupling center. Let the angle between them be 5° to 10° (standard operating angle).
4. Draw the two yokes:
   - **Yoke 1** is in the plane of the paper (you see both ears of the fork as a rectangle).
   - **Yoke 2** is perpendicular to the paper (you see one ear overlapping the other as a single rectangle).
5. Draw the **spider (cross)** at the intersection: 4 journals at 90° to each other.
6. Section plane: Pass a vertical plane through the spindle of Yoke 1. The cut reveals:
   - The needle bearings (shown as small circles in the bearing pockets).
   - The spider journal.
   - The yoke hub and one of its ears.
7. Hatching: Yoke 1 at 45° one way, spider at 45° the other way.
8. Center lines: Through both shaft axes, all 4 spider journals, and the bearing pockets.
9. Balloon numbers: 1 to 8 (Spider, Yoke 1, Yoke 2, Needle Rollers × 4, Circlip × 4, Dust Cap × 4, Grease Nipple — but bearings group as "1 set").
10. BOM with 6 to 8 lines.
11. Overall dimensions: Length (≈ 140 mm), bore diameter, web thickness.

---

#### **Solution for (b) — Working Principle & Applications (7 Marks)**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**

The universal joint transmits rotational motion and torque between two non-collinear, intersecting shafts through a **spider (cross) with four journal bearings**. Each journal is seated in the eye of one of the two yokes via **needle roller bearings**.

The fundamental kinematic chain is:

$$ \text{Shaft 1} \rightarrow \text{Yoke 1} \rightarrow \text{Spider (4 journals)} \rightarrow \text{Yoke 2} \rightarrow \text{Shaft 2} $$

The spider acts as an *intermediate floating member* with 3 degrees of freedom (rotates about its own axis + oscillates about two perpendicular axes). This permits the angle between the shafts to vary during operation, and the joint transmits motion even when the shafts are *not* aligned.

**Why is the operating angle limited to ≈ 45°? (2 marks):**

1. **Severe non-uniformity of motion:** The angular velocity ratio is given by:
   $$ \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1} $$
   As α → 45°, the denominator can approach zero (when θ₁ = 90°), causing ω₂ to approach *infinity* — a *kinematic lock*. This generates shock, vibration, and potential mechanical failure.
2. **Bearing wear:** At higher angles, the needle rollers experience increased *edge loading* and *slip*, accelerating wear.
3. **Reduced torque capacity:** The effective moment arm of the bearings decreases with cos(α), so the transmittable torque drops.

**Practical Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Automobile Drive Shafts:** The propeller shaft connecting the gearbox to the rear axle in rear-wheel-drive cars is a universal joint. As the axle moves up and down on the suspension, the angle varies continuously.
2. **Machine Tool Spindles:** Tapered joints between the spindle and the tool holder use Hooke's-type joints for flexibility.
3. *(Other valid examples: Robotic arm joints, helicopter rotor drives, agricultural PTO shafts.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Master Pitfall List

> [!WARNING]
> **The Top 5 Mark-Deduction Triggers in Module Experiment 2:**
> 1. **Forgetting the First-Angle Projection symbol** → **−2 marks** (mandatory per BIS).
> 2. **Two adjacent parts hatched in the SAME direction** → **−3 marks** (this is the cardinal rule of sectioning).
> 3. **No BOM table or incomplete BOM** → **−2 to −3 marks**.
> 4. **BOM numbers not matching the balloon numbers on the drawing** → **−1.5 marks** (cross-verification failure).
> 5. **Center lines omitted on the spindle / screw / shaft / spider journal axes** → **−1 mark per axis**.
>
> **Bonus Tip:** Always draw the motion arrow on the tailstock sleeve (double-headed axial arrow). Examiners specifically look for it. Drawing it can **gain +0.5 marks** as a "good practice" credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Assembly Drawing = Multiple parts + Section + BOM + Dimensions.** Remember all four elements are mandatory.
- [x] **First-Angle Projection** is mandatory for all KTU drawings (India convention).
- [x] **Hatching:** 45° parallel lines, 3 to 4 mm spacing, **opposite directions for adjacent parts** in section.
- [x] **Hidden lines are omitted inside the section** but retained in un-sectioned portions.
- [x] **Center lines (–·–·–)** must appear on every rotational/spherical part: spindles, screws, spiders, shafts, bearings.
- [x] **BOM position:** Top-right corner. **5 columns:** Part No., Name, Qty, Material, Remarks.
- [x] **BOM numbering:** Bottom-up assembly order (last fitted = 1, base = highest number).
- [x] **Balloon numbers:** Circles with the number, leader line ending in a dot on the part, placed at the drawing perimeter.
- [x] **Title Block position:** Bottom-right corner. Contains Title, Scale, First-Angle Symbol, Name, Roll No, Branch, Date, Sheet No.
- [x] **Lathe Tailstock** parts: Base, Body, Sleeve, Spindle, Screw, Handwheel, Nut, Cap, Key, Set Screws, Clamping Bolt.
- [x] **Universal Joint** parts: Yoke × 2, Spider, Needle Bearings × 4 sets, Circlips × 4, Dust Caps × 4, Grease Nipple.
- [x] **Material of body:** Cast Iron FG 200 (damping). **Material of spindle:** EN 31 (case-hardened).
- [x] **Operating angle of universal joint:** Limited to ≈ 45° to prevent kinematic lock and bearing failure.
- [x] **Lathe tailstock function:** Supports free end of workpiece via dead center to prevent deflection and chatter.
- [x] **Morse Taper** (MT2, MT3, MT4) is used on the spindle to hold the dead center.
- [x] **Standard fits:** Sleeve in body = H7/g6 (sliding), Spindle in sleeve = H7/h6 (locational-clearance).
- [x] **Standard sheet size:** A2 (594 × 420 mm) with 30 mm margin. Scale: 1:1 or 1:2.
- [x] **Motion arrow (double-headed)** on the tailstock sleeve to indicate axial sliding — earns bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Solid Modeling Software: Basic Commands, Development of Simple 3D Drawings, and File Formats (IGES, STL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Solid Modeling Software: Basic Commands, Development of Simple 3D Drawings, and File Formats (IGES, STL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solid Modeling Software** is a class of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) tools that use mathematical representations of three-dimensional (3D) solid objects to create, visualize, manipulate, and analyze the geometric and physical properties of engineering components. Unlike 2D drafting, solid modeling defines an object with complete volume information, enabling unambiguous representation through Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG), Boundary Representation (B-rep), or Sweep methods.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory context (PCRUL307), students are expected to work with industry-standard parametric solid modelers such as **SolidWorks**, **CATIA**, **Creo (Pro/E)**, **Fusion 360**, or **AutoCAD Inventor** to develop simple 3D parts, assemblies, and engineering drawings.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A solid model is a *complete* and *unambiguous* mathematical representation of a 3D object that contains both surface and volumetric data. It is the digital equivalent of a physical prototype.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a sculptor working with digital clay. Instead of using your hands, you use commands typed or clicked on a computer screen. You start with a 2D sketch (like drawing the outline of a face on a flat board), then "pull" it into 3D (like extruding clay through your fingers to give it depth). You can add features like holes, fillets (smoothed edges), and bosses (raised cylindrical features) — just as a sculptor would add eyes, smooth corners, and ears.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a solid model as a *digital LEGO assembly*. Each feature (cube, cylinder, hole) is a primitive that snaps into place, and the software tracks the exact dimensions, materials, and assembly relationships between every piece. The **.STL file** is like a 3D photograph of the finished LEGO model, while the **.IGES file** is like the original editable blueprint that any other CAD program can read.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics and Standards

- **Model Tolerance Standard:** ISO 1101 (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)
- **Default Unit Systems:** IPS (Inch-Pound-Second) and MMKS (Millimeter-Kilogram-Second)
- **Standard Initial Graphics Exchange Specification:** ANSI/USPRO/ISO 10303 (STEP)
- **Standard Tessellation Density for STL:** 1,000 – 10,000 triangles per square inch of surface
- **Standard Precision Factor in CATIA:** 0.001 mm (default)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Parametric Modeling:** A feature-based modeling technique where the dimensions and constraints of a feature automatically drive the geometry of the entire model. Changing one dimension propagates through the entire part tree.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categories of Solid Modeling Techniques

Solid modeling systems use three principal representation schemes:

**1. Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG)**
- Builds complex solids by applying Boolean operations (Union, Subtract, Intersect) on primitive shapes (block, sphere, cylinder, cone, torus).
- **Why:** Simple, intuitive, mathematically rigorous.
- **How:** Each Boolean operation produces a new node in a CSG tree.

**2. Boundary Representation (B-rep)**
- Describes a solid by its bounding surfaces (faces, edges, vertices) and their topological relationships.
- **Why:** Efficient for rendering, mesh generation, and visualization.
- **How:** Uses Euler's formula for closed solids.

**3. Sweep Representation**
- Generates a solid by moving a 2D profile along a path (linear sweep = extrusion; circular sweep = revolution).
- **Why:** Ideal for axisymmetric parts like shafts, bottles, and pipes.

### 2.2 Euler's Formula for Closed Polyhedral Solids

A fundamental topological invariant that every valid solid model must satisfy:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

Where:
- $V$ = number of vertices
- $E$ = number of edges
- $F$ = number of faces

For a cube: $V = 8$, $E = 12$, $F = 6$. Verification: $8 - 12 + 6 = 2$. ✓

For a tetrahedron: $V = 4$, $E = 6$, $F = 4$. Verification: $4 - 6 + 4 = 2$. ✓

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Area of Cylinder | $A = 2\pi r (r + h)$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Extruded boss/feature |
| Volume of Cylinder | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Mass property calculation |
| Volume of Sphere | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Revolved feature |
| Volume of Cone | $V = \dfrac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Revolved feature |
| Euler's Topology | $V - E + F = 2$ | dimensionless | Validity of solid model |
| STL Triangle Count (approx.) | $N \approx A \times \rho$ | triangles | 3D Printing export |
| Chordal Deviation (STL) | $\delta = r \left(1 - \cos \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | $\text{mm}$ | Mesh refinement |
| IGES Version | ANSI Y14.26M-1981 / ASME Y14.26M | — | Interoperability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Chordal Deviation ($\delta$):** The maximum perpendicular distance between the curved CAD surface and its faceted STL approximation. Lower $\delta$ = higher mesh quality = larger file size.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **3D Printing / Additive Manufacturing:** STL files drive FDM, SLA, and SLS printers in biomedical, aerospace, and consumer product industries.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Solid models are imported into ANSYS, Abaqus, or HyperWorks for stress, thermal, and modal analysis.
- **CNC Machining:** IGES and STEP files drive CAM software (Mastercam, Fusion 360 CAM) for toolpath generation.
- **Virtual Reality & Digital Twin:** Solid models form the geometric backbone of Industry 4.0 digital twins.
- **Medical Implants:** Patient-specific implants (cranial plates, knee replacements) are designed using solid models derived from CT/MRI scans.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Workflows and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Standard Workflow for Developing a Simple 3D Drawing

A typical 3D modeling session follows a sequential, parametric procedure. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step procedure (Universal across SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, Fusion 360, Inventor):

**Step 1 — Document Setup**
Open the software → New Part → Select template (e.g., `Part_MM.prt` for millimeters or `Part_IN.prt` for inches). The template pre-defines units, datum planes, and material libraries.

**Step 2 — Selection of Datum Planes / Sketch Planes**
Choose a base plane (Front, Top, or Right) on which to draw the first 2D profile. Datums are reference planes used to constrain geometry. They are infinite and have no mass.

**Step 3 — 2D Sketch Creation**
Use primitive sketch commands to create the base profile.

**Step 4 — Application of Dimensions and Constraints**
- *Dimensions* define size (e.g., line length = 50 mm, circle diameter = 20 mm).
- *Geometric constraints* define relationships (horizontal, vertical, perpendicular, tangent, equal, concentric).
- *Dimensional constraints* drive the size (fully define the sketch → color turns black in SolidWorks).

**Step 5 — Conversion to 3D Feature**
Exit the sketch environment. Apply a 3D feature:
- **Extrude Boss/Base:** Pulls the 2D profile perpendicular to the sketch plane.
- **Revolve:** Rotates the profile around a centerline.
- **Sweep:** Moves a profile along a path.
- **Loft:** Transitions between two or more profiles.

**Step 6 — Application of Secondary Features**
- Holes (simple, counterbore, countersink, threaded)
- Fillets (rounded edges)
- Chamfers (angled edges, typically 45°)
- Ribs, gussets, webs
- Shell (hollows out a solid body, leaving walls of specified thickness)

**Step 7 — Material Assignment and Mass Property Evaluation**
Assign material from the library (e.g., AISI 1020 Steel). The software calculates mass, volume, center of gravity, and moment of inertia automatically.

**Step 8 — Save and Export**
Save as native format (`.sldprt`, `.CATPart`, `.prt`, `.ipt`) and export to exchange formats (`.stl`, `.iges`, `.step`).

### 3.2 Worked Example: Derivation of STL File Size for a Sphere

**Problem:** Estimate the number of triangles required to mesh a sphere of radius $R = 25 \text{ mm}$ using an STL file with a chordal deviation of $\delta = 0.1 \text{ mm}$.

**Given:**
- Radius $R = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Chordal deviation $\delta = 0.1 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1:** Express the chordal deviation in terms of the triangle's circumscribed angle.

$$\delta = R \left(1 - \cos \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\cos \dfrac{\theta}{2}$.

$$\cos \dfrac{\theta}{2} = 1 - \dfrac{\delta}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$\cos \dfrac{\theta}{2} = 1 - \dfrac{0.1}{25} = 1 - 0.004 = 0.996$$

**Step 4:** Compute $\dfrac{\theta}{2}$.

$$\dfrac{\theta}{2} = \arccos(0.996) \approx 5.13°$$

**Step 5:** Compute the full angle $\theta$.

$$\theta \approx 10.26° = 0.1791 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the number of subdivisions along a great circle.

$$n_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\theta} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.1791} \approx 35.08 \approx 36 \text{ triangles per longitude}$$

**Step 7:** Calculate the total number of triangles on the sphere.

The standard sphere tessellation formula:

$$N = 2 \times n_{\text{lat}} \times (n_{\text{lat}} - 1) + 2$$

$$N = 2 \times 36 \times 35 + 2 = 2520 + 2 = 2522 \text{ triangles}$$

**Step 8:** Estimate the STL file size.
Each triangle requires **50 bytes** in ASCII STL (header, normal, 3 vertices × 12 bytes + attribute = 50 bytes).

$$\text{File Size} = 2522 \times 50 \approx 126{,}100 \text{ bytes} \approx 126 \text{ KB}$$

### 3.3 Procedural Comparison Matrix: Extrude vs Revolve vs Sweep vs Loft

| Feature Type | Required Input | Best Used For | Example Part |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extrude Boss | 2D closed profile + depth | Prismatic blocks, plates, brackets | L-bracket |
| Revolve | 2D profile + centerline | Axisymmetric parts | Shaft, pulley, bottle |
| Sweep | 2D profile + 2D/3D path | Constant cross-section along path | Pipe elbow, spring |
| Loft | Two or more profiles | Variable cross-section transitions | Aircraft fuselage, bottle |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### 4.1 Solid Modeling Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Part File] --> B[Select Datum Plane]
    B --> C[Create 2D Sketch]
    C --> D[Apply Dimensions and Constraints]
    D --> E{Sketch Fully Defined?}
    E -- No --> F[Add Missing Dimensions]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Exit Sketch Environment]
    G --> H[Apply 3D Feature: Extrude or Revolve]
    H --> I[Add Secondary Features: Holes, Fillets, Chamfers]
    I --> J[Assign Material and Compute Mass Properties]
    J --> K[Save Native File: SLDPRT or CATPart]
    K --> L[Export to Exchange Format: STL or IGES]
    L --> M[End: Model Ready for CAE or 3D Printing]
```

### 4.2 CAD File Format Interoperability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Native["Native CAD Formats"]
        N1[SolidWorks: SLDPRT]
        N2[CATIA: CATPart]
        N3[Creo: PRT]
        N4[Inventor: IPT]
        N5[Fusion 360: F3D]
    end

    subgraph Exchange["Exchange / Neutral Formats"]
        E1[IGES: IGS]
        E2[STEP: STP]
        E3[STL: Faceted Mesh]
        E4[Parasolid: X_T]
    end

    subgraph Downstream["Downstream Applications"]
        D1[3D Printing Slicer]
        D2[FEA Solver]
        D3[CNC CAM Software]
        D4[Visualization and VR]
    end

    N1 --> E1
    N2 --> E1
    N3 --> E1
    N4 --> E1
    N1 --> E2
    N2 --> E2
    N1 --> E3
    N3 --> E3
    E1 --> D2
    E2 --> D2
    E2 --> D3
    E3 --> D1
    E3 --> D4
    E1 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Basic Command Hierarchy in Solid Modeling Software

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Solid Modeling Command Tree] --> S[Sketch Commands]
    Root --> F[Feature Commands]
    Root --> E[Edit Commands]
    Root --> A[Assembly Commands]
    Root --> D[Drawing Commands]

    S --> S1[Line]
    S --> S2[Circle]
    S --> S3[Rectangle]
    S --> S4[Arc]
    S --> S5[Spline]
    S --> S6[Polygon]

    F --> F1[Extrude Boss or Base]
    F --> F2[Revolve]
    F --> F3[Sweep]
    F --> F4[Loft]
    F --> F5[Hole Wizard]
    F --> F6[Fillet]
    F --> F7[Chamfer]
    F --> F8[Shell]
    F --> F9[Pattern: Linear or Circular]
    F --> F10[Mirror]

    E --> E1[Move or Copy Body]
    E --> E2[Combine: Add, Subtract, Intersect]
    E --> E3[Delete Face]
    E --> E4[Replace Face]

    A --> A1[Insert Component]
    A --> A2[Mate: Coincident, Concentric, Distance]
    A --> A3[Interference Detection]

    D --> D1[Standard 3-View Drawing]
    D --> D2[Sectioned View]
    D --> D3[Isometric View]
    D --> D4[Bill of Materials]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define solid modeling. List any two solid modeling software used in industry.*

**Model Answer:**
Solid modeling is a technique used in CAD to create a complete, unambiguous 3D mathematical representation of an object, capturing both surface and volume information. The model stores topological data (faces, edges, vertices) and geometric data (curves, surfaces) to enable operations like Boolean operations, mass property calculation, and interference checking.

Two solid modeling software widely used in industry are:
1. **SolidWorks** — used extensively in mechanical product design and sheet metal.
2. **CATIA** — used in aerospace and automotive industries (e.g., Boeing, Airbus, Tata Motors).

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is an STL file? State one advantage and one limitation of the STL format.*

**Model Answer:**
STL (Standard Tessellation Language, originally *Stereolithography*) is a file format that represents the surface of a 3D object as a collection of triangular facets. Each triangle is defined by three vertices and an outward-pointing unit normal vector.

- **Advantage:** Universal compatibility — accepted by virtually all 3D printers and slicer software worldwide. *(1 mark)*
- **Limitation:** STL stores only surface (faceted) data with no color, material, texture, or assembly hierarchy information. *(1 mark)*

*(1 mark for definition)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose marks by (a) confusing *Extrude* with *Revolve*, (b) writing the *full form of STL incorrectly* (it is *Stereolithography*, NOT *Standard Triangle Language*), and (c) forgetting to mention the *ASCII vs Binary* difference of STL. Always specify ASCII or Binary STL.

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) representation scheme. List the three Boolean operations used in CSG with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between IGES and STEP file formats. Which one is preferred for modern CAD data exchange and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CSG Representation:**
Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) is a solid modeling technique in which complex objects are built by combining simple 3D primitives (block, cylinder, sphere, cone, torus) using Boolean operations.

**Required Sketch (describe in text):** A CSG tree showing a block and a cylinder combined via Union, with another cylinder subtracted to form a stepped shaft.

```
        UNION
       /      \
    BLOCK     CYLINDER_A
                 |
              SUBTRACT
              /      \
           UNION     CYLINDER_B
          /    \
       BLOCK  CYLINDER_A
```

**Three Boolean Operations:**
1. **Union (∪):** Combines two solids into one; the resulting volume is the sum. *Example:* Combining a block and a cylinder to form a stepped pin.
2. **Subtract (−):** Removes the volume of one solid from another. *Example:* Drilling a hole (cylinder) through a block.
3. **Intersect (∩):** Keeps only the volume common to both solids. *Example:* Finding the overlapping region of two intersecting cylinders.

**Properties of CSG:**
- Unambiguous, closed-form representation.
- Easily validated using Euler's formula: $V - E + F = 2$.
- Supports set-membership queries (point inside/outside solid).
- Limitation: not all shapes are representable (e.g., free-form sculptured surfaces).

*(3 marks for diagram description, 2 marks for Boolean operations, 2 marks for properties)*

**(b) IGES vs STEP:**

| Parameter | IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification) | STEP (Standard for the Exchange of Product Data) |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | ANSI/USPRO Y14.26M-1981 | ISO 10303 |
| **Year** | 1980 | 1994 |
| **Scope** | Geometry + limited topology | Geometry + topology + tolerances + material + PMI |
| **File Extension** | `.igs` or `.iges` | `.stp` or `.step` |
| **Data Type** | Surface and wireframe (older versions), solid (5.x+) | Full product data including assembly, kinematics |
| **Modern Use** | Legacy systems, surface modeling | Industry 4.0, digital twin, MBD |
| **Error Rate** | Higher (approx. 5–10% translation loss) | Lower (<1% loss) |

**Preferred Format:** **STEP (`.stp`)** is preferred for modern CAD data exchange because it supports:
- Full product manufacturing information (PMI).
- Tolerances and GD&T annotations.
- Assembly structure and kinematics.
- Material properties and metadata.
- Long-term archival and traceability required by Industry 4.0.

*(3 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for STEP advantages, 2 marks for industrial relevance)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to create a 3D model of a flange coupling hub in any solid modeling software. Mention the basic commands used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a Boundary Representation (B-rep)? Verify Euler's formula for a cube and a tetrahedron. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure to Model a Flange Coupling Hub:**

| Step | Operation | Command Used | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | File → New Part | New | Open a new part file in MMGS (millimeter) units |
| 2 | Select Front Plane | Plane Selection | Datum plane for the first sketch |
| 3 | Sketch → Circle | Circle | Draw a circle of diameter 80 mm (hub OD) |
| 4 | Smart Dimension | Dimension | Fully define the circle to diameter 80 mm |
| 5 | Features → Extrude Boss | Extrude | Extrude to depth 60 mm |
| 6 | Select Top Face of hub | Face Selection | Datum for second sketch |
| 7 | Sketch → Circle | Circle | Draw circle of diameter 40 mm (shaft hole) |
| 8 | Features → Extrude Cut | Cut-Extrude | Cut through all to create bore |
| 9 | Sketch → Circle pattern | Circular Pattern | Draw 4 holes on PCD 60 mm, dia. 12 mm |
| 10 | Features → Extrude Cut | Cut-Extrude | Cut through all to create bolt holes |
| 11 | Edge → Fillet | Fillet | Add 3 mm fillet on all sharp edges |
| 12 | Evaluate → Mass Properties | Mass Properties | Compute volume, mass, CG |
| 13 | File → Save As | Save | Save as `.SLDPRT` |
| 14 | File → Export → STL | Export | Export mesh for 3D printing |

*(3 marks for procedure table, 2 marks for command list, 2 marks for parameters)*

**(b) Boundary Representation (B-rep):**
B-rep describes a solid object by its bounding surfaces. The data is stored as a hierarchical structure of:
- **Vertices (V):** 0-dimensional points.
- **Edges (E):** 1-dimensional curves connecting vertices.
- **Faces (F):** 2-dimensional surfaces bounded by edges.
- **Loops:** Closed chains of edges bounding a face.
- **Shells:** Sets of connected faces forming a closed boundary.

**Euler's Formula:**

$$V - E + F = 2$$

**Verification for a Cube:**
- Vertices: 8 corners → $V = 8$
- Edges: 12 edges → $E = 12$
- Faces: 6 square faces → $F = 6$

$$V - E + F = 8 - 12 + 6 = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification for a Tetrahedron:**
- Vertices: 4 corners → $V = 4$
- Edges: 6 edges → $E = 6$
- Faces: 4 triangular faces → $F = 4$

$$V - E + F = 4 - 6 + 4 = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

Hence, Euler's topological invariant is verified for both solids.

*(3 marks for B-rep definition, 2 marks for cube verification, 2 marks for tetrahedron verification)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- **Solid Modeling** captures complete 3D volume information, unlike 2D drafting or wireframe models.
- The three principal representation schemes are **CSG, B-rep, and Sweep**.
- **Euler's formula** $V - E + F = 2$ must be satisfied by every valid solid model.
- **Basic Commands** fall into four families: Sketch (Line, Circle, Arc, Spline), Feature (Extrude, Revolve, Sweep, Loft, Hole, Fillet, Chamfer, Shell, Pattern, Mirror), Edit (Combine, Move/Copy, Delete Face), and Assembly/Drawing (Mate, BOM, Section View).
- **Parametric modeling** links dimensions and features so that design changes propagate automatically.
- **STL (Stereolithography)** files are faceted triangle meshes — universal for 3D printing, but lossy and lacking color/material data.
- **IGES (Initial Graphics Exchange Specification)** is an older neutral format (1980) for CAD data exchange; **STEP (ISO 10303)** is the modern, comprehensive replacement.
- **Chordal deviation** $\delta$ controls STL mesh refinement; smaller $\delta$ → more triangles → larger file size.
- **3D printing workflow:** Native CAD → Export STL → Slicer (Cura, PrusaSlicer) → G-code → Printer.
- **CAE workflow:** Native CAD → Export STEP/IGES → FEA Solver (ANSYS, Abaqus) → Mesh → Solve → Post-process.
- For KTU lab exams, always **fully define your sketch** (black color in SolidWorks) before applying any 3D feature.
- Always include a **datum reference** (Front, Top, Right plane) for every part you create.
- Mention the **unit system** (mm or inches) at the beginning of every drawing as per KTU 2024 evaluation norms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Assembly Models: Socket and Spigot Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly Models: Socket and Spigot Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Socket and Spigot Joint** is a permanent type of pipe joint widely used for connecting **Cast Iron (CI) pipes** in water supply mains, sewage systems, and underground drainage lines. It is a *detachable* (or semi-permanent) joint formed by inserting the plain end (called the **Spigot**) of one pipe into the enlarged/belled end (called the **Socket**) of the adjoining pipe, and the resulting **annular gap** is sealed with molten lead, which is then caulked (compacted) to make it leak-proof.

In the context of **PCRUL307 – Machine Drawing and Solid Modelling Laboratory (Module Experiment 2)**, the term refers to the **3D parametric solid model** of this joint, created using a CAD package (SolidWorks / CATIA / Fusion 360 / Creo), comprising at least two distinct parts — the *Socket Pipe* and the *Spigot Pipe* — assembled with proper **geometric mates** and presented in a sectioned view to expose the lead filling.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Socket** | The female, bell-shaped, enlarged end of a CI pipe (also called the *bell end*). |
| **Spigot** | The male, plain cylindrical end of a CI pipe that fits inside the socket. |
| **Annular Space** | The ring-shaped gap between the outer surface of the spigot and the inner surface of the socket. |
| **Caulking** | The process of compacting the lead with a caulking tool and hammer to make the joint water-tight. |
| **Hemp / Yarn** | Gasket material (jute or hemp rope soaked in tar) used as backing before pouring lead. |
| **Lead Run** | The molten lead poured into the annular space that solidifies to form the seal. |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU reference dimension set for a typical **DN 100 mm** socket–spigot joint: Outer pipe diameter **D = 100 mm**, wall thickness **t = 10 mm**, socket outer diameter **D₁ = 140 mm**, socket depth **L = 80 mm**, lead filling depth **≈ 25–30 mm**, and lead filling clearance **≈ 6–8 mm** radial.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to join **two drinking straws** end-to-end so that water does not leak out of the connection. If you simply push the ends together, water sprays everywhere. However, if one of the straws has a **wider, cup-like bell at its end** (like a flower vase mouth), and the other straw is plain at the end, you can **slide the plain end into the cup**. The remaining gap between them is like a small *moat*. If you pour **melted wax** into this moat and let it solidify, then press it firmly with a blunt rod, the joint becomes **completely leak-proof** — you now have a *socket and spigot joint*.

In simple words:

> **Cup = Socket**, **Plain straw end = Spigot**, **Wax = Lead**, **Pressing action = Caulking**.

Real-world places where you have *unknowingly* seen this joint:
- The big black cast-iron pipes under your city road carrying drinking water.
- Old building drainage stacks on the exterior walls.
- Historical fire hydrant connections in Kerala municipalities.

The reason it is still taught (and modeled in labs) is that the joint is **self-aligning**, **accommodates small angular deflections** (up to 3°–5°), and **does not require threading, welding, or flanges** — making it ideal for buried pipelines where threading/welding is impractical.

![Socket and Spigot Joint — Reference Sketch](attachment://socket_spigot_sketch.png)

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions Used in the Experiment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU lab manual for **PCRUL307 – Module Experiment 2** typically uses the following metrics, which students **must** reproduce in their 3D assembly:
>
> - **Standard Pipe Outer Diameter (D):** 100 mm (DN 100) — *the most common KTU lab dimension.*
> - **Wall Thickness (t):** 10 mm.
> - **Socket Outer Diameter (D₁):** 140 mm.
> - **Socket Length / Depth (L):** 80 mm.
> - **Radial Lead Clearance (c):** 6 mm (so socket inner dia = 100 + 2×6 = 112 mm; spigot OD = 100 mm).
> - **Lead Filling Depth from pipe face:** 25–30 mm.
> - **Hemp/Yarn Packing Depth (behind lead):** 20–25 mm.
> - **Total assembled length:** ≈ 400 mm (200 mm per pipe).

All values are in **millimeters (mm)** unless otherwise stated. The drawing sheet is usually **A3 landscape** with a title block in the bottom-right corner.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why is the Socket and Spigot Joint Needed? (Engineering Logic)

When a long pipeline is to be laid (e.g., a water main running several kilometres), **it is impossible to cast or transport a single pipe of that length**. Therefore, the pipeline is broken into standard lengths (commonly **3 m, 4 m, or 5 m**), and these individual pipes are **joined in situ** at the site.

Cast iron is **brittle** — it cannot be threaded (like a mild-steel pipe) and cannot be welded easily. Hence we need a joining method that:

1. Does not require machining the pipe ends.
2. Allows a small **deflection** so the pipeline can follow a gentle curve.
3. Is **leak-proof** under internal fluid pressure.
4. Can absorb **thermal expansion** of the pipe.
5. Can be dismantled (for repair) without destroying the pipe.

The socket-and-spigot joint satisfies all of these. The molten lead, when caulked, presses the spigot outward against the socket wall, creating a tight, gasket-less seal.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Joint

1. **Cleaning** — Both socket and spigot ends are thoroughly cleaned to remove dirt, rust, and grease.
2. **Inserting the Spigot** — The spigot of pipe B is inserted into the socket of pipe A until it bottoms out against the socket's inner shoulder.
3. **Hemp/Yarn Packing** — A rope of tarred jute (hemp) is wrapped around the spigot and pushed into the back of the socket using a yarning iron, leaving room for the lead.
4. **Pouring Lead** — Molten lead (at ≈ 400 °C) is poured into the remaining annular space, filling it completely.
5. **Caulking** — After the lead solidifies, it is hammered with a caulking tool so that it expands laterally and seals the gap tightly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (For Drawing Calculations)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all key dimensions and derived quantities** that students must compute for their solid model. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep the table clean.

| S.No. | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Typical KTU Value | Unit |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | Outer diameter of pipe (Spigot OD) | $D$ | Given | **100** | mm |
| 2 | Wall thickness of pipe | $t$ | Given | **10** | mm |
| 3 | Inner diameter of pipe | $d$ | $d = D - 2t$ | **80** | mm |
| 4 | Radial lead clearance | $c$ | Given (standard) | **6** | mm |
| 5 | Socket inner diameter | $D_{in,s}$ | $D_{in,s} = D + 2c$ | **112** | mm |
| 6 | Socket outer diameter | $D_{out,s}$ | Given (standard) | **140** | mm |
| 7 | Socket wall thickness | $t_s$ | $t_s = \frac{D_{out,s} - D_{in,s}}{2}$ | **14** | mm |
| 8 | Socket depth (length) | $L$ | Given | **80** | mm |
| 9 | Lead filling depth | $L_{Pb}$ | $L_{Pb} \approx 0.30 L$ to $0.40 L$ | **25 – 30** | mm |
| 10 | Hemp/Yarn packing depth | $L_h$ | $L_h = L - L_{Pb}$ | **50 – 55** | mm |
| 11 | Total length of one pipe (modelled) | $l_p$ | Given (lab) | **200** | mm |
| 12 | Total assembled length | $l_{tot}$ | $l_{tot} = l_p + l_p - L$ (overlap) | **320** | mm |
| 13 | Volume of lead (per joint) | $V_{Pb}$ | $V_{Pb} = \pi \cdot \frac{D_{in,s} + D}{2} \cdot c \cdot L_{Pb}$ | ≈ **49 800** | mm³ |
| 14 | Mass of lead (per joint) | $m_{Pb}$ | $m_{Pb} = V_{Pb} \cdot \rho_{Pb}$, with $\rho_{Pb} = 11.34$ g/cm³ | ≈ **565** | g |
| 15 | Allowable angular deflection | $\theta$ | Empirical | **3° – 5°** | degree |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU lab standard):**
> - Socket depth $L$ is roughly **0.8 × D**.
> - Lead depth $L_{Pb}$ is roughly **0.30 × L**.
> - Hemp depth $L_h$ is the remainder.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply** — underground cast iron mains in cities like Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, and Kozhikode.
- **Sewage and drainage** — soil and waste stacks in old Keralite buildings.
- **Fire-fighting mains** — underground hydrant lines.
- **Industrial effluent lines** — where slight misalignment between pipe segments is unavoidable.
- **Steam and gas lines** (low pressure) in older plants.

In **modern practice**, ductile iron (DI) pipes with push-on rubber-gasket socket-spigot joints have largely replaced the lead-caulked version, but the **geometric form of the joint is identical**, which is why it remains a standard 3D-modelling exercise in KTU laboratories.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Modelling Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Standard Dimensions

Starting from the **standard IS 1538 / IS 3114 specifications** adopted by KTU, we derive every modelling dimension step-by-step from the fundamental input $D = 100$ mm and $t = 10$ mm.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Inner Diameter of the Pipe

The pipe is a hollow cylinder. Its inner (bore) diameter is the outer diameter minus twice the wall thickness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= D - 2t \\[4pt]
d &= 100 - 2(10) \\[4pt]
d &= 100 - 20 \\[4pt]
d &= 80 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The wall has a uniform thickness $t$ on both sides of the bore, so we subtract $2t$ from the outer diameter $D$ to obtain the inner bore diameter $d$.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Socket Inner Diameter

The socket's inner diameter is the spigot's outer diameter plus the **radial lead clearance** on *both* sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{in,s} &= D + 2c \\[4pt]
D_{in,s} &= 100 + 2(6) \\[4pt]
D_{in,s} &= 100 + 12 \\[4pt]
D_{in,s} &= 112 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $c$ is the gap between the spigot surface and the socket wall on one side. Since the gap exists symmetrically on the opposite side as well, we add $2c$ to $D$.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Socket Wall Thickness

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_s &= \frac{D_{out,s} - D_{in,s}}{2} \\[4pt]
t_s &= \frac{140 - 112}{2} \\[4pt]
t_s &= \frac{28}{2} \\[4pt]
t_s &= 14 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The difference between outer and inner diameters of the socket is the *diametral wall thickness*; halving gives the radial wall thickness.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Lead Depth and Hemp Depth

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{Pb} &= 0.35 \times L = 0.35 \times 80 = 28 \text{ mm (rounded to 30 mm)} \\[4pt]
L_h &= L - L_{Pb} = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Empirical practice fixes the lead at about one-third of the socket depth; the remaining two-thirds is filled with hemp.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Volume of Lead per Joint

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{Pb} &= \pi \cdot \left( \frac{D_{in,s} + D}{2} \right) \cdot c \cdot L_{Pb} \\[4pt]
V_{Pb} &= \pi \cdot \left( \frac{112 + 100}{2} \right) \cdot 6 \cdot 30 \\[4pt]
V_{Pb} &= \pi \cdot 106 \cdot 6 \cdot 30 \\[4pt]
V_{Pb} &= \pi \cdot 19\,080 \\[4pt]
V_{Pb} &\approx 59\,935 \text{ mm}^{3} \;\;(\text{≈ 60 cm}^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The lead fills an annular ring of mean diameter $\frac{D_{in,s}+D}{2}$, radial thickness $c$, and axial length $L_{Pb}$. The annular area, multiplied by length, gives the volume.

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Mass of Lead Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Pb} &= V_{Pb} \times \rho_{Pb} \\[4pt]
m_{Pb} &= 59\,935 \text{ mm}^{3} \times 11.34 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^{3} \\[4pt]
m_{Pb} &\approx 679.7 \text{ g} \approx 0.68 \text{ kg per joint}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Density of lead $\rho_{Pb} = 11.34$ g/cm³ = $11.34 \times 10^{-3}$ g/mm³. Mass = volume × density.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step 3D Modelling Procedure (SolidWorks Convention)

> [!NOTE]
> This procedure applies generically to **any parametric CAD package** (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo, Fusion 360, NX, Inventor). The student must record each step in the lab record.

### Part A — Modelling the Spigot Pipe

1. **Start a new part** → units = **mm**, standard = **ISO**.
2. **Sketch on Front Plane** → draw a vertical centre line (origin).
3. **Sketch the profile** (right half) using a **Line + Arc**:
   - Outer vertical line: $D/2 = 50$ mm from centre.
   - Inner vertical line: $d/2 = 40$ mm from centre.
   - Top and bottom horizontal lines: close the profile at the top with a small chamfer/flat (50 mm wide across full pipe).
4. **Revolve Boss/Base** the closed profile **360°** about the centre line → pipe body.
5. **Shell** the body (or model directly with both walls if revolved profile is hollow) with inner wall at 40 mm radius → **wall thickness 10 mm confirmed**.
6. **Fillet** the outer corner with radius 1–2 mm (for realistic CI pipe).
7. **Save as** `Spigot_Pipe.SLDPRT`.

### Part B — Modelling the Socket Pipe

1. **Start a new part** → same template.
2. **Sketch on Front Plane** → vertical centre line.
3. **Sketch the profile** (right half) with these key heights from bottom (socket open end) upward:
   - 0 → 80 mm: socket outer radius **70 mm** (since $D_{out,s}/2 = 140/2$).
   - 0 → 80 mm: socket inner radius **56 mm** (since $D_{in,s}/2 = 112/2$).
   - 80 mm → (200 + something) mm: pipe outer radius **50 mm**.
   - Pipe inner radius **40 mm** throughout.
4. Close the profile at the bottom (open face of the socket) and at the top.
5. **Revolve Boss/Base 360°** → socket pipe body.
6. **Save as** `Socket_Pipe.SLDPRT`.

### Part C — Modelling the Lead Filling (Optional 3rd Part)

1. New part → sketch an **annular rectangle**:
   - Outer width (radial): 56 mm.
   - Inner width (radial): 50 mm.
   - Height: 30 mm.
2. Revolve 360° → solid ring.
3. **Save as** `Lead_Filling.SLDPRT`.

### Part D — Assembly

1. **New Assembly** → insert `Socket_Pipe.SLDPRT` first (fixed).
2. Insert `Spigot_Pipe.SLDPRT` → apply mates:
   - **Concentric** between the spigot outer cylindrical face and the socket inner cylindrical face.
   - **Coincident** between the spigot end face and the **inner shoulder** of the socket (this defines the engagement depth).
3. Insert `Lead_Filling.SLDPRT` → apply mates:
   - **Concentric** between the lead's inner/outer cylindrical faces and the socket/spigot.
   - **Coincident** between the lead's front face and the socket's open face.
4. **Save as** `Socket_Spigot_Assembly.SLDASM`.

### Part E — Sectioned View & Drawing

1. Open the assembly → **Section View** → clipping plane passing through the **front plane** (cutting the assembly into two symmetrical halves).
2. Expose the lead filling and the annular space.
3. **Make Drawing from Assembly** → A3 landscape, scale 1:1 or 1:2.
4. Add **centre line**, **dimensions**, **part list (BOM)**, **title block**, and **section hatching** (cast iron: 45° cross-hatch; lead: 30° single hatch; hemp: zigzag).

---

## 4.2 Joint Anatomy — Labelled Block Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram is a *block-level functional map* showing each component of the joint and how they physically interface. (It replaces a hand-drawn cross-section that cannot be rendered in Mermaid.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pipe1[Pipe A - Spigot End]
    spigot[Spigot OD = 100 mm]
    hemp[Annular Hemp Gasket - L = 50 mm]
    lead[Annular Lead Filling - L = 30 mm]
    socketwall[Socket Wall - t = 14 mm]
    socketbody[Pipe B - Socket Body]

    pipe1 --> spigot
    spigot --> hemp
    hemp --> lead
    lead --> socketwall
    socketwall --> socketbody

    style spigot fill:#bbdefb,color:#000000
    style hemp fill:#fff9c4,color:#000000
    style lead fill:#cfd8dc,color:#000000
    style socketwall fill:#bbdefb,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram left-to-right:** Water flows inside Pipe A (spigot end) → passes the spigot outer surface → enters the annular space where it is blocked by **hemp** (first barrier) and then by **caulked lead** (second barrier) → finally passes through the **socket wall** into Pipe B. This is why the joint is *double-sealed*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define a *Socket and Spigot Joint*. State **any two** advantages of the joint.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A socket and spigot joint is a **detachable pipe joint** used for connecting cast iron (CI) pipes, formed by inserting the plain **spigot end** of one pipe into the enlarged **socket (bell) end** of the adjoining pipe and sealing the annular gap with **molten lead**, which is then caulked.

*Two advantages (1½ marks each):*
1. **Accommodates small angular deflections** (3°–5°) between adjacent pipe lengths, allowing the pipeline to follow gentle curves without special fittings.
2. **No machining of pipe ends is required** — the joint can be made in situ on site, even for buried pipelines, and the lead seal is self-tightening under internal pressure.

---

### Q2. What is the function of **hemp (yarn) packing** in a socket and spigot joint? Why is the lead **caulked** after pouring?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hemp packing (1.5 marks):** Hemp (or tarred jute yarn) is first packed into the back of the annular space to act as a **backing** that prevents the molten lead from flowing into the interior of the pipe. It also provides the **first line of defence** against leakage, since caulked lead alone, if it shrinks on cooling, could leave a path for water.

**Caulking of lead (1.5 marks):** Lead shrinks slightly on cooling, which would leave a clearance. **Caulking** is the process of hammering the lead with a blunt caulking tool so that it is **forcibly expanded laterally**, compressing tightly against both the spigot outer surface and the socket inner surface. This mechanical compaction makes the joint **leak-proof** and also increases its resistance to internal pressure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – University Question Paper Style, Dec 2024]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**(a)** Sketch the **half-sectional front view** of an unassembled **Socket Pipe** and a **Spigot Pipe** (DN 100, CI), label all features, and write the **three principal dimensions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, with neat sketches, the **step-by-step procedure** for assembling the socket and spigot joint in **SolidWorks** (or any equivalent parametric CAD package), clearly listing the **mates** to be applied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Sub-part | Description | Marks |
|---|---|:---:|
| Half-sectional view of **Socket Pipe** | Vertical centre line, OD = 100 mm, ID = 80 mm, Socket OD = 140 mm, Socket ID = 112 mm, Socket depth L = 80 mm; section hatching at 45° (CI) | **3** |
| Half-sectional view of **Spigot Pipe** | OD = 100 mm, ID = 80 mm, length 200 mm, plain (chamfered) end; section hatching at 45° (CI) | **2** |
| Three principal dimensions called out | D = 100, $D_{out,s}$ = 140, L = 80 (all in mm, with $\phi$ symbol) | **1** |
| Title block + standard symbols | Centre line, hidden lines (dashed), datum labels | **1** |

**Principal dimensions (for full marks):** $\phi 100$, $\phi 140$, **80 mm** socket depth.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Create the spigot pipe (1 mark):** New Part → Front Plane sketch → draw a closed right-half profile (outer 50 mm radius, inner 40 mm radius, height 200 mm) → Revolve Boss 360° → Fillet the corners → Save as `Spigot_Pipe`.

**Step 2 — Create the socket pipe (1 mark):** New Part → Front Plane sketch → draw closed right-half profile that is $\phi 100$ for the pipe portion, transitions to $\phi 140$ OD and $\phi 112$ ID for the socket portion of depth 80 mm → Revolve 360° → Save as `Socket_Pipe`.

**Step 3 — Start the assembly (1 mark):** File → New → Assembly → insert `Socket_Pipe` first as the **fixed** component. Insert `Spigot_Pipe` next.

**Step 4 — Apply mates (3 marks):**
1. **Concentric Mate** between the cylindrical **outer face of the spigot** and the **inner face of the socket**.
2. **Coincident Mate** between the **end face of the spigot** and the **inner shoulder of the socket** (this defines the engagement depth).
3. (Optional) **Coincident Mate** between the **axis** of the two pipes (often auto-satisfied by the above).

**Step 5 — Section view & drawing (1 mark):** Section clipping plane through the Front Plane → half-section view → generate A3 drawing → add dimensions, BOM, title block, hatching → save and plot.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**(a)** Draw a **fully dimensioned half-sectional assembly drawing** of the socket and spigot joint (with the lead filling shown) and prepare a **Bill of Materials (BOM)** for the assembly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **volume of lead** and the **mass of lead** required to make one socket and spigot joint for a DN 100 cast iron pipe, given the standard dimensions used in the KTU lab. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Sub-part | Description | Marks |
|---|---|:---:|
| Half-sectional assembly view | Both pipes shown, lead filling visible, joint axis horizontal, section hatching on cut material | **3** |
| All key dimensions | $\phi 100$, $\phi 112$, $\phi 140$, L = 80, $L_{Pb}$ = 30, $L_h$ = 50, lead clearance 6 mm | **2** |
| Lead filling shown distinctly | Different hatching (30° single hatch) from cast iron (45° cross-hatch) | **1** |
| Bill of Materials (BOM) | Table with item no., part name, qty (1), material, file name | **1** |

**Sample BOM:**

| Item | Part Name | Qty | Material | File Name |
|:---:|---|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Socket Pipe | 1 | Cast Iron (IS 1538) | `Socket_Pipe.SLDPRT` |
| 2 | Spigot Pipe | 1 | Cast Iron (IS 1538) | `Spigot_Pipe.SLDPRT` |
| 3 | Lead Filling | 1 | Lead (Pb) | `Lead_Filling.SLDPRT` |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given (1 mark):** $D = 100$ mm, $c = 6$ mm, $L_{Pb} = 30$ mm, $\rho_{Pb} = 11.34$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Socket inner diameter (1 mark):**
$$
D_{in,s} = D + 2c = 100 + 12 = 112 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean diameter of the lead ring (1 mark):**
$$
D_{mean} = \frac{D + D_{in,s}}{2} = \frac{100 + 112}{2} = 106 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of lead (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{Pb} &= \pi \cdot D_{mean} \cdot c \cdot L_{Pb} \\[4pt]
&= \pi \times 106 \times 6 \times 30 \\[4pt]
&= 59\,935.6 \text{ mm}^{3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 60 \text{ cm}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of lead (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Pb} &= V_{Pb} \times \rho_{Pb} \\[4pt]
&= 59\,935.6 \text{ mm}^{3} \times 11.34 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^{3} \\[4pt]
&\approx 679.7 \text{ g} \approx 0.68 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (final statement, free):** Approximately **60 cm³** (≈ **0.68 kg**) of lead is required per joint.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in the Socket and Spigot Joint experiment:**
>
> 1. **Wrong socket inner diameter (–1 mark):** Many students set $D_{in,s} = 140$ mm (i.e., use the *outer* diameter as the inner diameter of the socket). The correct value is **112 mm** (spigot OD + 2 × radial clearance).
> 2. **Missing the radial lead clearance (–1 mark):** Forgetting the 6 mm gap on each side. The joint *must* allow space for molten lead to flow in.
> 3. **Concentric mate is missing or replaced by a coincident mate (–2 marks):** Without concentric mate, the spigot and socket axes are not aligned and the assembly is **kinematically invalid**.
> 4. **Hatching is uniform across all parts (–1 mark):** Cast iron, lead, and hemp must be **hatched differently** (CI = 45° cross-hatch, Pb = 30° single hatch, hemp = zigzag). Identical hatching is considered a drafting error.
> 5. **No BOM / Part List (–1 mark):** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a BOM in the drawing. A drawing without a BOM loses one mark even if the geometry is perfect.
> 6. **Dimensions given without $\phi$ symbol (–½ mark):** All diameter dimensions must be prefixed with the symbol $\phi$ (e.g., $\phi 100$). Linear dimensions do not need a $\phi$.
> 7. **Confusing spigot with socket in the model tree (–½ mark):** Always name the parts clearly: `Socket_Pipe.SLDPRT` and `Spigot_Pipe.SLDPRT`. Do not use generic names like `Part1`, `Part2`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Socket and Spigot Joint (PCRUL307 – Module Experiment 2):**
>
> - The joint joins **two cast iron pipes** — one has a **socket (bell)** end, the other has a plain **spigot** end.
> - Standard lab dimensions: **D = 100 mm**, **t = 10 mm**, **$D_{out,s}$ = 140 mm**, **socket depth L = 80 mm**, **lead clearance c = 6 mm**, **lead depth ≈ 30 mm**, **hemp depth ≈ 50 mm**.
> - Socket inner diameter = **D + 2c = 112 mm**.
> - **Annular gap** is filled first with **hemp (yarn)**, then with **molten lead**, which is finally **caulked**.
> - For 3D modelling, create **two (or three) parts** — `Socket_Pipe`, `Spigot_Pipe`, and optionally `Lead_Filling`.
> - Use the **Revolve Boss/Base** feature with a **closed right-half profile** to model the rotationally symmetric parts.
> - In the assembly, apply **Concentric + Coincident mates** between the spigot and socket; a third Concentric Coincident pair for the lead filling.
> - Generate a **sectioned view** through the **front plane** to expose the lead filling internally.
> - Drawing sheet = **A3 landscape**, scale 1:1 or 1:2, with a **BOM**, **title block**, **centre lines**, and **hatching** (CI = 45° cross; Pb = 30° single; hemp = zigzag).
> - Lead volume per joint ≈ **60 cm³**, lead mass ≈ **0.68 kg**.
> - Advantages: **self-aligning**, **accommodates 3°–5° deflection**, **no threading or welding**, **suitable for buried pipelines**.
> - Disadvantages: **heavy** (lead), **labour-intensive caulking**, being replaced by **rubber-gasket push-on joints** in modern ductile iron pipes.
> - The joint is **semi-permanent** — it can be dismantled by re-heating the lead, unlike welded or flanged joints.
> - **Always dimension diameters with the $\phi$ symbol** — a top KTU valuation tip.
> - **Always name files properly** (`Socket_Pipe`, `Spigot_Pipe`) — generic `Part1`/`Part2` is penalised.
> - **Hatch each material differently** in the section view — a very common deduction area.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Assembly Models: Knuckle Joint
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly Models: Knuckle Joint

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Knuckle Joint** is a mechanical pin-type articulation specifically engineered to transmit axial (tensile or compressive) load between two coplanar rods while permitting a limited angular swinging motion in the plane perpendicular to the pin axis. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme PCRUL307, the knuckle joint is treated as the canonical *assembly-level solid modelling exercise* because it comprises heterogeneous primitives — a **Single Eye (terminal piece)**, a **Double Eye / Fork (intermediate piece)**, a **Knuckle Pin**, a **Pin Head**, and a **Collar / Split Pin** — all of which must be individually modelled, constrained, mated, and rendered as a single parametric assembly with correct standard proportions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):** *Knuckle joint is a type of pin-and-eye joint used to connect two rods subjected to tensile loading, allowing angular movement up to 90° in the plane of the joint. The pin is the critical shear element, and design proportions are standardized with respect to the rod diameter **d**.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **door hinge**, but instead of carrying the weight of the door vertically, the knuckle joint carries **horizontal pulling force** like the chain links of a bicycle. Imagine two flat steel rods; you cannot weld them directly because they need to flex. So you forge a circular eye at the end of each rod. The two eyes are then interlocked and a steel pin is hammered through them — much like threading a needle through two overlapping hoops. The pin takes the **shear load**, the eyes take the **tensile/crushing load**, and the joint can pivot just like your elbow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world applications of knuckle joints:**
> - Connecting links of **bicycle chains** (after derailleurs).
> - **Cotters** in cross-head slides of steam engines.
> - **Lever and bell-crank linkages** in textile machinery.
> - **Tie-rod ends** in structural bracing systems.
> - **Rocker-arm pivots** in low-speed mechanisms.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Rod Diameter (d):** Treated as the **base unit**; all component dimensions are multiples of *d*.
- **Typical d range:** **10 mm to 50 mm** (standard machine design practice).
- **Material (as per KTU lab specimens):** **Mild Steel (MS)** for eye, fork, and collar; **EN8 / EN9** for the knuckle pin to provide additional shear strength.
- **Allowable Shear Stress (τ):** **40 N/mm²** (MS pin).
- **Allowable Crushing Stress (σc):** **60 N/mm²** (MS eye/fork).
- **Angular Swing Range:** Up to **90°** in the working plane.

> [!TIP]
> Always memorize that **d** is the *only* independent variable. Every other dimension is derived as a *fixed multiple* of **d**. This single fact carries **2 to 3 marks** in every KTU lab viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component Inventory & Functional Logic

A knuckle joint assembly consists of **five distinct components**, each performing a dedicated role:

| # | Component | Function | Load Carried |
|---|-----------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Single Eye** | Forged at the end of one rod; has one circular boss with a hole | Tensile + Crushing |
| 2 | **Double Eye (Fork)** | Forged at the end of the second rod; has two parallel prongs (bosses) | Tensile + Crushing |
| 3 | **Knuckle Pin** | Cylindrical pin passing through both eye and fork holes | Shear + Bending |
| 4 | **Pin Head** | Hexagonal/round head on one end of the pin | Retains pin from one side |
| 5 | **Collar + Split Pin** | Slides onto the threaded other end of the pin | Retains pin from other side |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (How the Joint Works)

1. The **single eye** of rod-1 is inserted *between* the two prongs of the **double eye (fork)** of rod-2.
2. The concentric holes of all three pieces are aligned precisely along the **Z-axis** (assembly axis).
3. The **knuckle pin** is driven through the aligned holes from the open side of the fork.
4. The **pin head** rests against the outer face of one fork prong, preventing the pin from sliding out.
5. On the opposite (threaded) end of the pin, a **collar** is tightened and locked using a **split pin (cotter pin)** through a transverse hole.
6. Load is transferred from rod-1 → single eye → knuckle pin (in shear) → fork → rod-2.

### 2.3 Standard Proportions (KTU Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These proportions are mandated by the **KTU Machine Drawing Lab Manual** and must be reproduced *verbatim* in every answer paper, drawing sheet, and CAD dimension annotation.

| Symbol | Component Dimension | Standard Formula | Multiplier of *d* |
|--------|--------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **d** | Diameter of solid rod | Given | **1.00 d** |
| **d₁** | Inside (boss) diameter of single eye | $d_1 = 1.25\,d$ | **1.25 d** |
| **d₂** | Outside diameter of single eye | $d_2 = 1.5\,d$ | **1.50 d** |
| **d₃** | Diameter of knuckle pin | $d_3 = d$ | **1.00 d** |
| **d₄** | Inside diameter of fork (between prongs) | $d_4 = 1.25\,d$ | **1.25 d** |
| **d₅** | Outside diameter of fork prong | $d_5 = 2.2\,d$ | **2.20 d** |
| **d₆** | Diameter of pin head | $d_6 = 1.5\,d$ | **1.50 d** |
| **t** | Thickness of single eye | $t = 0.75\,d$ | **0.75 d** |
| **t₁** | Thickness of each fork prong | $t_1 = 0.75\,d$ | **0.75 d** |
| **t₂** | Total thickness of fork (both prongs + single eye + clearance) | $t_2 = 2t_1 + t + c$ | **≈ 2.25 d** |
| **c** | Clearance between eye and fork | $c = 1\,mm$ to **2 mm** | small |
| **l** | Length of knuckle pin | $l = 2t_1 + t + c + \text{margin}$ | ≈ **2.5 d** |

### 2.4 Strength Design Equations

The knuckle pin is the **weakest element** and must be designed against two failure modes:

**Step 1 — Pin in Double Shear (failure mode: shear yielding)**

The pin is sheared at **two cross-sections** because the fork prongs lie on either side of the single eye. Therefore the resisting area is:

$$A_{shear} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \, d_3^{\,2}$$

**Step 2 — Eye / Fork in Crushing (failure mode: bearing)**

The projected bearing area between the pin and the eye-hole is:

$$A_{crush} = d_3 \times t$$

**Step 3 — Design Equations (Equating Load to Strength)**

For **shear**:

$$\frac{P}{A_{shear}} \leq \tau \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P = \tau \times \frac{\pi}{4} d_3^{\,2} \times 2$$

For **crushing**:

$$\frac{P}{A_{crush}} \leq \sigma_c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P = \sigma_c \times d_3 \times t$$

Where:
- **P** = Axial load on the rod (N)
- **τ** = Allowable shear stress of pin material (N/mm²)
- **σc** = Allowable crushing stress (N/mm²)
- **d₃** = Diameter of knuckle pin = d (mm)
- **t** = Thickness of single eye = 0.75 d (mm)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> In modern practice, knuckle joints have largely been replaced by **universal joints (U-joints)** and **ball-and-socket joints** in high-speed/automotive applications. However, they remain **dominant in low-speed, heavy-load linkages** such as **rolling-mill screwdowns, valve-actuating linkages, hydraulic-cylinder rod ends, agricultural implement drawbars, and railway coupling rods** — because their geometry is simple, manufacturing cost is low, and they provide a **positive mechanical lock** with no slipping.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Dimensioning a Knuckle Joint

**Given Data:**
- Rod diameter, **d = 30 mm**
- Material of pin = Mild Steel (MS)
- Allowable shear stress, **τ = 40 N/mm²**
- Allowable crushing stress, **σc = 60 N/mm²**

**To Find:**
1. All component dimensions using standard proportions.
2. The safe axial tensile load **P** that the joint can transmit.

---

#### Step A — Derivation of Component Dimensions

Using the KTU standard proportion table from Section 2.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 30.00 \, \text{mm} \quad \text{(given, base unit)} \\
d_1 &= 1.25 \, d = 1.25 \times 30 = 37.50 \, \text{mm} \\
d_2 &= 1.50 \, d = 1.50 \times 30 = 45.00 \, \text{mm} \\
d_3 &= 1.00 \, d = 1.00 \times 30 = 30.00 \, \text{mm} \\
d_4 &= 1.25 \, d = 1.25 \times 30 = 37.50 \, \text{mm} \\
d_5 &= 2.20 \, d = 2.20 \times 30 = 66.00 \, \text{mm} \\
d_6 &= 1.50 \, d = 1.50 \times 30 = 45.00 \, \text{mm} \\
t &= 0.75 \, d = 0.75 \times 30 = 22.50 \, \text{mm} \\
t_1 &= 0.75 \, d = 0.75 \times 30 = 22.50 \, \text{mm} \\
t_2 &= 2 t_1 + t + c = 2(22.5) + 22.5 + 2.0 = 69.50 \, \text{mm} \\
l &= 2 t_1 + t + c + 4 \, \text{mm (margin)} = 2(22.5) + 22.5 + 2.0 + 4.0 = 73.50 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Every dimension above is a *direct linear multiple* of the rod diameter **d**.
- The **clearance c = 2 mm** is added only to compute total fork thickness and pin length, not to any diameter.

---

#### Step B — Derivation of Safe Load (Shear Criterion)

The pin is sheared in **double shear** (two cross-sections fail simultaneously).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{shear} &= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \, d_3^{\,2} \\
A_{shear} &= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (30)^{2} \\
A_{shear} &= 2 \times \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 900 \\
A_{shear} &= 2 \times 0.7854 \times 900 \\
A_{shear} &= 1413.72 \, \text{mm}^2 \\
P_{shear} &= \tau \times A_{shear} \\
P_{shear} &= 40 \times 1413.72 \\
P_{shear} &= 56548.67 \, \text{N} \\
P_{shear} &\approx 56.55 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Shear area is **doubled** because two planes fail together (double shear).
- The value **π/4** converts circular area from diameter.

---

#### Step C — Derivation of Safe Load (Crushing Criterion)

The pin crushes the eye material over the projected bearing area = pin diameter × eye thickness.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{crush} &= d_3 \times t \\
A_{crush} &= 30 \times 22.5 \\
A_{crush} &= 675.0 \, \text{mm}^2 \\
P_{crush} &= \sigma_c \times A_{crush} \\
P_{crush} &= 60 \times 675.0 \\
P_{crush} &= 40500.0 \, \text{N} \\
P_{crush} &= 40.50 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- Crushing area is the **rectangular projection** of the pin onto the eye wall (rectangle of width d₃ and height t).

---

#### Step D — Selection of Safe Load (Designer's Criterion)

**The lesser of the two values governs the design** (a joint is only as strong as its weakest failure mode).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{safe} &= \min(P_{shear}, \, P_{crush}) \\
P_{safe} &= \min(56.55, \, 40.50) \, \text{kN} \\
P_{safe} &= 40.50 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crushing is the governing failure mode here.** Always report the *minimum* of the two loads as the safe design load. Forgetting to take the **minimum** is the single most common KTU answer-sheet error.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Comparison

| Failure Mode | Resisting Element | Resisting Area Equation | Allowable Stress | Governing? |
|--------------|-------------------|--------------------------|------------------|-----------|
| Shear of pin | Knuckle pin (double shear) | $A_{shear} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_3^{\,2} \times 2$ | τ (40 N/mm²) | Usually NOT governing |
| Crushing of eye | Eye / fork projected area | $A_{crush} = d_3 \times t$ | σc (60 N/mm²) | **Usually GOVERNS** |
| Tension in rod | Rod cross-section | $A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^{2}$ | σt (120 N/mm²) | Rarely governs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Knuckle Joint Assembly

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **logical assembly hierarchy**, the **load flow path**, and the **CAD modelling workflow** in a single integrated view.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Base Solid: Rod Diameter d]:::root --> B1[Single Eye Component]
    A0 --> B2[Double Eye Fork Component]
    A0 --> B3[Knuckle Pin Component]
    A0 --> B4[Pin Head Component]
    A0 --> B5[Collar and Split Pin]

    B1 --> C1[Extruded Eye with d1 inner hole and d2 outer dia]
    B2 --> C2[Two Prongs each with d4 inner hole and d5 outer dia]
    B3 --> C3[Cylindrical Pin with diameter d3 and length l]
    B4 --> C4[Hex Head with d6 diameter and t_head thickness]
    B5 --> C5[Collar with ID d3 and split pin transverse hole]

    C1 --> D1[Assembly Stage 1: Insert Single Eye inside Fork Prongs]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Assembly Stage 2: Align All Three Holes on Common Axis Z]
    D2 --> D3[Assembly Stage 3: Insert Knuckle Pin through aligned holes]
    D3 --> D4[Assembly Stage 4: Lock with Pin Head on one side]
    D4 --> D5[Assembly Stage 5: Tighten Collar and Insert Split Pin on other side]

    D5 --> E1[Final Assembled Knuckle Joint with Standard Proportions]
    E1 --> F1[Load Path: Rod1 to Single Eye to Pin in Shear to Fork to Rod2]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 CAD Modelling Workflow (SolidWorks / Fusion 360 / CATIA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Sketch 2D Profile of Single Eye in Front Plane] --> S2[Step 2: Revolve or Extrude to form Solid Body]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply Fillet to internal boss edges]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Save Part as Single Eye part file]
    S5[Step 5: Sketch 2D Profile of Fork in Front Plane] --> S6[Step 6: Extrude Fork Prong with Mirror Feature]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply Fillet to fork edges and Save Fork part file]
    S8[Step 8: Sketch Circle for Knuckle Pin and Extrude] --> S9[Step 9: Add Pin Head by Revolve and Save Pin part file]
    S10[Step 10: Open Assembly File and Insert all 4 Parts] --> S11[Step 11: Apply Coincident and Concentric Mate constraints]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Apply Distance Mate for clearance c between eye and fork]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Validate Mates using Interference Detection Tool]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14: Apply Material Mild Steel MS to all parts]
    S14 --> S15[Step 15: Generate Section View and Dimensioned Drawing for KTU Record]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Representation of Load Transfer Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1((Axial Load P on Rod 1)) --> EYE1[Single Eye under Tension]
    EYE1 --> PIN[Knuckle Pin under Double Shear]
    PIN --> FORK[Double Eye Fork under Tension]
    FORK --> L2((Axial Load P transmitted to Rod 2))

    EYE1 -.Crushing Stress Sc.-> EYE1
    PIN -.Shear Stress T.-> PIN
    FORK -.Crushing Stress Sc.-> FORK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagram Instead of a Hand Sketch?**
> The KTU 2024 lab exam expects students to demonstrate **parametric assembly modelling** rather than freehand drafting. A block-level functional architecture flow provides:
> 1. A clear **assembly hierarchy** (single parts → sub-assembly → final assembly).
> 2. A **traceable load path** for engineering validation.
> 3. A **CAD workflow roadmap** that examiners can audit step-by-step.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a knuckle joint. List its main components with one-line functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **knuckle joint** is a pin-type mechanical joint used to connect two co-planar rods subjected to axial tensile or compressive load, while permitting limited angular motion in the plane perpendicular to the pin.

The main components and their functions are:

1. **Single Eye** — forged at the end of one rod; carries tensile load and provides the bearing seat for the pin.
