2. **Double Eye (Fork)** — forged at the end of the other rod with two prongs; sandwiches the single eye and transfers load via the pin.
3. **Knuckle Pin** — cylindrical pin that passes through the aligned holes of eye and fork; resists the load in **double shear**.
4. **Pin Head** — prevents the pin from sliding out of one side of the fork.
5. **Collar with Split Pin** — locks the pin from the opposite threaded end.

*(Full marks: 1 for definition + 2 for component list.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the standard proportions of the following knuckle joint components in terms of rod diameter d: (i) diameter of single eye outside, (ii) diameter of knuckle pin, (iii) thickness of fork prong.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Outside diameter of single eye:** $d_2 = 1.5\,d$
2. **Diameter of knuckle pin:** $d_3 = d$
3. **Thickness of each fork prong:** $t_1 = 0.75\,d$

*(Full marks: 1 mark each for the correct symbolic formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Sketch a neat, proportionate half-sectional front view of an assembled knuckle joint and label **at least 8 standard dimensions** using the standard proportions in terms of rod diameter **d**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A knuckle joint is to be designed for a rod of diameter **d = 40 mm**. The pin is made of mild steel with allowable shear stress **τ = 42 N/mm²** and allowable crushing stress **σc = 56 N/mm²**. Calculate **(i)** the diameter of the knuckle pin, **(ii)** the thickness of the single eye, and **(iii)** the safe axial tensile load the joint can carry. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sketch and Dimensioning (7 marks):**

The drawing must include:

- Half-sectional front view with the cutting plane passing through the pin axis.
- Standard proportions tabulated next to the sketch:
   - $d_2 = 1.5 d = 60$ mm (outside of single eye)
   - $d_1 = 1.25 d = 50$ mm (inside of single eye boss)
   - $d_3 = d = 40$ mm (pin diameter)
   - $d_5 = 2.2 d = 88$ mm (outside of fork prong)
   - $d_4 = 1.25 d = 50$ mm (inside of fork prong)
   - $d_6 = 1.5 d = 60$ mm (pin head diameter)
   - $t = 0.75 d = 30$ mm (single eye thickness)
   - $t_1 = 0.75 d = 30$ mm (each fork prong thickness)
   - $c = 2$ mm (clearance)
- Centre line, hidden lines for the un-sectioned half.
- Title block with name, roll number, scale (1:1 for lab drawing).

*(Mark split: 3 for correct proportions, 2 for sectional view convention, 2 for neatness and labelling.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**(i) Diameter of knuckle pin:**

By KTU standard, the pin diameter equals the rod diameter:

$$d_3 = d = 40 \, \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Thickness of single eye:**

By KTU standard proportion:

$$t = 0.75 \, d = 0.75 \times 40 = 30 \, \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Safe axial tensile load:**

*Step 1 — Resist in shear (double shear):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{shear} &= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times d_3^{\,2} \times \tau \\
P_{shear} &= 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (40)^2 \times 42 \\
P_{shear} &= 2 \times 0.7854 \times 1600 \times 42 \\
P_{shear} &= 105557.5 \, \text{N} \\
P_{shear} &\approx 105.56 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Resist in crushing:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{crush} &= d_3 \times t \times \sigma_c \\
P_{crush} &= 40 \times 30 \times 56 \\
P_{crush} &= 67200 \, \text{N} \\
P_{crush} &= 67.20 \, \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Safe load (minimum governs):*

$$P_{safe} = \min(105.56, \, 67.20) \, \text{kN} = 67.20 \, \text{kN}$$

*(Mark split: 1 for pin diameter, 1 for thickness, 3 for shear calculation, 1 for crushing calculation, 1 for correct minimum selection.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** List the **five main components** of a knuckle joint with a one-line functional note for each. Explain with a neat sketch how the **load is transferred** from one rod to the other through the knuckle pin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a CAD lab examination, a student has modelled the **Single Eye**, **Fork**, **Knuckle Pin**, **Pin Head**, and **Collar** as separate part files in SolidWorks. Describe the **step-by-step procedure** (minimum 6 steps) for assembling these parts into a functional knuckle joint assembly with proper mate constraints. Mention **any two standard mates** used and explain the **Interference Detection** tool. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Components and Load Transfer (7 marks):**

The five main components are:

1. **Single Eye** — provides a circular boss with one hole; carries tensile load from the parent rod.
2. **Double Eye (Fork)** — provides two parallel prongs with aligned holes; sandwiches the single eye.
3. **Knuckle Pin** — cylindrical pin passing through all three aligned holes; the load-transfer element.
4. **Pin Head** — a hexagonal or round head on one end of the pin; retains the pin from sliding out.
5. **Collar with Split Pin** — fitted on the opposite threaded end; locks the pin axially.

**Load Transfer Path:**
Rod-1 → Single Eye (under tension) → Knuckle Pin (under **double shear**) → Fork (under tension) → Rod-2.

The pin acts as the **shear-failure element** while the eye/fork act as the **bearing/crushing elements**.

*(Mark split: 2.5 for component list, 2 for sketch, 2.5 for load-path explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) — CAD Assembly Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step-by-step Assembly Procedure:**

1. **Create a new Assembly File** in SolidWorks (`.sldasm`) and insert the *Single Eye* part as the **first fixed component** (marked as *Origin*).
2. **Insert the Fork part** and apply a **Coincident Mate** between the inner face of the upper fork prong and the top face of the single eye.
3. Apply a **Concentric Mate** between the inner cylindrical face of the fork prong hole and the outer cylindrical face of the single eye boss — this aligns the holes along the Z-axis.
4. Repeat for the lower fork prong using **Coincident** + **Concentric** mates, sandwiching the single eye.
5. **Insert the Knuckle Pin** and apply a **Coincident Mate** between the pin's flat end face and the outer face of one fork prong, followed by a **Concentric Mate** between the pin's cylindrical surface and the fork hole.
6. **Insert the Pin Head** and align it with the pin end-face using **Coincident Mate**; mate the head's inner hole concentrically with the pin shank.
7. **Insert the Collar** on the opposite threaded end of the pin, align its inner face coincident with the fork's outer face, and lock it with a **Split Pin** modelled as a small extruded wedge.
8. **Validate** the assembly using **Tools → Evaluate → Interference Detection**.

**Two Standard Mates Used:**
- **Coincident Mate** — aligns flat faces to be coplanar.
- **Concentric Mate** — aligns cylindrical axes to be collinear.

**Interference Detection Tool:**
This tool highlights any region where two solid bodies occupy the same space (unacceptable overlap). It validates that mates have been applied correctly. A properly mated knuckle joint must show **zero interference** between the moving parts (eye and fork, except in the clearance region *c*).

*(Mark split: 3 for procedure, 2 for mates explanation, 2 for interference detection explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callouts:**
>
> 1. **Do NOT take the maximum of P_shear and P_crush.** Always take the **minimum** as the safe load. This is the single most frequent deduction, costing **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the factor of 2 in double shear.** Forgetting the "× 2" while calculating pin shear area is a common slip-up. Deduct **0.5 mark** for each occurrence.
> 3. **Do NOT use σt (tensile stress) in design calculations.** Knuckle pin never fails in pure tension — it fails in **shear** or **crushing**. Using σt is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 4. **In the CAD assembly, always mark the FIRST inserted component as "Fixed" (Origin).** Otherwise, every mate becomes relative to a moving reference and the assembly will drift or fail validation.
> 5. **Standard proportions are mandatory in sketches.** Using assumed/random dimensions instead of the 1.25 d, 1.5 d, 2.2 d multiples leads to **full proportion marks** being cut.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Knuckle joint** = pin-type joint for tensile load with limited angular swing.
- **Five components:** Single Eye, Fork (Double Eye), Knuckle Pin, Pin Head, Collar + Split Pin.
- **Base dimension** = rod diameter **d**; all other dimensions are fixed multiples of **d**.
- **Pin fails in double shear** — area formula includes a **× 2** factor.
- **Eye/fork fail in crushing** — area is **d₃ × t** (rectangular projection).
- **Safe load = minimum of P_shear and P_crush** — *never* the maximum.
- **Crushing usually governs** the design in mild-steel knuckle joints.
- **CAD assembly sequence:** insert → mate → validate → dimension → render.
- **Two essential mates:** *Coincident* (flat faces) and *Concentric* (cylindrical axes).
- **Always use Interference Detection** to validate the final assembly.
- **Sectional view must use correct cutting plane** through the pin axis for KTU lab records.
- **Material for lab models:** Mild Steel (MS) for eye/fork/collar; EN8 for pin.
- **Angular swing range:** up to **90°** in the joint plane.
- **Real-world applications:** bicycle chains, bell-crank linkages, hydraulic-cylinder rod ends, agricultural drawbars, railway couplings.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Assembly Models: Rigid Flange Couplings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Assembly Models: Rigid Flange Couplings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rigid Flange Coupling (KTU 2024 Terminology):** A *Rigid Flange Coupling* is a permanent, solid-type shaft coupling consisting of two separate cast-iron flanges, each keyed to the respective shaft ends and rigidly bolted together through a circular bolt pattern. It transmits torque without any relative motion between the connected shafts and is designed to handle both axial and torsional loads in perfectly aligned shaft systems.

The assembly is broadly classified into two KTU-prescribed categories:

| Type | Salient Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Unprotected Type** | Flange face is flush with shaft end; bolt heads/nuts project outward | Enclosed gearboxes, low-injury risk zones |
| **Protected Type** | Hub projects beyond flange face forming a recess for bolt heads | Open drives, exposed shaft lines, mining mills |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Handshake of Two Shafts"

Imagine joining two rotating rods by welding a flat circular plate (a *disc* or *flange*) to the end of each rod, and then fastening the two plates together with bolts. The moment one rod tries to spin, the friction-locked bolts instantly drag the second rod into perfect synchronization. The **flanges are the palms**, the **bolts are the gripping fingers**, and the **keyway is the interlock that prevents slipping**. This is, in essence, what a rigid flange coupling does — it is the *mechanical handshake* that rigidly binds two coaxial shafts.

## 1.3 Inventory of Components (The Assembly Tree)

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 3D modelling lab record, the assembly consists of **six distinct parts**, each modelled separately and then constrained in the assembly environment.

- **Flange (×2)** — cast-iron disc with integral hub
- **Shaft segments (×2)** — short reference lengths for mating
- **Key (×1 or ×2)** — sunk/square key for torque transmission
- **Bolt (×n)** — hexagonal head bolt, ISO metric
- **Nut (×n)** — hexagonal nut
- **Washer (×n or ×2n)** — plain washer under each bolt head and nut

> **Standard metric to memorize (bold):** **n = 3, 4, or 6** bolts depending on shaft diameter *d*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — "Why" and "How"

1. **Torque transfer pathway:** Shaft → Key → Flange hub → Flange face → Bolts → Second flange → Second key → Second shaft.
2. **Why a rigid type?** When shafts are *perfectly aligned* and misalignments are negligible (< 0.05 mm), a rigid coupling is the most efficient and compact choice. There is zero backlash, zero wear, and 100% torque efficiency.
3. **Why two flange styles?** The *protected* type provides safety by recessing the bolt heads within the hub projection, while the *unprotected* type is cheaper and easier to machine/disassemble.
4. **Why a key is mandatory?** The bolt clamping force alone cannot transmit tangential torque; the key provides positive mechanical interlock (form-closure) between hub and shaft.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The KTU 2024 machine drawing lab expects empirical proportions in terms of the **shaft diameter, d**. All dimensions are linear in *d* — the examiner typically supplies *d* and the student derives all other dimensions.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Empirical Proportion | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft diameter (given) | $d$ | — | mm |
| 2 | Outside diameter of flange | $D$ | $4d$ (unprotected) / $2.5d$ (protected) | mm |
| 3 | Hub outer diameter | $D_1$ | $2d$ | mm |
| 4 | Hub length | $L$ | $1.5d$ | mm |
| 5 | Pitch (bolt) circle diameter | $D_2$ | $3d$ | mm |
| 6 | Flange thickness | $t_f$ | $0.5d$ | mm |
| 7 | Number of bolts | $n$ | $3 \;\vert\; 4 \;\vert\; 6$ | — |
| 8 | Bolt diameter | $d_b$ | $0.5d$ (use nearest ISO M-size) | mm |
| 9 | Key width | $b$ | $d/4$ | mm |
| 10 | Key height | $h$ | $d/4$ (then $h/2$ sunk in shaft) | mm |
| 11 | Key length | $L_k$ | $1.5d$ | mm |
| 12 | Chamfer on flange | $c$ | $0.05d$ to $0.1d$ | mm |
| 13 | PCD for protected recess | $D_3$ | $D_2 - 2 d_b$ | mm |
| 14 | Recess depth (protected) | $L_r$ | $0.5 d_b$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trick:** The *number of bolts, n*, is selected based on *d*:
> - $d \le 25\;\text{mm} \Rightarrow n = 3$
> - $25 < d \le 50\;\text{mm} \Rightarrow n = 4$
> - $d > 50\;\text{mm} \Rightarrow n = 6$
> Round $d_b$ **up** to the next standard ISO metric size (M8, M10, M12, M16, M20, M24…).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial gearbox input/output shafts** in steel plants and cement mills
- **Pump and compressor drives** in power-generation auxiliaries
- **Marine propulsion shafting** segments
- **Test rig shafts** in mechanical engineering laboratories
- **CNC machine spindle–motor coupling** where exact alignment is guaranteed by precision bearings

> The 3D assembly model created in this lab experiment is the **digital twin** used for FEA, BOM generation, and CNC toolpath planning in the actual production cycle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Design Problem (Exhaustive Derivation)

> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]:**
> Design a *protected-type* rigid flange coupling to connect two shafts of diameter **d = 40 mm**, transmitting a torque of **T = 250 N·m**. Determine all the principal dimensions required to model the assembly in 3D CAD software.

### 3.1.1 Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= 40 \;\text{mm} \\
T &= 250 \;\text{N·m} = 250 \times 10^3 \;\text{N·mm} \\
\text{Type} &= \text{Protected (recessed bolts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Flange Outside Diameter (D)

For protected type:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 2.5 \times d \\
D &= 2.5 \times 40 \\
D &= 100 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Hub Outer Diameter (D₁)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 2 \times d \\
D_1 &= 2 \times 40 \\
D_1 &= 80 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Hub Length (L)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1.5 \times d \\
L &= 1.5 \times 40 \\
L &= 60 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Pitch Circle Diameter for Bolts (D₂)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= 3 \times d \\
D_2 &= 3 \times 40 \\
D_2 &= 120 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Number and Size of Bolts

Since $d = 40$ mm lies in the $25 < d \le 50$ band:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 4 \;\text{bolts} \\
d_b &= 0.5 \times d = 0.5 \times 40 = 20 \;\text{mm} \Rightarrow \text{adopt ISO M20}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Standard IS/ISO metric coarse sizes are M6, M8, M10, M12, M16, M20, M24, M30… 20 mm is a *direct* ISO size, so M20 is selected.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Flange Thickness (t_f)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= 0.5 \times d \\
t_f &= 0.5 \times 40 \\
t_f &= 20 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Key Dimensions

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{d}{4} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \;\text{mm} \\
h &= \frac{d}{4} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \;\text{mm (full key height)} \\
L_k &= 1.5 \times d = 1.5 \times 40 = 60 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Sunk in shaft by $h/2 = 5$ mm; hence hub keyway depth = $h/2 = 5$ mm.

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Recess Dimensions (Protected Type)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_3 &= D_2 - 2 d_b = 120 - 2(20) = 80 \;\text{mm} \\
L_r &= 0.5 d_b = 0.5 \times 20 = 10 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.10 Step 9 — Chamfer (c)

$$
\begin{aligned}
c &= 0.05 \times d = 0.05 \times 40 = 2 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.11 Final Tabulated Result

| Component | Dimension | Value (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft diameter | $d$ | **40** |
| Flange OD | $D$ | **100** |
| Hub OD | $D_1$ | **80** |
| Hub length | $L$ | **60** |
| Bolt PCD | $D_2$ | **120** |
| Bolt size | M20 | $\times$ **4 nos** |
| Flange thickness | $t_f$ | **20** |
| Key (b × h × Lₖ) | — | **10 × 10 × 60** |
| Recess dia | $D_3$ | **80** |
| Recess depth | $L_r$ | **10** |
| Chamfer | $c$ | **2** |

### 3.1.12 Verification — Torque Capacity of Bolts (Why it Works)

Tangential force at bolt pitch circle per bolt:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \frac{2T}{n \cdot D_2} \\
F_b &= \frac{2 \times 250 \times 10^3}{4 \times 120} \\
F_b &= \frac{500\,000}{480} \\
F_b &\approx 1041.7 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Bolt core area (M20, core dia ≈ 17.29 mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_c &= \frac{\pi}{4} d_c^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(17.29)^2 \approx 234.8 \;\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Tensile stress in bolt (assuming preload dominates):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_t &= \frac{F_b}{A_c} = \frac{1041.7}{234.8} \approx 4.44 \;\text{N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma_t \ll \sigma_{allowable}$ of grade 4.6 steel ($\approx 240$ N/mm²), the **M20 × 4 design is safe** and dimensionally conservative.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Modelling-to-Formula Cross-Reference

| CAD Feature | Linked Formula | Sketch Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Revolved boss/extrude (flange disc) | $D,\; t_f$ | Disc profile |
| Revolved hub on flange | $D_1,\; L$ | Hub |
| Counterbore / pocket (protected) | $D_3,\; L_r$ | Bolt-head recess |
| Extruded cut (shaft) | $d$ | Through-bore |
| Slot/extrude cut (keyway) | $b,\;h$ | Sunk keyway |
| Hex boss (bolt head) | $d_b$ | Bolt modelling |
| Circular pattern (bolts) | $n,\; D_2$ | Bolt placement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Assembly Build Sequence

> The following block-diagram documents the **3D modelling workflow** expected in the KTU 2024 lab record. The student must follow this exact logical chain during the CAD demonstration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive shaft diameter d] --> B[Compute all empirical dimensions from formulas]
    B --> C[Step 1: Model Flange Part 1]
    C --> C1[Sketch flange disc profile: D, t_f]
    C1 --> C2[Add hub: D1, L]
    C2 --> C3[Add keyway slot: b, h/2]
    C3 --> C4[Add bolt holes on PCD: D2, n, d_b]
    C4 --> C5[Add protected recess: D3, L_r if applicable]
    C5 --> D[Step 2: Model Flange Part 2 - identical to Part 1]
    D --> E[Step 3: Model Shaft Segments - diameter d, length L_s]
    E --> F[Step 4: Model Key - b x h x L_k]
    F --> G[Step 5: Model Bolts and Nuts - M-size d_b]
    G --> H[Step 6: Model Washers - standard ISO]
    H --> I[Step 7: Open Assembly File]
    I --> J[Insert Shaft 1]
    J --> K[Insert Flange 1 - coincident face with shaft end]
    K --> L[Insert Key - mate to keyway slot]
    L --> M[Insert Shaft 2 - coaxial to shaft 1]
    M --> N[Insert Flange 2 - mirror orientation]
    N --> O[Insert 4 Bolts with washers and nuts]
    O --> P[Circular pattern bolts around PCD]
    P --> Q[Apply standard mates: coincident, concentric, distance]
    Q --> R[Step 8: Generate Drawing Views]
    R --> S[Front view, Sectioned view, BOM]
    S --> T[End: Save Assembly .SLDASM / .STEP]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic Logic (ASCII Block Representation)

```
                FRONT VIEW (SECTIONED)
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  ◯  ◯  ◯  ◯  ← n Bolt Holes on PCD D2     │
   │   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐             │
   │   │  ┌────┐  │    │  ┌────┐  │             │
   │   │  │KEY │  │    │  │KEY │  │             │
   │   │  │    │  │    │  │    │  │             │
   │   │  └────┘  │    │  └────┘  │  ← Hub D1   │
   │   │  SHAFT d │    │ SHAFT d  │             │
   │   │  ◯  ◯  ◯ │    │ ◯  ◯  ◯ │             │
   │   │   ┌──┐   │    │   ┌──┐   │             │
   │   │   │D3│   │    │   │D3│   │ ← Recess    │
   │   │   │  │   │    │   │  │   │             │
   │   │   └──┘   │    │   └──┘   │             │
   │   │   ↕ L_r   │    │  ↕ L_r   │             │
   │   │ Flange1   │    │ Flange2  │             │
   │   │    D=100  │    │  D=100   │             │
   │   │  tf=20    │    │ tf=20    │             │
   │   │     [BOLTED JOINT — n×M20]            │
   └────────────────────────────────────────────┘
              |←—— L =60 ——→|←—— L =60 ——→|
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Proportion recall anchors for the sketch:** $D : D_1 : d = 2.5 : 2 : 1$ (protected type) and $D_2 \approx 3d$. These are the four golden ratios the examiner will look for in the sectioned view.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> Differentiate between *protected* and *unprotected* rigid flange couplings with a neat sketch.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Unprotected | Protected |
|---|---|---|
| Flange outside dia, D | $4d$ | $2.5d$ |
| Hub projection | Flush with shaft end | Projects beyond flange face |
| Bolt heads | Exposed, project outward | Recessed inside hub projection |
| Operator safety | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use | Enclosed gearboxes | Open industrial drives |

Sketch: Show two flanges — unprotected has bolt heads protruding; protected has a circular recess of diameter $D_3$ and depth $L_r$ accommodating the hex head. **(3 marks: 1 for table/differences, 1 for dimension rule, 1 for sketch)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> List the standard empirical proportions used to design a rigid flange coupling in terms of shaft diameter *d*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Flange OD: $D = 4d$ (unprotected) / $2.5d$ (protected) **(1 mark)**
- Hub OD: $D_1 = 2d$, Hub length: $L = 1.5d$ **(1 mark)**
- Bolt PCD: $D_2 = 3d$, Bolt dia: $d_b = 0.5d$, n = 3/4/6, Flange thickness: $t_f = 0.5d$, Key: $b = h = d/4$, $L_k = 1.5d$ **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Design a *protected-type* rigid flange coupling for two shafts of diameter **d = 50 mm** transmitting torque **T = 500 N·m**. List all principal dimensions with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the step-by-step procedure to create the 3D assembly of the above coupling in any parametric CAD software, with all assembly constraints used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1:** Given $d = 50$ mm, $T = 500$ N·m, Protected type.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 2.5d = 2.5 \times 50 = 125 \;\text{mm} \\
D_1 &= 2d = 100 \;\text{mm} \\
L &= 1.5d = 75 \;\text{mm} \\
D_2 &= 3d = 150 \;\text{mm} \\
n &= 4 \;\text{(since } 25 < d \le 50 \text{)} \\
d_b &= 0.5d = 25 \;\text{mm} \Rightarrow \text{adopt ISO M24} \\
t_f &= 0.5d = 25 \;\text{mm} \\
b &= d/4 = 12.5 \Rightarrow \text{use standard 14 mm} \\
h &= d/4 = 12.5 \Rightarrow \text{use standard 9 mm (sunk key, IS 2292)} \\
L_k &= 1.5d = 75 \;\text{mm} \\
D_3 &= D_2 - 2d_b = 150 - 48 = 102 \;\text{mm} \\
L_r &= 0.5 d_b = 12 \;\text{mm} \\
c &= 0.05d \approx 2.5 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sketch:** Front view with section showing the recessed bolt heads within the hub projection. **(7 marks: 5 for calculations, 2 for labelled sketch)**

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Step | Operation | Feature/CAD Command | Constraint Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create Flange part | Revolved boss from sketch | Dimensions $D, D_1, L, t_f$ |
| 2 | Add bolt holes | Extrude cut (circular) | PCD $D_2$, dia $d_b$ |
| 3 | Add protected recess | Extrude cut (circular) | $D_3, L_r$ |
| 4 | Add keyway slot | Extrude cut (rectangular) | $b, h/2$ |
| 5 | Model shaft, key, bolt, nut, washer | Separate part files | ISO standards |
| 6 | Open Assembly | Insert all parts | — |
| 7 | Mate flange to shaft | Concentric on $d$ + coincident on face | Concentric, Coincident |
| 8 | Position second shaft/flange | Coaxial alignment | Coincident faces |
| 9 | Place bolts | Concentric to holes + distance along axis | Concentric, Distance |
| 10 | Circular pattern bolts | Around PCD | Pattern with n=4 |
| 11 | Verify degrees of freedom = 0 | Drag check | All rigid joints fixed |
| 12 | Generate drawing views | Front, Section, BOM | Standard projections |

**(7 marks: 3 for component modelling, 3 for assembly mates, 1 for drawing output)**

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Design an *unprotected-type* rigid flange coupling for shafts of diameter **d = 60 mm**, transmitting **T = 800 N·m**. Provide a free-body diagram showing the tangential force on bolts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between rigid and flexible couplings. Why is a flexible coupling *not* recommended in the present application? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 4d = 4 \times 60 = 240 \;\text{mm} \\
D_1 &= 2d = 120 \;\text{mm} \\
L &= 1.5d = 90 \;\text{mm} \\
D_2 &= 3d = 180 \;\text{mm} \\
n &= 6 \;\text{(since } d > 50 \text{)} \\
d_b &= 0.5d = 30 \;\text{mm} \Rightarrow \text{adopt ISO M30} \\
t_f &= 0.5d = 30 \;\text{mm} \\
b &= 14 \;\text{mm (IS 2292 standard)} \\
h &= 9 \;\text{mm} \\
L_k &= 1.5d = 90 \;\text{mm} \\
c &= 3 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Tangential force per bolt:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= \frac{2T}{n \cdot D_2} = \frac{2 \times 800 \times 10^3}{6 \times 180} = \frac{1\,600\,000}{1080} \approx 1481.5 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

FBD: Show torque $T$ acting on shaft 1, transmitted through key to flange hub, then distributed as tangential shear $F_b$ on six bolts on the PCD of diameter $D_2 = 180$ mm. **(7 marks: 4 for dimensions, 2 for force calculation, 1 for FBD)**

#### Model Solution — (b)

| Aspect | Rigid Coupling | Flexible Coupling |
|---|---|---|
| Misalignment tolerance | Zero (requires perfect alignment) | Handles parallel, angular, axial misalignment |
| Components | Flanges, bolts, key | Buna-N spider / gear mesh / disc pack |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Torque efficiency | 100% | 95–98% |
| Application | Gearbox–motor, perfectly aligned shafts | Motor–pump long drives, shaft misalignment |
| Maintenance | None (permanent) | Periodic elastomer/gear replacement |

> **Reason for not using flexible coupling here:** The problem statement indicates *perfectly aligned* shafts (typical of gearbox output). A flexible coupling would add unnecessary cost, introduce damping losses, and require periodic spider/gear replacement. Hence the rigid flange coupling is the optimal engineering choice. **(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 3 for justification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Dimension D confusion:** Students frequently use $4d$ for *both* types. Remember: $4d$ is **only for unprotected**; protected uses $2.5d$. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Bolt PCD:** Using $D_2 = 2d$ or $D_2 = 4d$ instead of the empirical $3d$. **(-1 mark)**
> 3. **Key standard size:** Calculating $b = d/4 = 12.5$ mm but failing to round to the **next standard IS 2292 size (14 mm)**. **(-0.5 mark)**
> 4. **In assembly:** Forgetting the *concentric* mate between bolt shank and the hole — using only coincident faces. **(-0.5 mark)**
> 5. **Drawing views:** Submitting only the assembly view, omitting the **half-sectional front view** (mandatory in KTU lab records). **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Units:** Writing torque in N·m when substituting into a $D_2$ (in mm) formula — forgetting to convert to N·mm. **(-1 mark)**
> 7. **BOM:** Missing standard ISO designation for bolts (e.g., "Hex Bolt M20 × 60 ISO 4014") in the Bill of Materials. **(-0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> **Things to remember at a glance:**

- ✅ **Rigid Flange Coupling = 2 flanges + bolts + key**; permanent, zero misalignment tolerance, 100% torque efficiency.
- ✅ **Two types:** *Unprotected* ($D = 4d$, bolts exposed) and *Protected* ($D = 2.5d$, bolts recessed for safety).
- ✅ **Golden empirical ratios** in terms of shaft diameter $d$:
  - $D = 2.5d \;\vert\; 4d$
  - $D_1 = 2d$, $L = 1.5d$, $D_2 = 3d$, $t_f = 0.5d$, $d_b = 0.5d$
  - Key: $b = h = d/4$ (round to IS 2292 standard), $L_k = 1.5d$
  - Number of bolts $n = 3$ ($d \le 25$); $n = 4$ ($25 < d \le 50$); $n = 6$ ($d > 50$)
- ✅ **Recess for protected type:** $D_3 = D_2 - 2d_b$, $L_r = 0.5 d_b$.
- ✅ **CAD modelling sequence:** Model all 6 parts separately → assemble with *concentric*, *coincident*, and *distance* mates → circular pattern for bolts → generate half-sectioned front view with BOM.
- ✅ **Conversion discipline:** Torque must be in N·mm before using $D_2$ (in mm) in the bolt force equation.
- ✅ **Standard ISO designations to memorize:** Hex Bolt ISO 4014, Hex Nut ISO 4032, Plain Washer ISO 7089.
- ✅ **Why not flexible coupling?** When shafts are guaranteed aligned, rigid is cheaper, more efficient, and zero-maintenance.
- ✅ **Verification formula:** $F_b = 2T / (n \cdot D_2) \le F_{b,allowable}$ for design validation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to FEA Software (ANSYS, Abaqus) for Structural Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRUL307 - MACHINE DRAWING AND SOLID MODELLING LABORATORY > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to FEA Software (ANSYS, Abaqus) for Structural Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Finite Element Analysis (FEA) **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** is a numerical simulation technique used in engineering to obtain approximate solutions to boundary value problems involving complex geometries, loading, and material behaviour. It works by subdividing a large, complex problem domain (a structural component, thermal system, or fluid domain) into a finite number of smaller, simpler, and well-defined sub-regions called **finite elements**. The mathematical behaviour of each element is described by a set of algebraic equations derived from the governing physical laws (e.g., equilibrium equations from solid mechanics). These element-level equations are systematically assembled into a global system of linear or non-linear algebraic equations, which is then solved numerically to predict the response of the entire structure. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FEA is a *computational* technique, not an exact analytical method. The accuracy of the result depends heavily on the **mesh density**, the **element type chosen**, and the proper application of **boundary conditions**. ## 1.2 Introduction to ANSYS and Abaqus | Software | Developed By | Primary Use Case | Solver Type | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **ANSYS Workbench / Mechanical** | ANSYS Inc., USA | Multi-physics (Structural, Thermal, CFD, Electromagnetic) | Implicit & Explicit | | **Abaqus / Abaqus CAE** | Dassault Systèmes (originally HKS) | Advanced non-linear structural & multi-physics simulation | Implicit (Standard) & Explicit | ### 1.2.1 ANSYS **ANSYS** is a general-purpose, commercially dominant Finite Element Analysis (FEA) software package. It is widely adopted in the aerospace, automotive, biomedical, and energy industries. The modern interface is the **ANSYS Workbench** platform, which allows seamless project schematic flow from CAD geometry (via SpaceClaim or imported files) to meshing, setup, solution, and post-processing. ### 1.2.2 Abaqus **Abaqus** is renowned for handling **complex non-linear problems** (large deformation, hyper-elastic materials, contact mechanics, composites). It is split into two main solvers: * **Abaqus/Standard**: An implicit solver best for static, low-speed dynamic, and steady-state problems. * **Abaqus/Explicit**: An explicit solver used for high-speed dynamics (crash, impact, drop test) and highly non-linear quasi-static events. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Lego Block" Approach** > Imagine you are asked to calculate the **weight of a complexly shaped stone statue**, but the statue is too irregular to use a simple volume × density formula. What do you do? > > You *break the statue into hundreds of small, manageable Lego-like blocks* (rectangular bricks, cylinders, cubes). You estimate the volume and weight of each tiny block individually, and then you **add them all together** to get the total weight. > > **FEA works exactly the same way**: > * The **statue** = your 3D mechanical component (e.g., a crankshaft). > * The **Lego blocks** = *finite elements* (tetrahedra, hexahedra, shells). > * The **weight of each block** = the stiffness + stress + strain relationship governed by physics. > * The **addition process** = assembling the **Global Stiffness Matrix** $[K]$ and solving $[K]\{u\} = \{F\}$. The more (and smaller) blocks you use, the more accurate your total weight calculation becomes. This is precisely why **mesh refinement** is critical in any FEA study. ## 1.4 Why FEA is Indispensable in Modern Engineering * **Cost Reduction:** Replaces dozens of physical prototypes with digital prototypes. * **Safety:** Predicts failure points and stress concentrations before manufacturing. * **Optimization:** Allows engineers to test hundreds of design iterations rapidly to find the lightest, strongest, and cheapest design. * **Visualization of Invisible Physics:** Stress, strain, temperature gradient, and fluid velocity fields can be visualized as color contours, which cannot be measured directly with sensors inside solid materials. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of FEA (The Universal Workflow) Every FEA software (ANSYS, Abaqus, COMSOL, NASTRAN) executes the simulation in three fundamental stages. This is a **guaranteed KTU theory question**. ### Pillar 1: Pre-Processing This is where the engineer builds the model. The quality of the input dictates the quality of the output. 1. **Geometry Creation / Import:** Build the CAD model inside the software (ANSYS SpaceClaim, Abaqus CAE Part module) or import a neutral file (STEP, IGES, Parasolid, STL). 2. **Material Property Assignment:** Define the constitutive behaviour. * Isotropic Linear Elastic (Steel, Aluminium) — needs Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. * Plastic, Hyper-elastic, Visco-elastic, Composite, Orthotropic. 3. **Element / Section Assignment:** Choose the element formulation (1D beam, 2D shell, 3D solid). 4. **Meshing:** Discretize the geometry into elements. The smaller the element, the higher the accuracy, but the greater the computational cost. 5. **Boundary Conditions & Loading:** * *Displacement Boundary Conditions (DBC)*: Fixing a face ($u = 0$, $v = 0$, $w = 0$). * *Force / Pressure / Thermal / Moment Loads.* ### Pillar 2: Solution (The Solver) The solver numerically solves the discretized equations. It assembles the global stiffness matrix and applies the boundary conditions to solve for the nodal displacements. * *Implicit Solver* (ANSYS default, Abaqus/Standard): Solves a large system of equations $[K]\{u\} = \{F\}$. Excellent for static and low-frequency dynamics. Unconditionally stable but computationally expensive per time step. * *Explicit Solver* (Abaqus/Explicit, ANSYS LS-DYNA): Does not solve a global matrix; it propagates the solution from node to node using the central difference method. Excellent for crash, impact, and highly non-linear events. Conditionally stable (requires a very small time step). ### Pillar 3: Post-Processing This is where the engineer interprets the result. * **Deformation plots:** Visualizing the displaced shape. * **Contour plots:** Stress ($\sigma_{vonMises}$), Strain ($\varepsilon$), Temperature ($T$), Displacement magnitude ($U$). * **Path plots and Probes:** Extracting precise data values along a line or at a specific node. * **Animation:** Time-history playback of transient simulations. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility of FEA in Industry** > In the automotive industry, FEA is used to simulate a **frontal crash** of a car hitting a rigid wall at 56 km/h. FEA predicts the crumple zone behaviour, passenger compartment intrusion, and airbag deployment timing. In aerospace, it predicts the **flutter** of an aircraft wing, and in civil engineering, it predicts the **buckling** of a column in a high-rise building. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MANDATORY Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exam (PCRUL307)** | Symbol / Equation | Definition | Standard Unit (SI) | Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (1D Linear Elastic) | $\text{Pa} = \text{N/m}^2$ | Stress-strain in a bar | | $\{F\} = [K] \cdot \{u\}$ | Global Stiffness Equation | $\text{N}$, $\text{N/m}$, $\text{m}$ | Core FEA governing equation | | $[K_e] = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$ | Element Stiffness Matrix | $\text{N/m}$ | Volumetric integration of an element | | $\sigma_{vonMises} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | Von-Mises Yield Criterion | $\text{Pa}$ | Ductile material failure check | | $\sigma_{principal} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Principal Stresses (2D) | $\text{Pa}$ | Max/Min normal stress on a plane | | $FOS = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{vonMises}}$ | Factor of Safety | Dimensionless | Design verification | | $P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{(KL)^2}$ | Euler's Buckling Load | $\text{N}$ | Slender column stability | | $\Delta t_{crit} = \frac{L_{min}}{c}$ | Critical Time Step (Explicit) | $\text{seconds}$ | Abaqus Explicit stability limit | | $c = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$ | Wave Speed in Material | $\text{m/s}$ | Used in explicit dynamics | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Marking Scheme Reminder:** When writing formulas in the exam, always define the symbols first. Writing bare equations without units and definitions will result in a **0.5 to 1 mark penalty** in the valuation key. ## 2.3 Constitutive Behaviour (The "D" Matrix) For a 3D linear elastic isotropic material, the stress-strain relationship (used in FEA to build the $[D]$ matrix) is: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} \\ \sigma_{yy} \\ \sigma_{zz} \\ \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} \\ \varepsilon_{yy} \\ \varepsilon_{zz} \\ \gamma_{xy} \\ \gamma_{yz} \\ \gamma_{zx} \end{bmatrix} $$ This $[D]$ matrix is what makes ANSYS and Abaqus capable of distinguishing between Steel (E = 200 GPa, $\nu$ = 0.3) and Aluminium (E = 70 GPa, $\nu$ = 0.33). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Stiffness Matrix for a 1D Linear Spring Element This is a **high-priority KTU derivation**. Even though modern software does this internally, board examiners frequently ask students to derive or explain it to test conceptual depth. ### Step 1: Define the Element Geometry Consider a 1D linear spring (truss/bar element) of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's Modulus $E$. It has two nodes: **Node 1** (left, at $x = 0$) and **Node 2** (right, at $x = L$). > The Degrees of Freedom (DOF) are the axial displacements at each node: $u_1$ and $u_2$. ### Step 2: Assume a Displacement Field Since the element is a straight bar, we assume a **linear variation of displacement** between the two nodes: $$ u(x) = N_1(x) u_1 + N_2(x) u_2 $$ where $N_1$ and $N_2$ are the **shape functions** (also called interpolation functions). ### Step 3: Determine the Shape Functions Apply the boundary conditions: * At $x = 0$: $u(0) = u_1 \Rightarrow N_1(0) = 1$ and $N_2(0) = 0$. * At $x = L$: $u(L) = u_2 \Rightarrow N_1(L) = 0$ and $N_2(L) = 1$. Assuming $N_1$ and $N_2$ are linear in $x$: $$ N_1(x) = 1 - \frac{x}{L} $$ $$ N_2(x) = \frac{x}{L} $$ ### Step 4: Calculate the Strain (B Matrix) Axial strain is the derivative of axial displacement with respect to $x$: $$ \varepsilon(x) = \frac{du}{dx} = \frac{dN_1}{dx} u_1 + \frac{dN_2}{dx} u_2 $$ Evaluating the derivatives: $$ \frac{dN_1}{dx} = -\frac{1}{L}, \quad \frac{dN_2}{dx} = \frac{1}{L} $$ Therefore, the strain-displacement matrix $[B]$ is: $$ [B] = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 5: Apply Hooke's Law to get Stress (D Matrix) For a 1D bar, the constitutive matrix is simply $[D] = E$. So: $$ \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = E \cdot [B] \cdot \begin{Bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{Bmatrix} $$ ### Step 6: Use the Principle of Minimum Potential Energy to Derive $[K]$ The total potential energy $\Pi$ of the element is the sum of the **strain energy** $U$ and the **work done by external forces** $W$: $$ \Pi = U - W $$ The strain energy stored in the element due to axial loading is: $$ U = \frac{1}{2} \int_{V} \sigma^T \varepsilon \, dV $$ Substituting $\sigma = [D][B]\{u\}$ and $\varepsilon = [B]\{u\}$: $$ U = \frac{1}{2} \{u\}^T \left( \int_{V} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV \right) \{u\} $$ The term in the parenthesis is, by definition, the **Element Stiffness Matrix** $[K_e]$: $$ [K_e] = \int_{V_e} [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV $$ To find the equilibrium state, we minimize $\Pi$ with respect to the nodal displacements: $$ \frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial \{u\}} = \{0\} $$ This yields the stiffness equation: $$ [K_e] \{u\} = \{F_e\} $$ ### Step 7: Evaluate the Integral for a 1D Bar For a 1D bar, $dV = A \, dx$. The integration limits are from $x = 0$ to $x = L$. The matrices $[B]$, $[D]$, and area $A$ are constants, so they can be factored out: $$ [K_e] = \int_{0}^{L} \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{1}{L} \\ \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \cdot E \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{1}{L} & \frac{1}{L} \end{bmatrix} \cdot A \, dx $$ $$ [K_e] = \frac{EA}{L^2} \int_{0}^{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} dx $$ Since the matrix is constant, the integral is just the length $L$: $$ [K_e] = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ This $2 \times 2$ matrix is the **final element stiffness matrix** for a 1D linear spring/truss element. The diagonal terms ($K_{11}$ and $K_{22}$) represent the stiffness at each node, and the off-diagonal terms ($K_{12}$ and $K_{21}$) represent the coupling between the two nodes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Notice the matrix is symmetric ($K_{12} = K_{21}$). This is a fundamental property of all linear elastic FEA stiffness matrices, guaranteed by **Maxwell-Betti's Reciprocal Theorem**. ## 3.2 The Global Assembly and Solution For a structure with $n$ elements and $N$ nodes, the individual element matrices $[K_e]$ are assembled into a single **Global Stiffness Matrix** $[K]$ of size $N \times N$ using a process called **Direct Stiffness Method**. The final system is: $$ [K]_{N \times N} \{U\}_{N \times 1} = \{F\}_{N \times 1} $$ Boundary conditions (e.g., fixing Node 1, so $u_1 = 0$) are applied by: 1. Deleting the corresponding row and column from $[K]$. 2. Moving the known force/displacement terms to the right-hand side. The resulting reduced system is solved using numerical linear algebra methods, typically: * **Direct Solvers:** Gaussian Elimination, LU Decomposition, Cholesky Factorization (best for small to medium models, used in ANSYS Sparse solver). * **Iterative Solvers:** Conjugate Gradient, GMRES (best for very large models, used in ANSYS PCG and AMG solvers). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Universal FEA Workflow in ANSYS Workbench & Abaqus CAE This block diagram represents the exact, stage-by-stage process a B.Tech student must follow inside the software interface during a typical PCRUL307 lab examination. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STAGE 1: GEOMETRY IMPORT"] --> B["STAGE 2: ENGINEERING DATA"] B --> C["STAGE 3: MESH GENERATION"] C --> D["STAGE 4: STATIC STRUCTURAL SETUP"] D --> E["STAGE 5: SOLVER EXECUTION"] E --> F["STAGE 6: POST PROCESSING"] A1["Read STEP / IGES File"] --> A A2["Healing Geometry: Edge Merge, Small Feature Suppression"] --> A B1["Assign Material: Structural Steel E=200 GPa, nu=0.3"] --> B B2["Assign Section: Shell Thickness 5 mm OR Solid Body"] --> B C1["Element Size: Default 10 mm OR Fine 2 mm"] --> C C2["Element Shape: Tetrahedral 3D OR Hex Dominant"] --> C C3["Mesh Quality Check: Skewness less than 0.85, Aspect Ratio less than 5"] --> C D1["Supports: Fixed Support on Bottom Face"] --> D D2["Loads: Force 5000 N on Top Face"] --> D D3["Analysis Type: Static Structural"] --> D E1["Solver iterates to find nodal displacements"] --> E E2["Calculation of Element Strains via B Matrix"] --> E E3["Calculation of Element Stresses via D Matrix"] --> E F1["View Total Deformation Contour"] --> F F2["View Equivalent von Mises Stress Plot"] --> F F3["Probe maximum stress value and its location"] --> F F4["Compare with material yield strength for FOS"] --> F ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Software Architecture & Solver Philosophy This diagram shows the internal distinction between ANSYS Mechanical and Abaqus Standard/Explicit. This is a **favourite theory question** because it tests whether the student understands *which tool to choose for which problem*. ```mermaid graph TD USER["ENGINEERING PROBLEM STATEMENT"]:::userStyle USER --> DECISION{"Is there high-speed
impact, crash, or
very large non-linearity?"} DECISION -->|"NO: Static, Linear,
Modal, Thermal"| IMPLICIT["IMPLICIT SOLVER PATH"] DECISION -->|"YES: Crash, Drop Test,
Forming, Blast"| EXPLICIT["EXPLICIT SOLVER PATH"] IMPLICIT --> ANSYS_M["ANSYS MECHANICAL
Default Implicit Solver"] IMPLICIT --> ABAQUS_S["ABAQUS STANDARD
Implicit Solver"] EXPLICIT --> ANSYS_L["ANSYS LS-DYNA
Explicit Module"] EXPLICIT --> ABAQUS_E["ABAQUS EXPLICIT
Central Difference Method"] ANSYS_M --> EQ1["Solves K times U = F
Unconditionally Stable
Large Time Steps Allowed"] ABAQUS_S --> EQ1 ANSYS_L --> EQ2["Propagates wave through mesh
Conditionally Stable
Requires very small time step
dt less than L over c"] ABAQUS_E --> EQ2 EQ1 --> RESULT1["Accurate Static Results
Stress, Strain, Buckling, Modal"] EQ2 --> RESULT2["Accurate Dynamic Results
Crash Pulse, Impact Force, Energy Absorption"] classDef userStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Mesh Refinement Strategy KTU examiners often ask: *"What happens if you refine the mesh?"* This diagram visually answers that. ```mermaid flowchart LR M1["MESH A: Coarse
5 mm Element Size"] --> Q1["Element Count: 5,000"] M2["MESH B: Medium
2 mm Element Size"] --> Q2["Element Count: 50,000"] M3["MESH C: Fine
1 mm Element Size"] --> Q3["Element Count: 400,000"] Q1 --> R1["Result: 150 MPa
Low Accuracy
Fast Solve: 10 sec"] Q2 --> R2["Result: 178 MPa
Acceptable Accuracy
Solve: 2 min"] Q3 --> R3["Result: 182 MPa
High Accuracy
Solve: 30 min"] R1 -.->|Poor Mesh: High Error| FAIL["Diverged from true solution"] R3 -.->|Excessive Cost| WARN["Computational Waste"] R2 --> CONV["Mesh Independent Solution Achieved"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Note on the Diagram:** The true stress is **180 MPa**. Notice how Mesh A (5,000 elements) is very inaccurate. Mesh B (50,000 elements) is already within 1.1% of the true value. Mesh C (400,000 elements) is more accurate, but the extra 7.5× computational cost yielded only a marginal improvement. This is the engineering concept of **Mesh Convergence** — students must always run a convergence study before trusting their FEA results. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Finite Element Analysis. List any two commercial FEA software packages used in industry. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 1]` **Model Answer:** Finite Element Analysis (FEA) is a **numerical technique** for finding approximate solutions to complex engineering problems governed by differential equations. It divides a large problem domain into smaller, simpler **finite elements** connected at **nodes**, solves the governing equations for each element, and assembles them to predict the overall structural, thermal, or fluid behaviour. Two commercial FEA software packages are: 1. **ANSYS** (by ANSYS Inc.) 2. **Abaqus** (by Dassault Systèmes) *(Alternative valid answers: NASTRAN, COMSOL Multiphysics, Altair HyperWorks, Siemens NX Nastran.)* ### Question 2: Differentiate between Implicit and Explicit solvers in FEA software. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper 2]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Implicit Solver | Explicit Solver | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Method** | Solves $[K]\{u\} = \{F\}$ simultaneously | Propagates the solution forward in time using central difference method | | **Stability** | Unconditionally stable | Conditionally stable (requires $\Delta t < L/c$) | | **Time Step** | Large time steps permitted | Very small time steps mandatory | | **Best Suited For** | Static, modal, low-speed dynamic, linear problems | High-speed crash, impact, drop test, large deformation | | **Examples** | ANSYS Mechanical, Abaqus/Standard | Abaqus/Explicit, ANSYS LS-DYNA | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > * Students often confuse the roles of **Stiffness Matrix $[K]$**, **Strain-Displacement Matrix $[B]$**, and **Constitutive Matrix $[D]$**. Memorize the order: $K_e = \int B^T D B \, dV$. The transposition of $[B]$ is the *first* matrix inside the integral, the constitutive matrix $[D]$ is in the *middle*, and $[B]$ without transpose is the *last*. > * Another common mistake is omitting the **integration step**. If you write $[K_e] = [B]^T [D] [B] V_e$ without the $\int$ sign, you will lose **2 marks**. > * Always mention the **units** of the final stiffness matrix. It is **N/m** (Force per unit displacement). Failing to state units costs 1 mark in the valuation key. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three main stages of a Finite Element Analysis** workflow. State the purpose of each stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A circular steel shaft of diameter 25 mm and length 1.2 m is fixed at one end and subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN at the free end. The material properties are: Young's Modulus $E = 200$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$. Determine: **(i)** the axial stress, **(ii)** the axial strain, and **(iii)** the total elongation of the shaft. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A(a): The three main stages of FEA are: 1. **Pre-Processing:** This involves defining the geometry, applying material properties, generating the mesh, and assigning boundary conditions and loads. *Purpose: To create a discretized mathematical model of the physical problem.* 2. **Solution (Solver):** The software numerically assembles the global stiffness matrix and solves the simultaneous linear/non-linear algebraic equations. *Purpose: To compute nodal displacements, from which strains and stresses are derived.* 3. **Post-Processing:** The numerical results are visualized using contour plots, deformed shape displays, and tabulated data extraction. *Purpose: To interpret the simulation output and validate the design against failure criteria.* *(Examiner Note: A hand-drawn or Mermaid block diagram showing the three stages with arrows is mandatory for full 7 marks.)* #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Given Data:** * Diameter $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$ * Length $L = 1.2 \text{ m}$ * Axial Load $P = 50 \text{ kN} = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$ * $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$ * $\nu = 0.3$ **Step 1: Calculate the Cross-Sectional Area** The shaft is circular, so: $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 $$ Evaluating the square and the fraction: $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.000625 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Calculate the Axial Stress (Part i)** $$ \sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \sigma = 101.859 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 101.86 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the Axial Strain (Part ii)** $$ \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{101.86 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} $$ $$ \varepsilon = 5.093 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ **Step 4: Calculate the Total Elongation (Part iii)** $$ \delta L = \varepsilon \times L = 5.093 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.2 $$ $$ \delta L = 6.112 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.6112 \text{ mm} $$ **Final Answer Summary:** | Quantity | Symbol | Value | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Cross-sectional Area | $A$ | $4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ | | Axial Stress | $\sigma$ | **101.86 MPa** | | Axial Strain | $\varepsilon$ | $5.093 \times 10^{-4}$ | | Total Elongation | $\delta L$ | **0.6112 mm** | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]` **(a)** Derive the **element stiffness matrix** for a 1D linear spring (bar) element of length $L$, area $A$, and Young's Modulus $E$, using the principle of minimum potential energy. State clearly the roles of the $[B]$ and $[D]$ matrices. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A steel plate with dimensions 200 mm × 150 mm × 8 mm is subjected to a uniaxial tensile force of 120 kN along its length. The material is Structural Steel with a yield strength of 250 MPa. Using the Von-Mises stress criterion, calculate the Factor of Safety (FOS). Assume plane stress conditions. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B(a): *(Full derivation is presented in SECTION 3.1 above. For the exam, the student must write the following compact sequence:)* 1. Displacement field: $u(x) = N_1(x) u_1 + N_2(x) u_2$, with shape functions $N_1 = 1 - x/L$ and $N_2 = x/L$. 2. Strain-displacement: $\varepsilon = du/dx = [B]\{u\}$, where $[B] = \begin{bmatrix} -1/L & 1/L \end{bmatrix}$. 3. Stress-strain: $\sigma = E \varepsilon = [D][B]\{u\}$, where $[D] = E$. 4. Total potential energy: $\Pi = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \sigma \varepsilon \, dV - \{u\}^T \{F\}$. 5. Minimize: $\partial \Pi / \partial u = 0 \Rightarrow [K_e]\{u\} = \{F\}$. 6. Evaluate: $[K_e] = \int_0^L [B]^T [D] [B] A \, dx = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. **Role of $[B]$ (Strain-Displacement Matrix):** Maps nodal displacements to strains within the element. Its derivative of the shape functions. **Role of $[D]$ (Constitutive Matrix):** Represents the material property, linking stress to strain. For linear elastic isotropic material, it is a function of $E$ and $\nu$. #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Given Data:** * Length $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ * Width $w = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ * Thickness $t = 8 \text{ mm} = 0.008 \text{ m}$ * Force $F = 120 \text{ kN} = 120{,}000 \text{ N}$ * Yield Strength $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$ **Step 1: Calculate the Cross-Sectional Area** The force is applied along the length, so the resisting cross-section is width × thickness: $$ A = w \times t = 0.15 \times 0.008 = 0.0012 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Calculate the Axial Stress** $$ \sigma_x = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{120{,}000}{0.0012} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 100 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3: Determine the Stress State for Von-Mises** Under uniaxial tension, the principal stresses are: $$ \sigma_1 = 100 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = 0 $$ *(Plane stress assumption means the other two in-plane stresses are zero, and the third principal stress is also zero for thin plate uniaxial loading.)* **Step 4: Calculate Von-Mises Stress** Using the simplified uniaxial Von-Mises formula $\sigma_{vM} = \sigma_1$ when $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$: $$ \sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\frac{(100-0)^2 + (0-0)^2 + (0-100)^2}{2}} $$ $$ \sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\frac{10000 + 0 + 10000}{2}} = \sqrt{10000} = 100 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 5: Calculate the Factor of Safety** $$ FOS = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{vonMises}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.5 $$ **Final Answer:** The Factor of Safety is **2.5**. Since $FOS > 1$, the plate is safe under the given loading condition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** - [ ] **FEA** is a numerical, approximate method. It is *not* an exact closed-form analytical solution. - [ ] The **three universal stages** are: Pre-Processing, Solution, Post-Processing. (Memorize the order!) - [ ] The **master FEA equation** is $[K]\{U\} = \{F\}$, where $[K]$ is the Global Stiffness Matrix. - [ ] The **element stiffness matrix** is derived as $[K_e] = \int [B]^T [D] [B] \, dV$. - [ ] $[B]$ is the **strain-displacement matrix** (derived from shape function derivatives). - [ ] $[D]$ is the **constitutive matrix** (derived from material properties: $E$ and $\nu$). - [ ] **ANSYS** is general-purpose; **Abaqus** is renowned for advanced non-linearity. - [ ] **Implicit solvers** are unconditionally stable and best for static problems. - [ ] **Explicit solvers** are conditionally stable and best for crash/impact dynamics. - [ ] **Mesh Convergence** is a mandatory verification step. Refining the mesh too much wastes time; too little yields wrong answers. - [ ] **Von-Mises Stress** is the standard failure criterion for ductile materials in FEA post-processing. - [ ] Always define the **units** of every quantity in your answer (N, m, Pa, MPa, GPa). - [ ] For a 1D bar, $[K_e] = \frac{EA}{L} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ — this is the **most-asked FEA formula** in KTU board exams. - [ ] In the lab record, always include: **screenshot of mesh**, **contour plot with colour legend**, and a **convergence table** showing stress vs. element count.
